title: Indian cuisine
full_text: Indian cuisine is highly diverse and reflects thousands of years of cultural exchange, regional variation, and historical development across the Indian subcontinent. It includes many regional traditions and a wide range of flavors, ingredients, and cooking styles. Staple foods commonly include rice, whole-wheat flour, pearl millet, and a wide variety of lentils and pulses. Indian cooking also makes extensive use of vegetable oils, with regional preferences such as peanut oil in parts of the north and west, mustard oil in the east, and coconut oil in parts of the south and along the western coast. Indian cuisine has been shaped by ancient agricultural traditions as well as later contacts with Persians, Central Asians, Portuguese, and British influences. New World ingredients such as tomatoes, potatoes, peanuts, squash, and chili peppers were introduced during the colonial period and became important in many dishes. Over time, Indian cuisine developed strong regional differences, with distinctive North Indian, South Indian, East Indian, and West Indian food traditions. Lentils, curries, spice blends, breads, rice dishes, pickles, and dairy-based foods are central to many meals. Indian cuisine also includes a rich variety of sweets, snacks, and festive dishes. Food practices are also influenced by religion and culture, including vegetarian traditions and dietary restrictions in some communities. Indian cuisine has spread widely beyond India through migration and has become influential internationally, with dishes such as curry and tandoori foods gaining worldwide popularity. The Portuguese and British introduced baking techniques and a variety of New World vegetables, including maize, tomato, potato, sweet potatoes, peanuts, squash, and chilli, which became staples of Indian cuisine during the colonial period.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Indian cuisine is diverse and reflects a long history of cultural interaction, regional development, and culinary exchange across the Indian subcontinent.
It includes many regional cuisines with different ingredients, flavors, and cooking methods.

sections.heading: History
content: The cuisine developed over thousands of years, beginning with early agricultural traditions in the Indian subcontinent.
Later influences from Persian, Central Asian, Portuguese, and British contacts contributed new foods, techniques, and ingredients.
Ingredients such as tomatoes, potatoes, peanuts, squash, and chili peppers were introduced during the colonial period and became widely used.

sections.heading: Staple ingredients
content: Common staples include rice, whole-wheat flour, pearl millet, and many kinds of lentils and pulses.
Indian cooking also uses a range of oils, with regional preferences including peanut oil, mustard oil, and coconut oil.

sections.heading: Regional diversity
content: Indian cuisine varies strongly by region, with distinct North Indian, South Indian, East Indian, and West Indian traditions.
Different areas use locally available grains, vegetables, fruits, spices, and cooking methods.

sections.heading: Dietary practices
content: Food traditions in India are influenced by religion, culture, and local customs.
Vegetarianism is important in many communities, and some groups follow dietary restrictions or fasting-related food practices.

sections.heading: Global influence
content: Indian cuisine spread internationally through migration and cultural exchange.
Curries and tandoor-based dishes are among the best-known Indian foods outside India.

title: Pakistani cuisine
full_text: Pakistani cuisine combines influences from the Indian subcontinent, Central Asia, Persia, and the Mughal culinary tradition. It is known for strong flavors, generous use of spices, and a wide variety of meat dishes, breads, rice dishes, lentils, and desserts. Regional diversity is important, with Punjabi, Sindhi, Balochi, Pashtun, Kashmiri, and northern mountain food traditions contributing distinct styles and ingredients. Meat plays a major role in many meals, especially beef, mutton, chicken, and kebab dishes, while lentils, vegetables, and legumes are also common. Rice dishes such as pulao and biryani are popular, and flatbreads including naan, roti, and paratha are widely eaten. Pakistani cuisine also includes many sweets, tea varieties, and festive foods. Cooking methods often involve grilling, slow-cooking, tandoor baking, and rich gravies. Urban food culture includes street food, barbecue, and restaurant cooking, while home cooking varies by region and community.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Pakistani cuisine reflects a mix of South Asian, Persian, Central Asian, and Mughal influences.
It is known for bold flavors, spice use, and a wide range of meat, rice, lentil, and bread dishes.

sections.heading: Historical influences
content: The cuisine developed through interaction between local food traditions and influences from Persian, Central Asian, and Mughal culinary practices.
These influences helped shape cooking styles, festive dishes, and the use of rich gravies, kebabs, and rice dishes.

sections.heading: Regional cuisines
content: Pakistani cuisine varies by region, including Punjabi, Sindhi, Balochi, Pashtun, Kashmiri, and northern mountain traditions.
Different regions emphasize different ingredients, breads, meats, and preparation methods.

sections.heading: Common foods
content: Common foods include naan, roti, paratha, lentils, vegetables, kebabs, curries, pulao, and biryani.
Tea, sweets, and desserts are also important in everyday and festive eating.

title: Bangladeshi cuisine
full_text: Bangladeshi cuisine is shaped by the geography, climate, rivers, and agricultural traditions of Bangladesh. Rice and fish are central to many meals, and the cuisine is known for its use of freshwater fish, lentils, vegetables, mustard oil, and aromatic spices. Regional and community differences are important, with variations influenced by local ingredients and historical links to Bengali, Mughal, Persian, and neighboring food traditions. Many dishes balance heat, richness, and fragrance, with curries, bhortas, lentil dishes, rice preparations, sweets, and snacks forming key parts of the cuisine. Hilsa is one of the most iconic fish in Bangladeshi food culture. Mustard, green chilies, onions, turmeric, and other spices are common in everyday cooking. Bangladeshi cuisine also includes festive and ceremonial foods, as well as a strong sweet-making tradition with desserts based on milk, rice, and sugar.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Bangladeshi cuisine is strongly shaped by river life, agriculture, and Bengali food traditions.
Rice and fish are especially central, along with lentils, vegetables, mustard oil, and spices.

sections.heading: Common ingredients
content: Freshwater fish, rice, lentils, mustard oil, onions, green chilies, turmeric, and other aromatic spices are widely used.
Hilsa is among the best-known and most culturally important fish in Bangladeshi cuisine.

sections.heading: Cooking style
content: The cuisine includes curries, bhortas, rice dishes, vegetable preparations, and lentil-based foods.
Flavor often comes from spice blends, mustard, and careful balancing of richness and heat.

sections.heading: Sweets and festive foods
content: Bangladeshi cuisine has a strong tradition of sweets and ceremonial foods.
Milk-based and rice-based desserts are important in celebrations and hospitality.

title: Nepali cuisine
full_text: Nepali cuisine reflects the country’s geography, ethnic diversity, and connections with neighboring India, Tibet, and other Himalayan regions. It varies significantly by altitude, region, and community, but many meals are centered on staple combinations of grain, lentils, and vegetables. Dal bhat, usually served with rice, lentil soup, and side dishes, is one of the best-known everyday meals. Other common foods include pickles, vegetable curries, flatbreads, dumplings such as momo, and regionally specific meat dishes. In mountainous and Himalayan areas, ingredients and cooking styles differ from those of the plains. Nepali food often uses spices in a balanced way rather than aiming for extreme heat, and it includes both simple daily meals and rich festive dishes. The cuisine reflects local agriculture and seasonal availability, as well as the traditions of many ethnic and regional communities.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Nepali cuisine is shaped by geography, ethnic diversity, and links with Indian and Tibetan food traditions.
It varies by region, altitude, and community.

sections.heading: Staple foods
content: Dal bhat is one of the best-known staple meals, usually consisting of rice, lentil soup, and side dishes.
Vegetables, pickles, flatbreads, and dumplings such as momo are also common.

sections.heading: Regional diversity
content: Food differs between the plains, hills, and Himalayan regions because of climate, agriculture, and local traditions.
Ingredients and preparation methods change according to what is available locally.

sections.heading: Cuisine character
content: Nepali cuisine includes both everyday meals and special festive dishes.
It often emphasizes balance, local ingredients, and seasonal cooking.

title: Biryani
full_text: Biryani is a layered rice dish made with rice, spices, and usually meat, though vegetarian versions also exist. It is widely associated with the Indian subcontinent and has many regional styles across South Asia. Biryani is known for combining fragrant rice with spiced ingredients cooked together or assembled in layers. Common additions include chicken, mutton, beef, eggs, potatoes, yogurt, onions, herbs, and aromatic spices. Different styles vary in seasoning, cooking method, and texture. Some versions are cooked by layering partially cooked rice with meat and then finishing the dish together, while others cook ingredients in different ways before combining them. Biryani is often connected with Mughal and Persian-influenced culinary traditions, though it has developed many local forms in South Asian cuisines. It is widely served in homes, restaurants, celebrations, and festive occasions.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Biryani is a rice dish made with rice, spices, and usually meat, although vegetarian versions also exist.
It is one of the best-known dishes associated with South Asian cuisine.

sections.heading: Ingredients
content: Common ingredients include rice, meat or vegetables, yogurt, onions, herbs, and aromatic spices.
Eggs or potatoes are included in some regional versions.

sections.heading: Preparation
content: Biryani is often prepared by layering rice with spiced ingredients and finishing the cooking together.
Different styles vary in seasoning, assembly, and cooking technique.

sections.heading: Regional forms
content: There are many regional varieties of biryani across the Indian subcontinent.
The dish is popular in everyday dining as well as festivals and celebrations.

title: Curry
full_text: Curry is a broad term used for dishes with sauce, gravy, or spiced preparation, especially in South Asian and related cuisines. The meaning of the word varies by region and context, and it can refer to many different dishes rather than a single recipe. Curries may be made with vegetables, meat, fish, legumes, or eggs, and they often use combinations of spices, herbs, onions, garlic, ginger, and other flavoring ingredients. The term became widely used in English during the colonial period to describe a range of South Asian dishes. In practice, many cuisines use specific local names rather than relying on the general word curry. The flavor, thickness, ingredients, and cooking methods of curry dishes differ greatly across regions. Curry has also spread globally and been adapted into many local forms outside South Asia.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Curry is a broad term for many sauced or spiced dishes, especially those associated with South Asian cuisines.
It does not refer to a single standardized dish.

sections.heading: Ingredients
content: Curries may include vegetables, meat, fish, legumes, or eggs.
They often use spice mixtures, herbs, onions, garlic, ginger, and other flavoring ingredients.

sections.heading: Meaning of the term
content: The English word curry became common during the colonial period as a general label for many South Asian dishes.
Local cuisines often use more specific names for particular dishes.

sections.heading: Global spread
content: Curry has spread internationally and taken many local forms outside South Asia.
Different regions adapted it to their own ingredients and tastes.

title: Dosa
full_text: Dosa is a thin savory pancake or crepe associated especially with South Indian cuisine. It is typically made from a fermented batter of rice and black gram and cooked on a hot flat surface until crisp or soft depending on the style. Dosa is eaten plain or served with fillings and side dishes such as chutneys and sambar. It is widely consumed for breakfast or other meals and is known for its balance of texture, fermentation-based flavor, and versatility. Regional variations include different thicknesses, fillings, and serving styles. Masala dosa, one of the best-known forms, includes a spiced potato filling. Dosa has become popular across India and internationally.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Dosa is a thin savory pancake or crepe strongly associated with South Indian cuisine.
It is made from a fermented batter, usually based on rice and black gram.

sections.heading: Preparation
content: The batter is spread on a hot griddle or flat cooking surface and cooked until it reaches the desired texture.
Dosa may be crisp or softer depending on style and preparation.

sections.heading: Serving style
content: Dosa is often eaten with chutneys and sambar.
Some versions are filled, especially masala dosa with spiced potato filling.

sections.heading: Popularity
content: Dosa is widely eaten in South Asia and has spread far beyond its regional origins.
It is commonly served for breakfast but may also be eaten at other times of day.

title: Samosa
full_text: Samosa is a fried or baked pastry with a savory filling, widely eaten in South Asia and many other regions. It is usually triangular in shape and commonly filled with spiced potatoes, onions, peas, lentils, meat, or mixtures of these ingredients, depending on local style. Samosa is a popular snack, street food, and festive item. It is often served with chutney or other accompaniments. The dish has historical roots beyond South Asia and spread through cultural exchange over time, later becoming deeply established in South Asian food traditions. Today there are many regional versions in both size and filling, and samosas are eaten in homes, markets, and restaurants.

sections.heading: Introduction
content: Samosa is a savory pastry, usually triangular, that is fried or baked and filled with spiced ingredients.
It is one of the best-known snacks in South Asian cuisine.

sections.heading: Fillings
content: Common fillings include potatoes, peas, onions, lentils, and meat.
The exact filling varies by region and local preference.

sections.heading: Serving style
content: Samosa is often eaten as a snack or street food and is commonly served with chutney.
It is also popular during gatherings and festive occasions.

sections.heading: History and spread
content: The dish has roots in historical culinary exchange and later became well established across South Asia.
Many regional forms now exist in both filling and preparation style.

title: Tandoori chicken
full_text: Tandoori chicken is a dish made from chicken marinated in yoghurt and spices, then roasted in a tandoor — a cylindrical clay oven traditionally heated with charcoal or wood. The dish originated in the Punjab region of South Asia and is now one of the most widely recognised dishes of Indian cuisine globally. The modern form was popularised in the late 1940s at the Moti Mahal restaurant in Delhi by Kundan Lal Gujral, Kundan Lal Jaggi, and their associates, who were Punjabi migrants from Peshawar following the partition of India. However, the practice of cooking marinated meat in clay ovens has roots stretching back to the Harappan civilisation around 3000 BCE, and ancient Sanskrit medical texts describe meat seasoned with spices and roasted in clay ovens. To prepare tandoori chicken, raw skinned chicken pieces are marinated in a two-stage process: first in lemon juice, salt, and red chilli, then in a mixture of yoghurt, garlic-ginger paste, Kashmiri red chilli powder (which gives the dish its characteristic red-orange colour), cumin, coriander, garam masala, and sometimes dried fenugreek leaves. The marinated chicken is skewered and cooked at very high temperatures in the tandoor, which can reach up to 480°C, producing a smoky exterior, slight char marks, and a juicy interior. The dish is served hot with sliced onions, lemon wedges, mint chutney, and naan. Tandoori chicken is also the basis for chicken tikka and, by extension, chicken tikka masala.

title: Dal
full_text: Dal (also spelled daal or dhal) refers both to dried, split pulses — including lentils, dried peas, and chickpeas — and to the wide range of cooked dishes prepared from them. It is a foundational element of South Asian cuisine and among the most important sources of protein in the region, particularly for vegetarians. The word derives from the Sanskrit root dal, meaning "to split." India is the world's largest producer of pulses, and dal has been consumed on the subcontinent for millennia — ancient religious texts including the Yajurveda reference it, and researchers have dated the preparation of split pulses as far back as 800–300 BCE. The most common types used in South Asian cooking include toor dal (split pigeon peas), chana dal (split Bengal gram), masoor dal (red lentils), urad dal (black gram), and moong dal (mung beans), each with distinct flavours, textures, and cooking characteristics. Dal is typically prepared by simmering the pulses in water with turmeric and salt until soft, then finishing the dish with a tadka (tempering) of hot oil or ghee bloomed with whole spices — commonly cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, and asafoetida — which is poured over the cooked dal to release a burst of flavour and aroma. The dish ranges from thin and soup-like to thick and porridge-like depending on the preparation. Regional variations are numerous: dal makhani from Punjab is slow-cooked with butter and cream; Gujarati dal is sweet-sour with jaggery and tamarind; and South Indian sambar is a tamarind-based lentil stew with vegetables. Dal is universally eaten with rice, chapati, or other flatbreads.

title: Roti
full_text: Roti is an unleavened flatbread made from stoneground whole-wheat flour (atta) and water, and is one of the most important staple foods of the Indian subcontinent. The word derives from the Sanskrit roṭikā, meaning "bread." Roti is eaten daily across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka, and has spread through diaspora communities to East Africa, the Caribbean, and Southeast Asia. It serves as the primary accompaniment to curries, dals, vegetables, and chutneys, and is typically eaten by tearing pieces by hand to scoop up the accompanying dish. The standard preparation involves mixing atta with water (and sometimes a little oil or ghee) into a smooth, pliable dough, which is divided into small balls, rolled into thin circles on a flat board, and cooked on a hot cast-iron griddle (tawa). The roti is pressed gently and turned until lightly browned on both sides, then often placed briefly over a direct flame to make it puff. Freshly made roti is typically brushed with ghee or butter before serving. The term roti encompasses several closely related flatbreads: chapati, which is made in the same way; paratha, a laminated or layered version cooked with more oil; puri, a deep-fried version; and tandoori roti, baked on the inside wall of a tandoor oven. Regional variations include makki roti (corn flour) and bajra roti (pearl millet flour), common in Punjab and Rajasthan respectively. Roti is a staple across economic backgrounds and is deeply embedded in South Asian food culture.

title: Pilaf
full_text: Pilaf (also spelled pilau, pulao, or plov depending on the region) is a rice dish — or in some regions a wheat or other grain dish — in which the rice is cooked in a well-seasoned stock or broth together with spices, and often meat, vegetables, dried fruit, or nuts. It is one of the most geographically widespread dishes in the world, found across Central Asia, South Asia, the Middle East, the Caucasus, East Africa, the Balkans, and the Caribbean. The origins of pilaf are generally traced to Persia (present-day Iran), where the technique of cooking rice in spiced broth was well established by the time of the tenth-century scholar Avicenna, who described various pilaf preparations in his medical texts. The method spread throughout the Islamic world during the Abbasid Caliphate, evolving into the Spanish paella, the South Asian pulao and biryani, the Turkish pilav, and the Central Asian plov. In the Indian subcontinent, the dish is known as pulao (or pilau) and typically involves cooking basmati rice in a spiced broth together with whole spices such as cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and bay leaves, along with meat or vegetables. The key technique is toasting the rice briefly in oil or ghee before adding the liquid, which helps the grains stay separate. Biryani is closely related but more complex, using separately cooked rice and meat layered together and finished by dum cooking. Central Asian plov, particularly from Uzbekistan, is considered the national dish and is made with lamb, yellow carrots, and cumin.

title: Bangladeshi cuisine
full_text: Bangladeshi cuisine refers to the culinary traditions of Bangladesh, a country in South Asia whose food culture has been shaped by its riverine geography, predominantly Muslim population, Bengali cultural heritage, and the historical influence of Mughal rule. Rice is the absolute staple of Bangladeshi cuisine, consumed at almost every meal, often accompanied by fish, lentils (dal), vegetables, and meat. Fish is the primary protein source, reflecting Bangladesh's extensive river system, delta coastline, and the Bengali adage "mache bhate Bangali" — "fish and rice make a Bengali." The national fish is the hilsa (ilish), beloved for its rich, oily flavour and cultural significance. Bangladeshi cuisine is closely related to that of West Bengal in India, but distinguished by the predominance of beef and the absence of pork (in keeping with Islamic dietary law), a greater use of mustard oil, and distinct Mughlai culinary influences from the Nawabs of Dhaka. The Mughal heritage brought rich kebabs, layered breads, korma, and kachchi biryani to Old Dhaka, where a vibrant street food culture centred on the Chowk Bazaar endures to this day. Key staple accompaniments to rice include a variety of bhortas (mashed preparations), bhajis (fried vegetables), lentil soups, and fish or meat curries. Spices such as mustard seeds, turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, and green chillies are central to Bangladeshi cooking. Sweets (mishti) are also a major part of the food culture, including rasgulla, roshomalai, sandesh, mishti doi, and pantua.

title: Bengali cuisine
full_text: Bengali cuisine encompasses the food traditions of the Bengal region, shared between Bangladesh and the Indian state of West Bengal, and is one of the most distinctive regional cuisines of the Indian subcontinent. It is characterised by a nuanced use of spices — particularly mustard seeds, nigella seeds (kalonji), fenugreek, and panch phoron (a five-spice blend) — a sophisticated balance of flavours including bitter, sour, pungent, and sweet, and a strong emphasis on fish and rice as core staples. Bengali cuisine developed a uniquely structured multi-course tradition of eating, where dishes are served in sequence rather than simultaneously: starting with bitter and cleansing preparations, moving through vegetables, fish, and meat courses, and concluding with rice and lentils or a sweet. Fish occupies an almost sacred place in Bengali food culture; the hilsa (ilish) is regarded as the king of fish and a symbol of Bengali identity. Mustard oil, mustard paste, and poppy seed paste (posto) are characteristic flavouring agents. The cuisine is also distinguished by its extraordinary tradition of milk-based sweets — rasgulla, roshomalai, sandesh, mishti doi, and kheer kodom — which have spread far beyond the region. Historically, the Mughal influence on Dhaka's cuisine brought korma, biryani, and lavish kebabs, while the Hindu Bengali tradition of West Bengal emphasises vegetarian dishes and a strict vegetarianism in the Brahmin community. Regional sub-cuisines include Dhakaiya (Old Dhaka), Chittagonian, Sylheti, and those of the coastal Sundarbans region.

title: Ilish curry
full_text: Ilish curry (also called shorshe ilish or hilsa curry) is considered the national dish of Bangladesh and one of the most beloved preparations in all of Bengali cuisine. It is made from ilish (Tenualosa ilisha), a species of oily fish related to the herring, which is the national fish of Bangladesh and represents around 12% of the country's total fish production. The most iconic preparation is shorshe ilish — hilsa cooked in a sharp, pungent mustard gravy. To prepare it, hilsa steaks are rubbed with turmeric and salt, then lightly pan-fried in mustard oil. A paste of black and yellow mustard seeds, blended with green chillies, salt, and water, is prepared separately. The fried fish is then simmered in this mustard paste with nigella seeds (kalonji) and green chillies, with the dish finished in mustard oil to maximise its distinctive pungency and aroma. The mustard flavour is considered the essential character of this dish; unlike most South Asian fish curries, ilish curry typically does not use onion, ginger, or garlic, which are considered to mask the delicate flavour of the fish. Hilsa is also cooked in yoghurt (doi ilish), steamed in banana leaves (bhapa ilish), or simply pan-fried as ilish bhaja. The fish is particularly prized during the monsoon season when it migrates upstream to spawn, and it holds deep cultural significance in Bangladesh — traditionally eaten at Pohela Boishakh (Bengali New Year) as panta ilish, and given as wedding gifts.

title: Bhuna khichuri
full_text: Bhuna khichuri is a richly spiced, dry-textured rice and lentil dish that is one of the most beloved comfort foods in Bangladesh and West Bengal. The word "bhuna" refers to the dry-frying or toasting method that distinguishes this preparation from the thinner, more porridge-like patla (runny) khichuri. To make bhuna khichuri, the lentils — typically moong dal (split yellow mung beans), sometimes combined with masoor dal (red lentils) or chana dal — are first dry-roasted in a pan until they become fragrant and acquire a light golden colour. The rice (traditionally a fragrant short-grain variety such as kalijeera or chinigura) is soaked alongside the toasted lentils. Separately, onions are fried golden brown in mustard oil with whole spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, bay leaves, cumin). Ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, red chilli, and coriander powder are added and fried until the oil separates. The drained rice and lentils are then folded into this spiced oil base and fried together for several minutes before water is added and the pot is covered to cook on a low flame until dry and fluffy. The finished dish is garnished with fried onions (beresta), ghee, garam masala, and fresh coriander. Bhuna khichuri is considered the quintessential rainy-day comfort food in Bangladesh and is typically served with egg curry, fried eggplant (begun bhaja), beef or chicken bhuna, and assorted bhortas.

title: Bhorta
full_text: Bhorta (also spelled bharta or vorta) is a category of mashed preparations that is one of the most fundamental and culturally significant elements of Bangladeshi and Bengali cuisine. The term covers a wide range of dishes in which a single main ingredient — vegetable, fish, dried fish, legume, or egg — is either boiled, roasted, or fried, and then mashed together with raw mustard oil, finely chopped raw onion, green chillies, salt, and often garlic. The result is a flavourful, textured mash eaten as a side dish alongside plain rice. Common varieties include aloo bhorta (mashed potato), begun bhorta (roasted or pan-fried eggplant/aubergine mash), shutki bhorta (dried fish mash), and dal bhorta (mashed lentils). The shutki bhorta made from sun-dried hilsa or other fish is considered particularly intensely flavoured and is a beloved everyday dish in Bangladesh. Mustard oil is the defining fat in bhorta — its sharp, pungent character is inseparable from the preparation's character, and ghee or vegetable oil are not considered suitable substitutes in authentic cooking. Bhorta evolved from peasant cooking traditions in rural Bengal, where it was a practical way of making simple ingredients — boiled rice-cooker potatoes, charred eggplants — into intensely flavoured side dishes without elaborate spice work. It has since been elevated to a symbol of Bangladeshi national cuisine and cultural identity, served prominently during Pohela Boishakh (Bengali New Year) alongside panta bhat.

title: Panta bhat
full_text: Panta bhat (also written panta bhaat, Bengali: পান্তা ভাত) is a traditional dish of cooked rice that has been soaked in water overnight and allowed to undergo mild lacto-fermentation. It is one of the most culturally significant foods in Bangladesh and the greater Bengal region, particularly associated with rural and working-class communities. To prepare panta bhat, leftover cooked rice is placed in a pot or clay vessel with enough water to submerge it, then left at room temperature overnight or for up to 24 hours. During this time, naturally occurring lactic acid bacteria ferment the rice slightly, producing a sour, cooling, and nutritionally enriched product. The following morning, the rice is drained and served cold with salt, raw onion, sliced green chillies, and mustard oil. Common accompaniments include fried hilsa fish (ilish bhaja), dried fish bhorta, potato bhorta, and various other bhortas. Panta bhat is particularly associated with Pohela Boishakh, the Bengali New Year celebrated on April 14, where it has become a symbol of Bangladeshi cultural identity — consumed by people of all economic backgrounds as an act of solidarity with the nation's rural roots. The dish also has practical origins: soaking cooked rice in water was a way to preserve it and make it digestible in the absence of refrigeration. Nutritional research has found that the fermentation process significantly increases the iron, potassium, and calcium content of panta bhat compared to plain rice.

title: Bakarkhani
full_text: Bakarkhani (also spelled bakorkhani) is a thick, flaky, crispy flatbread with roots in Mughlai cuisine that has become one of the most iconic bread traditions of Old Dhaka, Bangladesh. It is one of the most famous speciality foods of Dhakai cuisine, prepared and sold by specialist bakers (bakarkhaniwallas) in the old city, particularly in areas such as Chowk Bazaar and Shakhari Bazaar, where it has been made for centuries. Bakarkhani is made from refined wheat flour (maida) enriched with milk, ghee, sugar, and sometimes cardamom, kneaded into a stiff layered dough. The dough is rolled into rounds, dimpled on the surface, and baked in a tandoor oven until crisp and lightly golden, producing a biscuit-like, slightly sweet flatbread with a flaky, layered interior. It is characterised by a distinctive perforated surface made by pressing a spiked implement (chako) into the dough before baking. Bakarkhani has a long shelf life due to its dry, crisp texture, which historically made it suitable for travel and storage. It is traditionally served with tea and is a popular item during Ramadan, particularly for sehri (the pre-dawn meal) and iftar (the meal breaking the fast). The name is believed to derive from the name of a Nawab or nobleman associated with the flatbread's origin in the Mughal court. Dhaka's bakarkhani was so distinctive that it was introduced to Kolkata by Dhakaiya migrants following the partition of India in 1947.

title: Mustard oil
full_text: Mustard oil (Brassica juncea) is the traditional and defining cooking fat of Bangladesh and the greater Bengal region, and it plays a central role in shaping the distinctive character of Bengali and Bangladeshi cuisine. It is pressed from the seeds of the brown or black mustard plant and has a sharp, pungent, intensely aromatic quality — described as simultaneously bitter, nutty, and aggressively flavourful — that sets it apart from all other vegetable oils. In Bangladeshi cooking, mustard oil is used both as a cooking medium and as a raw finishing ingredient: many bhortas (mashed preparations), salads, and lightly cooked fish dishes are finished with a drizzle of raw mustard oil, which provides the dish's signature pungency. It is the near-universal oil for cooking fish curries, stir-fried vegetables, and lentil dishes in Bangladesh, West Bengal, and eastern India (including Odisha, Assam, and Bihar). The oil contains a compound called allyl isothiocyanate, which is responsible for its heat and pungency; this compound is also what gives mustard its characteristic sharp bite. Mustard oil has a high smoke point and is considered particularly good for deep-frying and high-heat cooking. Historically, mustard oil was the sole edible oil available in Bengal before the introduction of soybean oil and refined vegetable oil. It has been gradually displaced in urban cooking by refined oils but remains culturally essential and is considered irreplaceable in authentic Bengali preparations.

title: Hilsa
full_text: The hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha), known in Bengali as ilish (ইলিশ), is a species of oily fish in the herring family (Clupeidae) that is the national fish of Bangladesh, a geographical indication product of the country, and one of the most culturally and economically significant fish in South Asia. Bangladesh accounts for approximately 97% of the world's total hilsa catch, and the fish contributes around 12% of Bangladesh's total fish production. The hilsa is a migratory fish that spends its adult life in coastal marine waters of the Bay of Bengal and the Indian Ocean, then migrates upstream into freshwater rivers — particularly the Meghna, Padma, and Jamuna systems — during the monsoon season to spawn. Fish caught during this upstream migration, particularly in the Padma (Ganges) River, are considered the most prized and flavourful due to the diet and water conditions encountered during their journey. Hilsa has an exceptionally rich, oily flesh with a complex, buttery, slightly sweet flavour that is highly distinctive. Its numerous fine bones, which dissolve during slow cooking, are considered part of its charm by devotees. The fish is rich in omega-3 fatty acids, vitamins B12 and D, and other nutrients. Due to its cultural significance, hilsa is eaten at celebratory and auspicious occasions — Pohela Boishakh (Bengali New Year), weddings, and religious festivals — and has deep symbolic meaning in Bengali identity. It can be prepared in more than 50 ways, including in mustard gravy (shorshe ilish), steamed in banana leaves (bhapa ilish), or simply pan-fried.

title: Rice
full_text: Rice is the absolute culinary and cultural cornerstone of Bangladesh — the most important ingredient in the country's food system, consumed at virtually every meal by virtually every Bangladeshi household. Bangladesh is one of the world's largest rice producers, and the country's geography — defined by the floodplains and delta of three major rivers — is exceptionally suited to wetland rice cultivation, which has been practised here for thousands of years. Bangladeshi cuisine uses many different rice varieties, each suited to different culinary applications. Long-grain, non-sticky varieties of aromatic fine rice such as kalijeera and chinigura are used in special preparations including khichuri, polao (pilaf), and festival dishes, prized for their fragrance and delicate texture. Plain white rice (sada bhat) forms the centrepiece of everyday meals, served with fish curry, dal, vegetables, and bhorta. Atap chawl (non-parboiled short-grain rice) is used in specific dishes requiring a sticky consistency, and as pitha (rice cake) flour. Parboiled rice varieties are widely consumed daily. Panta bhat — rice soaked overnight in water to ferment slightly — is an ancient preparation associated with rural Bengali culture and eaten as a cooling summer and New Year dish. Rice is also processed into flour for making pitha, ground for rice noodles, and fermented for rice wine (chowa) in tribal communities. Rice cultivation and its cultural rituals remain central to Bangladeshi agricultural festivals including Nabonno (new harvest festival).

title: Dal
full_text: Dal (lentils and other pulses) is the second most important food category in Bangladeshi cuisine after rice, forming a nutritional cornerstone of the daily diet across all economic levels. Bangladesh grows and consumes a variety of dals, the most common of which include masoor dal (red lentils), moong dal (green mung beans or their split, dehusked yellow form), chana dal (split Bengal gram), and matar dal (split peas). Dal is most commonly prepared as a thin soupy stew — simmered with turmeric, salt, and sometimes tomato, then finished with a phoron (tempering) of mustard seeds, dried red chillies, and occasionally onion and garlic fried in mustard oil. The simplest everyday dal is thin and liquid, served over rice as the base of a light meal. Thicker preparations such as dal bhuna, in which the dal is cooked down to a concentrated paste with fried onions and spices, are richer and served with flatbreads. Moong dal is particularly important in Bangladesh as the lentil used for bhuna khichuri (the beloved spiced rice-lentil dish), and is also dry-roasted and used in sweets. Chana dal paste (besan) is used in fritters (pakora), flatbreads (missi roti), and as a thickener. Dal is eaten daily as part of the traditional dal-bhat (dal and rice) combination that forms the backbone of the Bangladeshi diet, providing essential plant-based protein and dietary fibre across socioeconomic groups. In times of food scarcity, dal and rice represent the most economical and nutritious combination available.

title: Bhuna (slow sauté / reduction)
full_text: Bhuna (also spelled bhuno or bhona) is the central cooking technique of Bangladeshi and Bengali cuisine, in which onions, ginger, garlic, and spices are slowly sautéed and fried in oil over medium heat with constant stirring, then concentrated through the gradual evaporation of moisture until the mixture is reduced to a thick, deeply flavoured, almost paste-like masala base. The word bhuna derives from the Hindi/Urdu verb meaning "to fry" or "to roast." The technique is considered the foundation of most Bangladeshi meat curries, fish preparations, and vegetable dishes. The process proceeds in stages: first, sliced onions are fried in mustard oil or ghee until they pass through translucent, then golden, then a deep caramel-brown (this stage, called "peyan kacha" in Bengali, can take 20–30 minutes on its own). Ginger and garlic pastes are then added and fried until their raw smell completely cooks out. Ground spices — turmeric, red chilli, coriander, cumin — are incorporated next with small splashes of water to prevent burning, then fried until the oil separates from the masala. The moment the oil visibly pools around the edges of the spice paste is the diagnostic sign that the bhuna is complete. Only then is the main ingredient — fish, meat, or vegetables — added. A dish cooked entirely by this method, producing very little gravy and intensely concentrated flavour, is called a bhuna preparation (gosht bhuna, murgi bhuna). This technique requires patience and attentiveness; shortcuts produce flat, raw-tasting curries.

title: Bhutanese cuisine
full_text: Bhutanese cuisine is the traditional food culture of the Kingdom of Bhutan, a small landlocked country in the Eastern Himalayas situated between India and China. It is characterised by bold, intensely spicy flavours, minimal use of elaborate spice blends, and a heavy reliance on locally grown ingredients — particularly chili peppers, local cheeses, red rice, buckwheat, and dairy products from yaks and cows. Bhutanese cuisine has been most strongly influenced by Tibetan culinary traditions, with secondary influences from Nepal and northeastern India. The defining feature of Bhutanese food is its treatment of chili peppers not as a seasoning but as a primary vegetable: whole fresh or dried chillies are eaten in large quantities as the main ingredient of stews and side dishes, rather than being used sparingly to add heat. The national dish, ema datshi (chili and cheese stew), embodies this philosophy entirely. Dairy products — particularly the soft fresh cow's or yak's milk cheese called datshi and the hard dried yak cheese chhurpi — are used extensively in cooking and as daily snacks. Bhutanese red rice (a semi-milled variety with a distinctive nutty flavour) is the primary staple grain, grown in the Paro Valley and eaten at virtually every meal. Buckwheat is grown in the Bumthang region and used for noodles (puta), pancakes (khur-le), and dumplings (hoentay). Meats consumed include yak, beef, pork, chicken, and lamb. Buddhism profoundly influences Bhutanese food culture: many people avoid killing animals directly, and vegetarian dishes are common.

title: Ema datshi
full_text: Ema datshi (Dzongkha: ཨེ་མ་དར་ཚིལ་, meaning "chili cheese") is the national dish of Bhutan and the most iconic preparation in Bhutanese cuisine. It is a spicy stew made from whole fresh or dried chili peppers simmered together with the local fresh cow's or yak's milk cheese called datshi, along with onion, garlic, tomatoes, butter, and salt, until the cheese melts into a thick, creamy sauce surrounding the softened whole chillies. Unlike most cuisines where chillies are used as a spice, in ema datshi the chillies function as the primary vegetable — they are eaten in substantial pieces as the main component of the dish, not as a flavouring agent. The variety of chillies used (sha ema) is a Capsicum annuum cultivar similar in heat level to cayenne, poblano, or Anaheim peppers; several varieties may be used, including green, red, and white (sun-dried green) chillies. The dish appears at every meal throughout Bhutan — from humble farmhouse tables to restaurant menus across the country. Numerous variations exist: kewa datshi uses potatoes instead of chillies; shamu datshi uses mushrooms; shakam datshi uses dried beef; and sikam datshi uses dried pork. Ema datshi is almost always served with a bowl of Bhutanese red rice and often accompanies other dishes such as phaksha paa. Its roots lie in Tibetan culinary tradition, adapted to Bhutanese local produce. The cheese used, datshi, ranges from fresh and mild to aged (zoetay), which is greenish, pungent, and stronger.

title: Phaksha paa
full_text: Phaksha paa (literally "pork" in Dzongkha) is a popular Bhutanese pork dish that is one of the most well-known meat preparations in the country. It is made from slices of pork — often pork belly or pork shoulder — stir-fried and then simmered with whole or cracked dried red chillies, radishes, and seasonal vegetables such as spinach, bok choy, or turnip greens, in a flavourful, spicy broth. The dish may be prepared as a relatively dry stir-fry or as a soup-like stew depending on the amount of liquid added. The dried chillies used are integral to the dish's character and are not just for heat — they provide a smoky, fruity depth of flavour that is central to many Bhutanese meat preparations. Pork is one of the most commonly eaten meats in Bhutan, along with yak, beef, chicken, and lamb. The pork used is often the cured or dried pork (shakam) rather than fresh pork, giving the dish a particularly intense, concentrated flavour. Phaksha paa is typically served with Bhutanese red rice and ema datshi or other datshi dishes. It belongs to a broader family of Bhutanese dried meat preparations that also includes shakam paa (dried beef with chillies), sikam paa (dried fatty pork rind with chillies), and yak sha (yak meat preparations). The dish reflects Bhutan's cold mountain climate and traditional food preservation practices, in which meats are dried over the winter for year-round use.

title: Jasha maru
full_text: Jasha maru (also spelled jasha maroo or jasha muru) is a spicy Bhutanese chicken stew that is among the most popular meat dishes in the country, considered somewhat milder than other Bhutanese preparations but still deeply flavoured. It is made from diced or minced chicken cooked with onions, garlic, ginger, green chillies, tomatoes, coriander leaves, and a small amount of water or chicken broth until the chicken is tender and the sauce is rich and aromatic. The preparation produces a semi-dry stew with a warming, ginger-forward flavour profile. Unlike ema datshi or phaksha paa, jasha maru does not rely primarily on dried chillies or cheese for its character; instead, the combination of fresh aromatics and tomatoes creates a brighter, lighter flavour. It is commonly enriched with a touch of butter or local cheese towards the end of cooking to give the sauce a slightly creamy finish. Jasha maru is served with red rice and is considered an accessible entry point for visitors to Bhutanese cuisine who find the more intensely chilli-forward dishes challenging. It appears on restaurant menus across Bhutan and is also commonly prepared in Bhutanese homes. The name "jasha" refers to chicken in Dzongkha, and "maru" describes the stewed, gravied preparation. The dish reflects the broader Bhutanese love for spiced, warming stews eaten alongside the country's distinctive red rice.

title: Hoentay
full_text: Hoentay (also spelled hentey or hoentey) is a traditional Bhutanese dumpling originating from the Haa Valley in western Bhutan, one of the most visited regions of the country. It is closely related to the Himalayan momo (steamed dumpling) but is distinctive in using a wrapper made from buckwheat flour rather than refined wheat flour, giving the dumpling a darker grey colour, a nuttier flavour, and a slightly denser, chewier texture. The filling of hoentay is typically made from a combination of spinach or turnip leaves, soft local cheese (datshi), and sometimes dried meat, mixed together with seasoning. The filled dumplings are pinched closed into a crescent or pleated shape and then either steamed or fried. Steamed hoentay are soft and yielding; fried versions develop a crispy exterior that contrasts with the tender, savoury filling inside. Hoentay is served with a side of chilli sauce or ezay (Bhutanese chilli condiment made from dried red chillies, garlic, and sometimes tomato and shallots). The dish reflects the importance of buckwheat in the cuisine of Bumthang, Haa, and other high-altitude regions of Bhutan, where buckwheat grows in conditions too cold for rice cultivation. Hoentay is eaten as a snack, starter, or light meal and appears prominently during festivals and celebrations in the Haa Valley. It has gained popularity across Bhutan and is now available in restaurants in Thimphu and Paro.

title: Red rice
full_text: Bhutanese red rice is a semi-milled, medium-grain variety of rice (Oryza sativa) that is the primary staple grain of Bhutan and one of the most distinctive food products associated with the country. It is predominantly grown in the terraced paddy fields of the Paro Valley and is the only variety of rice grown commercially at high altitude — approximately 2,400 to 2,600 metres above sea level. The rice retains its bran layer during milling, which gives it a characteristic pinkish-red colour and a slightly nutty, earthy flavour similar to brown rice but with its own distinctive profile. The texture when cooked is slightly sticky and chewy, making it well suited to eating with spiced stews and soups where it can soak up sauces. Nutritionally, Bhutanese red rice is richer in fibre, vitamins, and minerals than white rice and has a lower glycaemic index. It is served at virtually every meal in Bhutan alongside ema datshi, phaksha paa, jasha maru, and other dishes. Red rice has gained international recognition and is now exported globally through specialty food importers, where it is valued as a healthful whole grain with an unusual flavour. In Bhutan, the rice is deeply embedded in cultural identity: offering red rice at religious ceremonies, festivals, and community feasts is a central act of hospitality.

title: Buckwheat
full_text: Buckwheat (Fagopyrum esculentum and Fagopyrum tataricum) is one of the most important crops of Bhutan, particularly in the high-altitude regions of Bumthang, Haa Valley, and northeastern Bhutan, where the climate is too cold and the soil too rocky for paddy rice cultivation. Despite its name, buckwheat is not a true cereal grain — it is a pseudocereal, the seed of a flowering plant related to sorrel and rhubarb, and is naturally gluten-free. In Bhutanese cuisine, buckwheat flour is used to make several traditional preparations: hoentay (the distinctive dumplings of the Haa Valley, made with buckwheat wrappers and filled with cheese, spinach, and turnip leaves); khur-le (buckwheat pancakes, eaten for breakfast with cheese and butter or honey); and puta (buckwheat noodles from the Bumthang region, stir-fried with eggs, garlic, and vegetables). Roasted buckwheat is also eaten as a porridge (tsampa) or snack. Buckwheat has been cultivated in Bhutan for centuries and is considered a highly nutritious food, rich in protein (containing all essential amino acids), dietary fibre, magnesium, and antioxidants such as rutin. Its adaptation to cold, high-altitude conditions makes it an irreplaceable crop for mountain communities. Buckwheat flour has a distinctive grey-brown colour, a slightly bitter earthy taste, and a rustic texture that gives Bhutanese preparations made with it a characteristically hearty quality.

title: Chili peppers
full_text: Chili peppers (Capsicum annuum) occupy an exceptional place in Bhutanese food culture that is virtually unique in the world: rather than being used as a seasoning or spice, chillies are treated as a primary vegetable in Bhutanese cooking and are eaten in large quantities as the main ingredient of stews and side dishes. The chilli variety most commonly grown and used in Bhutan is the sha ema, a long pepper similar in heat level to cayenne, Anaheim, or Anaheim-type peppers. Bhutanese chillies are available in three main forms: fresh green chillies (the most pungent and widely used), sun-dried white chillies (green chillies that have been washed in boiling water and sun-dried, producing a mellower flavour), and dried red chillies (fully matured and dried, with a smokier, more concentrated heat). Chilies are not simply added to Bhutanese dishes — they constitute the structural core of the most important dish in the cuisine, ema datshi (chili and cheese stew), which is considered the national dish. They also appear prominently in phaksha paa (pork with dried red chillies) and in the condiment ezay (a paste of dried chillies, garlic, shallots, and tomatoes). Bhutanese people consume chilli peppers from infancy and associate a meal without substantial chilli heat as lacking — a common saying holds that any dish without ema is considered incomplete. Chilli farming is a significant agricultural activity in the country's southern lowland regions.

title: Yak products
full_text: Yak (Bos grunniens) products — particularly milk, butter, dried meat, and cheese — are among the most culturally important and nutritionally significant animal products in Bhutanese cuisine, particularly for communities in the high-altitude regions of northern Bhutan where yaks are herded. Yak milk is richer in fat and protein than cow's milk and is used primarily to produce butter and two forms of cheese. The fresh, soft cheese called datshi (the essential ingredient in ema datshi and the broader family of datshi dishes) is traditionally made from yak milk — though cow's milk datshi is now more common — and has a mild, slightly tangy flavour that enriches the cheesy sauces of Bhutanese stews. The hard, dried cheese called chhurpi (or chugo) is made by pressing yak milk curds, drying them in small cubes, and smoking them. Chhurpi is one of the world's hardest cheeses and is consumed not as a cooking ingredient but as a long-duration chew or suck, a daily snack particularly popular among herders, school children, and anyone spending time outdoors in cold weather. Yak butter is used in the preparation of suja (butter tea), the traditional salt-butter tea that is a staple beverage throughout Tibet and the Himalayan Buddhist communities of Bhutan. Dried yak meat (shakam) is a preserved protein that is stewed with chillies (as in shakam datshi) or grilled as strips. Yak keeping is both an economic activity and a culturally significant aspect of nomadic pastoralist life in Bhutan's highlands.

title: Stewing with datshi (cheese)
full_text: Stewing with datshi (the soft, fresh cheese made from cow's or yak's milk) is the defining cooking technique of Bhutanese cuisine and the method by which the country's most beloved and most famous dishes are prepared. Datshi stewing is a simple technique in which a main vegetable, dried meat, or mushroom is simmered together with butter, water, and crumbled or cubed datshi cheese in a pot until the cheese melts and emulsifies into the cooking liquid, creating a thick, creamy, savoury sauce that coats the main ingredient. The technique is extraordinarily versatile and produces an entire family of dishes known collectively as datshis: ema datshi (chili and cheese), kewa datshi (potato and cheese), shamu datshi (mushroom and cheese), shakam datshi (dried beef and cheese), and sikam datshi (dried pork and cheese), among many others. The key to the technique is the behaviour of datshi itself: unlike most melting cheeses which become rubbery when overheated, datshi melts smoothly into the water to create a naturally thickened sauce without the need for flour, starch, or cream. Fresh garlic, onion, tomato, and green or dried chillies are the most common aromatics added to datshi stews. The result is a deeply comforting, protein-rich stew with a pleasingly pungent dairy character and significant chilli heat. Datshi stewing requires minimal equipment and produces food quickly, reflecting the practical mountain kitchen traditions of a country where fuel was historically scarce and efficiency prized.

title: Drying meat
full_text: Drying meat is a traditional food preservation technique that is central to Bhutanese highland food culture and has shaped the distinctive flavour profile of many of the country's most characteristic meat dishes. Bhutan's cold, dry mountain climate and high altitude — particularly in the northern and central regions where temperatures drop severely in winter — create ideal natural conditions for air-drying meat without refrigeration or salt as primary preservatives. The most commonly dried meats in Bhutan are yak, beef, and pork. Freshly slaughtered meat is cut into long strips or large flat pieces and hung on wooden racks or ropes in sheltered but well-ventilated areas — under eaves, inside smoking rooms, or in the cold, dry mountain air — where it dries slowly over several weeks to months. Cold smoking may be added to enhance flavour and further extend preservation. The resulting dried meat (called shakam for beef/yak and sikam for pork fat) has an intensely concentrated, slightly gamey, chewy character that is deeply different from fresh meat. These dried meats are then used as cooking ingredients in datshi stews: shakam datshi (dried beef with cheese and white chillies) and sikam datshi (dried pork fat with cheese and chillies) are among the most important dried meat preparations. The practice of meat drying also reflects Buddhist influences on Bhutanese society: rather than slaughtering animals at will throughout the year, communities traditionally performed seasonal slaughters in the autumn and preserved the meat for winter use, minimising the frequency of killing.

title: Indian cuisine (corpus planning note)

full_text: Indian cuisine is included in this South Asian corpus because the South Asian cuisine reference page lists India among the cuisines within scope. This corpus should cover the following regional/state or subregional traditions where relevant: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian. title: Indian cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Indian cuisine encompasses the extraordinarily diverse culinary traditions of the Indian subcontinent, shaped over 8,000 years of history by geography, climate, religion, ethnic diversity, and successive waves of cultural exchange. It is among the world's most complex and varied national cuisines, with each of India's 28 states and 8 union territories maintaining distinct food traditions rooted in local ingredients, religious practice, and historical circumstance. The staple foods are rice (dominant in the south, east, and coastal regions), wheat in the form of flatbreads — roti, chapati, paratha, naan, puri — (dominant in the north and northwest), and a vast range of pulses (dal) which serve as the primary protein source across much of the population. Approximately 30% of Indians are vegetarian, a proportion shaped by Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist religious traditions; the remaining majority eats meat, though beef is avoided by most Hindus and pork by Muslims. The major culinary inflections include: the Mughal heritage of aromatic rice dishes, kebabs, kormas, and tandoor cooking in the north; the rice-coconut-tamarind axis of South Indian cuisine; the fish-and-mustard traditions of Bengal in the east; the largely vegetarian and slightly sweet cuisine of Gujarat in the west; and the seafood-and-chilli traditions of the coastal states. Key ingredients used across Indian cooking include turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, chillies, mustard seeds, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, curry leaves, and asafoetida. India is the world's largest spice producer, and the spice trade from India to Europe was a primary catalyst for the European Age of Discovery.

title: Bengali cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Bengali cuisine is the culinary tradition of the Bengal region — shared between the Indian state of West Bengal and Bangladesh — and is one of the most distinctive, sophisticated, and historically significant regional cuisines of the Indian subcontinent. It is characterised by a structured multi-course eating tradition (the only such tradition in South Asia analogous to European service à la russe), a profound emphasis on freshwater fish, a characteristic five-spice blend called panch phoron (mustard, cumin, nigella, fenugreek, fennel), the pervasive use of mustard oil as the cooking medium, and an extraordinary tradition of milk-based sweets. A traditional Bengali meal proceeds in courses: beginning with a bitter preparation (shukto — a mixed vegetable dish with bitter gourd), followed by shak (leafy greens), dal, vegetable dishes, fish curry (machher jhol), and finishing with chutney and sweets. Fish, particularly freshwater varieties including rohu, katla, hilsa (ilish), and catfish, occupies an almost sacred cultural role — the Bengali identity phrase "mache bhate Bangali" ("fish and rice make a Bengali") encapsulates this. The hilsa (ilish) is particularly revered. Sweets made from chhana (fresh cottage cheese curd) including rasgulla, sandesh, mishti doi, and rasmalai are prized throughout India and beyond. East Bengali cooking (closer to Bangladesh) tends to be spicier than the milder, more refined West Bengali tradition. Mughal influence introduced rich kormas, biryanis, and kebabs into the Bengali repertoire, particularly in Kolkata.

title: Bihari cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Bihari cuisine is the traditional food culture of Bihar, a state in eastern India located between the Bengal region to the east and the Hindi-speaking heartland to the west, and reflects the agricultural character and predominantly Hindu and Muslim population of one of India's most densely populated states. The cuisine is generally simple, robust, and shaped by Bihar's status as one of India's most important wheat and paddy growing regions. Litti chokha is the most iconic Bihari dish — hard balls of whole wheat dough stuffed with sattu (roasted and ground chickpea flour) mixed with spices, baked directly over charcoal or in a wood fire until crisp, and served with chokha (a mashed preparation of roasted brinjal/eggplant and tomato seasoned with mustard oil, raw garlic, and green chilli). This combination is the defining rustic meal of Bihar and holds deep cultural significance as a symbol of the state's identity. Sattu — roasted gram flour — is the most characteristic ingredient of Bihari cuisine, consumed as a cold drink mixed with water and salt in summer, used as a stuffing, and incorporated into various preparations. Dal, rice, and seasonal vegetables form the everyday diet, with meat (goat curry — meat saalan) consumed on special occasions. Puran poli (sweet stuffed flatbread with jaggery and lentils), kheer, and various fried snacks are part of festival cuisine. The Mithila region of northern Bihar has its own distinct sub-tradition including makhana (foxnuts) preparations, litti, and specific sweets associated with the Madhubani culture.

title: Gujarati cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Gujarati cuisine is the traditional food of the state of Gujarat in western India, and is one of the most distinctive regional cuisines of the subcontinent — characterised above all by its predominantly vegetarian character (reflecting the strong Jain and Vaishnavite Hindu traditions of the region) and its characteristic sweet-sour-spicy flavour balance in which a small amount of sugar or jaggery is added to almost every savoury dish. Gujarat has one of the highest rates of vegetarianism in India, and Jain dietary restrictions (which also exclude root vegetables for many adherents) have profoundly shaped the cuisine's creativity with legumes, grains, dairy, and vegetables. The typical Gujarati thali is one of the most varied and colourful in India, featuring rotli (thin wheat flatbread), dal or kadhi (yoghurt-based curry), rice, several vegetable dishes (shaak), papad, pickle, and a sweet. The snack (farsan) culture of Gujarat is particularly celebrated — dhokla (steamed fermented chickpea flour cake), khandvi (rolled cooked chickpea flour tubes), thepla (fenugreek-spiced flatbread), fafda, chakli, and sev are among the most famous. Regional sub-traditions include North Gujarati, Kathiawadi, Kachchhi, and South Gujarati styles. Undhiyu (a mixed winter vegetable and bean dish), dal dhokli (wheat dumplings in spiced lentil curry), and mohanthal (besan fudge) are other iconic preparations. Buttermilk (chhaas) is the standard daily beverage. Gujarat's location on the Arabian Sea historically supported an active trade culture that brought diverse influences into the cuisine.

title: Kashmiri cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Kashmiri cuisine is the culinary tradition of the Kashmir Valley and the Jammu and Kashmir region — one of India's most distinctive and geographically isolated regional cuisines, shaped by the valley's cold mountain climate, Persian and Central Asian cultural influences (particularly following Timur's invasion in 1398 and subsequent rule by Muslim sultans), and the co-existence of Hindu Kashmiri Pandit and Muslim culinary streams. The most striking feature is the centrality of mutton — the Kashmir Valley has the highest per capita mutton consumption in South Asia — and the sophisticated spice vocabulary including Kashmiri saffron (among the world's finest, grown in Pampore), dried ginger (saunth), fennel seeds, cardamom, and Kashmiri red chilli (dried whole and used for deep colour and mild heat). The greatest culinary institution of Kashmir is the wazwan — an elaborate multi-course feast of up to 36 courses, cooked by specialist chefs called wazas, prepared exclusively for weddings and major celebrations. The wazwan is considered one of South Asia's grandest culinary traditions. Signature dishes include rogan josh (slow-cooked aromatic lamb in a rich red sauce derived from Kashmiri chillis), yakhni (lamb in a yoghurt-based aromatic broth), dum aloo (whole potatoes cooked in yoghurt-fennel sauce), gushtaba (minced meat dumplings in yoghurt gravy), and kahwa (a fragrant green tea with saffron, cardamom, and almonds). Kashmiri Pandit cuisine notably excludes onion and garlic, using asafoetida instead as an aromatic base.

title: Mughlai cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Mughlai cuisine is the distinctive culinary tradition developed in the imperial kitchens of the Mughal Empire (1526–1857), representing a synthesis of Central Asian (Turkic and Persian) cooking styles with the spices, techniques, and ingredients of the Indian subcontinent. It is the most historically influential of all Indian regional cuisines, having shaped the food traditions of northern India — particularly Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, and Hyderabad — and spawning the sub-traditions of Awadhi cuisine (Lucknow) and Hyderabadi cuisine (Hyderabad). The Mughal emperors were great patrons of the culinary arts; elaborate cookery books (Nuskha-i-Shahjahani, Khulāṣat-i Mākūlāt) from the period record hundreds of dishes. Key characteristics include the use of whole and ground aromatic spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, mace, nutmeg, saffron), dried fruits and nuts, rich cream and yoghurt-based sauces, slow-cooking and braising techniques for meats, and the tandoor clay oven for breads and grilled preparations. The Mughals introduced to India the layered rice dish (biryani — combining Persian pilaf with Indian spices), marinating meat in yoghurt before cooking, leavened breads, stuffed preparations, and milk-based desserts including halwa, falooda, and shahi tukra. Signature dishes include biryani, korma, nihari, seekh kebab, shami kebab, pasanda, murgh musallam, and naan. The Mughals avoided pork and beef as Muslims respectful of Hindu sensibilities, making lamb and chicken the primary proteins.

title: Punjabi cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Punjabi cuisine is the culinary tradition of the Punjab region of India (particularly the Indian state of Punjab, Haryana, and the Punjab-influenced cuisine of Delhi), and is the single most internationally recognised and globally exported regional Indian cooking style. It is characterised by robust, full-bodied flavours; generous use of dairy products (ghee, butter, cream, lassi, paneer); tandoor-cooked breads and meats; and a palette of warming spices including garam masala, coriander, cumin, and red chilli. The Punjab is India's most fertile agricultural state — known as the breadbasket of India — and this agricultural abundance is reflected in a cuisine that celebrates hearty, filling, protein-rich food. Wheat is the defining staple grain; breads including tandoori roti, naan, paratha, kulcha, and puri are central to every meal. Dairy is consumed in enormous quantities: pure desi ghee is used lavishly in cooking and finishing; white butter (makhan) is applied to hot breads; lassi (sweet or salted yoghurt drink) is the signature beverage. The most iconic Punjabi preparations include sarson da saag (mustard greens cooked in ghee) with makki di roti (corn flatbread), dal makhani (slow-cooked black lentils in cream and butter), butter chicken, tandoori chicken, chole bhature (spiced chickpeas with fried bread), and rajma chawal (kidney beans with rice). The tandoor clay oven is central to Punjabi cooking identity. Many of the dishes now globally recognised as "Indian food" — butter chicken, chicken tikka, naan, saag paneer — are Punjabi in origin.

title: Rajasthani cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Rajasthani cuisine is the food tradition of Rajasthan, India's largest state — a largely arid, semi-desert region characterised by extreme temperatures, scarce water, and limited agricultural diversity — and reflects these geographical constraints in its emphasis on cooking methods and preservation strategies designed for harsh conditions. Historically, food that could be cooked without water, could last for several days without refrigeration, and could be transported easily was preferred, shaping a cuisine quite distinct from the water-abundant cuisines of eastern and southern India. Dried and preserved foods, gram flour (besan) as a vegetable substitute, and cooking in ghee and milk rather than water are signature features. Daal baati churma is the most emblematic Rajasthani preparation: hard wheat flour balls (baati) baked in a cow-dung or wood fire or buried in embers, served with a robust five-lentil dal and churma (coarsely ground sweetened wheat crumble with ghee). Ker sangri (dried ker berries cooked with dried sangri beans) is a classic desert survival dish. Gatte ki sabzi (gram flour dumplings in yoghurt curry), laal maas (fiery red goat curry using dried mathania chillies), safed maas (white goat curry with almond-cream sauce), and dal baati all reflect the region's ingenuity with limited ingredients. The Marwari vegetarian sub-tradition from Jodhpur and Bikaner is one of India's most refined vegetarian cooking styles. Rajasthani sweets including ghevar, balushahi, and malpua are celebrated throughout North India.

title: Tamil cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Tamil cuisine is the culinary tradition of the Tamil people of Tamil Nadu in southern India and the Tamil-speaking communities of Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Singapore, and the global Tamil diaspora. It is one of the oldest continuous culinary traditions in the world, with references to specific foods and cooking techniques appearing in ancient Sangam literature from the 3rd century BCE onwards. Rice is the absolute staple, and a traditional Tamil meal served on a banana leaf — with rice at the centre surrounded by sambar, rasam, kootu (vegetable-lentil), poriyal (stir-fried vegetable), pickle, papad, curd, and payasam (dessert) — is one of the most formally structured meals in South Asian food culture. Tamarind is the defining souring agent, used in sambar, rasam, and chutneys. Coconut (grated, as milk, as oil) appears throughout the cuisine. A uniquely Tamil ingredient is the dried curry leaf (karapincha), used as an aromatic throughout cooking. Tamil cuisine is heavily vegetarian in the Brahmin (specifically Iyer and Iyengar) traditions, which also exclude garlic and onion. Non-Brahmin and coastal Tamil communities eat fish, seafood, and meat. Chettinad cuisine, from the Chettinad region in central Tamil Nadu, is the most intensely spiced Tamil regional style, famous for dishes like Chettinad chicken curry and kuzhi paniyaram. Breakfast preparations — dosa, idli, vada, pongal, upma — are among the most internationally recognisable South Indian foods. Filter coffee (kaapi) is the dominant beverage, prepared with freshly ground coffee through a traditional brass filter.

title: Kerala cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Kerala cuisine is the food tradition of Kerala, the narrow coastal state on India's southwestern tip, shaped by its unique geography as a land bordered by the Arabian Sea to the west and the Western Ghats mountain range to the east — a geography that gave rise to one of the world's most biodiverse spice-growing regions and one of the most maritime-influenced cuisines in South Asia. Kerala was for centuries the primary global source of black pepper, cardamom, ginger, and cinnamon, and the spice trade brought Arab, Portuguese, Dutch, and British traders who all left culinary imprints. Coconut is the foundational ingredient — coconut oil (not mustard oil or ghee) is the standard cooking fat, fresh grated coconut and coconut milk appear in the majority of curries and preparations, and the tall coconut palm is the state's most recognisable symbol. Rice (particularly red rice varieties) is the staple grain. Fish and seafood are central to the cuisine due to the extensive Kerala coastline, backwaters, and river systems: karimeen (pearl spot), fish moilee (coconut milk fish curry), prawn curries, and dried fish preparations are fundamental. The Syrian Christian and Mappila (Kerala Muslim) communities have distinctive meat-based traditions including beef preparations. Sadya — a traditional vegetarian feast of 28 or more dishes served on a banana leaf during the harvest festival of Onam — is the definitive Kerala ceremonial meal. Kerala also has a strong breakfast tradition of puttu (steamed rice flour cylinders with coconut), appam (rice hoppers), and idiyappam (string hoppers) with stew or curry.

title: Karnataka cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Karnataka cuisine encompasses the food traditions of Karnataka, a large south-western Indian state with significant regional diversity between its coastal Konkan strip, the Deccan plateau interior, the Malnad hills, and the Chettinad-influenced southern districts. Several distinct sub-cuisines exist: Udupi cuisine (from the coastal town of Udupi) is one of India's most celebrated pure vegetarian cooking traditions, associated with the temples of Udupi and their influence on vegetarian restaurant cooking across India; Mangalorean cuisine (from the port city of Mangalore) emphasises seafood and coconut with Portuguese and Goan influences; Kodava (Coorg) cuisine centres on pork and a distinctive red rice; and Mysore cuisine has a slightly sweet character influenced by the former Mysore Kingdom. The statewide staple is rice, served with sambar, rasam, kootu, and various chutneys. Ragi (finger millet) is a nutritionally important ancient grain used in ragi mudde (balls of cooked ragi flour) particularly in rural northern Karnataka. Bisibelebath (hot lentil-rice dish with ghee and vegetables), Mysore masala dosa (the buttered, spiced version of the crispy rice-lentil crepe), and Bisi bele bath are iconic preparations. The use of coconut in cooking is more prevalent in the coastal south, giving way to groundnut and sesame use in the drier Deccan plateau. Karnataka is noted for its coffee production around Chikkamagaluru and Coorg, and filter coffee is the signature beverage of the region. Chiroti (flaky sweet pastry) and Mysore pak (ghee fudge) are celebrated sweets.

title: Andhra cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Andhra cuisine is the culinary tradition of Andhra Pradesh, a coastal Telugu-speaking state in southeastern India, and is widely considered the spiciest regional cuisine in India — characterised by fierce use of dried red chillies (particularly the distinctive guntur chilli), tamarind for souring, and a cooking style that employs generous quantities of chilli paste and oil. The cuisine belongs to the broader Telugu culinary tradition shared with the neighbouring state of Telangana. Rice is the absolute staple, eaten with a variety of lentil preparations (pappu — Andhra-style dal), stewed vegetables (pulusu), and intensely spiced curries. Gongura — a sour leafy vegetable (Hibiscus sabdariffa, sorrel leaves) that is used as a pickle, chutney, and cooking ingredient — is the most characteristic ingredient in Andhra cooking, unique in South Asia for its sharp, sour tang. Avakaya (raw mango pickle made with mustard seed, dried chilli, and oil) is another defining Andhra condiment, considered one of India's finest regional pickles. Coastal Andhra produces excellent seafood preparations including prawn, crab, and fish curries. Andhra biryani is noted for its intensity and the use of dum cooking. The breakfast tradition includes pesarattu (a green mung bean dosa unique to Andhra), upma, and idli-vada. Perugu (yoghurt) and its fermented variant are consumed to counteract the cuisine's intense heat. The city of Hyderabad, historically in the Andhra region though now the capital of Telangana, hosts the famous Hyderabadi biryani tradition.

title: Hyderabadi cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Hyderabadi cuisine (also called Deccani cuisine) is the culinary tradition of the city of Hyderabad and its historic surrounding region in the Deccan plateau — an extraordinarily sophisticated urban culinary tradition that developed under the patronage of the Nizams of Hyderabad, who ruled as the wealthiest princes in colonial-era India from the 17th to 20th centuries. It represents a fusion of Mughlai, Arabic, Persian, and Turkic cooking styles with the South Indian ingredients and techniques of the Telugu and Kannada culinary traditions, producing a cuisine that uniquely bridges North and South Indian flavour registers. The defining signature is the use of tamarind, dried coconut, and red chillies alongside the aromatic spices of Mughal cooking — a combination that appears nowhere else in such balance.
