The crowning jewel of Hyderabadi cuisine is dum biryani — long-grain basmati rice layered with marinated mutton (the kacchi biryani method, in which raw marinated meat is layered with partially cooked rice and finished by dum), flavoured with saffron, fried onions, mint, and rose water, and cooked in a sealed pot. Hyderabadi biryani is widely considered the finest biryani tradition in India. Hyderabadi haleem — a slow-cooked porridge of wheat, lentils, and minced meat — is a GI-protected product and an iconic Ramadan preparation. Other signature dishes include baghara baingan (eggplant in a sesame-peanut-coconut sauce), mirchi ka salan (chilli curry in groundnut-sesame sauce), double ka meetha (bread pudding), and qubani ka meetha (apricot pudding). The formal Hyderabadi dinner, called the dastarkhwan, traditionally proceeds through five courses.

title: Awadhi cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Awadhi cuisine is the culinary tradition of the Awadh region of northern India, centred on the city of Lucknow in Uttar Pradesh — one of the most refined and celebrated of all Indian regional cooking styles, developed under the patronage of the Nawabs of Awadh (Oudh) who governed the region from the early 18th century and who were renowned for their cultivation of the arts, poetry, music, and above all food. Awadhi cuisine shares Mughal heritage with many North Indian traditions but is distinguished by its extreme subtlety, delicacy, and restraint compared to the bolder Mughlai style — where Mughlai cooking tends to richness through cream and dried fruits, Awadhi cooking achieves complexity through slow cooking, careful spicing, and the perfection of technique. The defining Awadhi technique is dum pukht (literally "steam-cooked") — the sealing of a heavy-bottomed vessel (handi) with dough or cloth, trapping all steam and allowing the ingredients to cook in their own vapours and juices over a very low flame. This technique preserves and concentrates every nuance of flavour and aroma. Kebabs are central to Awadhi identity: galouti kebab (minced meat with over 100 spices, shaped into patties so soft they "melt on the tongue"), seekh kebab (lamb mince on skewers), kakori kebab, and shami kebab are all Lucknawi classics. Awadhi biryani (dum-cooked with aromatic spices and saffron), nihari (slow-cooked lamb shank), warqi paratha (layered flaky flatbread), and sheermal (saffron milk bread) complete the canon. The bawarchis (master chefs) of Awadh are credited with inventing the dum cooking technique.

title: Goan cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Goan cuisine is the food tradition of Goa, a small coastal state on India's southwestern coast with a history unlike any other Indian state — it was a Portuguese colony for 451 years (1510–1961), and this unusually long colonial relationship, combined with Goa's pre-existing Konkani Hindu and Saraswat traditions, produced a cuisine that sits at the fascinating intersection of Indian, Portuguese, and Southeast Asian culinary elements. The most striking feature is the widespread use of vinegar (introduced by the Portuguese) to marinate and preserve meats, and the uninhibited consumption of pork and beef among the substantial Catholic Goan community — making Goa the only Indian state where pork curry (pork vindaloo, xacuti, sorpotel) is a fundamental and beloved everyday preparation rather than an exception. Rice and fish are the universal staples, reflecting the state's coastal geography and abundant fishing grounds: kingfish, pomfret, mackerel, shark, crabs, prawns, lobster, and mussels all feature prominently. Coconut milk, fresh coconut, and kokum (a souring agent from the mangosteen family) are characteristic Goan ingredients. Vindaloo (from the Portuguese vinha d'alhos — wine/vinegar and garlic marinade) is the most internationally recognisable Goan dish, though the restaurant version served globally is often an inauthentic hot curry. Other signature preparations include Goan fish curry (with coconut milk and kokum), sorpotel (pork offal in vinegar-spiced sauce), chouriço (Goan pork sausage), bebinca (layered coconut milk cake), and poi (bread rolls). Feni — distilled from coconut sap or cashew fruit — is the traditional Goan spirit.

title: Maharashtrian cuisine
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Maharashtrian cuisine is the food tradition of Maharashtra, India's third-largest state, encompassing the country's financial capital Mumbai and a vast and geographically diverse territory that stretches from the Arabian Sea coastline through the Western Ghats hill ranges to the Vidarbha plateau. The cuisine reflects this diversity: coastal Maharashtra (the Konkan coast, particularly Malvani cuisine) features rice, fish, and coconut milk preparations very similar to Goa; the Western Ghats and Deccan plateau interior uses jowar (sorghum), bajra (pearl millet), and groundnut in place of rice and coconut. This fundamental coastal-interior divide makes Maharashtrian cuisine among the most internally varied of Indian regional traditions. Mumbai and its surroundings are home to one of India's most vibrant street food cultures: vada pav (deep-fried potato patty in a bread roll — considered Mumbai's signature street food), pav bhaji (buttery spiced vegetable mash with bread), misal pav (spiced sprouted lentils in gravy with bread), bhel puri, pani puri, and sev puri are all beloved preparations. Puran poli (sweet flatbread stuffed with jaggery-lentil filling) is the most important festival food. Kolhapuri cuisine from southwestern Maharashtra is noted for its fierce, oil-rich spicing — Kolhapuri chicken and mutton preparations using special Kolhapuri masala are beloved throughout India. Modak (sweet steamed dumplings of rice flour with coconut and jaggery filling) is the sacred food of Ganesh Chaturthi. Shrikhand (strained yoghurt sweetened with sugar and cardamom) is the signature Maharashtrian dessert. Maharashtrians also produce excellent rice-based snacks and fermented preparations.

title: Biryani
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Biryani is a mixed rice dish originating in South Asia, traditionally made with long-grain basmati rice, meat (chicken, goat, beef, lamb, or seafood), aromatic spices, and sometimes vegetables. Its name derives from the Persian word biryan or beriyan, meaning "to fry" or "to roast," and from birinj, the Persian word for rice. The dish is thought to have developed during the Mughal Empire, where the delicate Persian pilaf tradition met the pungent, spice-forward rice dishes of the Indian subcontinent. According to food historian Lizzie Collingham, biryani evolved in the royal kitchens of the Mughal court. Other accounts suggest the dish reached South India before the Mughal era, brought by travelling merchants or soldiers. Biryani is now the most-ordered dish on Indian online food-delivery platforms, widely served at weddings and celebrations across South Asia and among diaspora communities globally. The spices and condiments used include ghee, saffron, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, bay leaves, nutmeg, mace, coriander, mint, ginger, garlic, onions, tomatoes, and yoghurt. Two principal cooking methods exist: kacchi biryani, in which raw marinated meat and uncooked rice are layered and cooked together in a sealed pot (dum cooking); and pakki biryani, in which the meat and rice are partially cooked separately before being layered and finished together. Regional varieties include Hyderabadi biryani (made with goat meat and saffron, considered a culinary emblem of the city), Lucknawi Awadhi biryani, Kolkata biryani (notable for the addition of potato), Sindhi biryani, and many others. Biryani is typically accompanied by dahi chutney, raita, mirchi ka salan, or a sour aubergine dish.

title: Butter chicken
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Butter chicken, also known as murgh makhani (Hindi: मुर्ग मखनी), is a mildly spiced curry made from chicken cooked in a rich, creamy tomato and butter-based gravy. It is one of the most popular Indian dishes worldwide and is widely available on Indian restaurant menus across the globe. The dish originated in Delhi in the mid-twentieth century, most commonly attributed to Kundan Lal Gujral and the Moti Mahal restaurant. The standard account holds that leftover tandoori chicken was repurposed by simmering it in a simple sauce of tomatoes, butter, and spices, creating a dish that combined the smokiness of tandoor-cooked chicken with a mild, velvety gravy. The preparation begins with chicken marinated overnight in a mixture of yoghurt, lemon juice, Kashmiri red chilli, garam masala, ginger paste, and garlic paste. The marinated chicken is traditionally cooked in a tandoor, though grilling or oven-roasting are common alternatives. The gravy is made from pureed tomatoes enriched with butter and cream, and seasoned with cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, and dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi), which is considered an essential flavouring. The resulting dish is notably milder and slightly sweeter than many other Indian curries, a quality that has contributed significantly to its global appeal. It differs from the related chicken tikka masala in that it is cooked without onions and relies on butter rather than oil. Butter chicken is commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice.

title: Rogan josh
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Rogan josh is an aromatic curried meat dish central to Kashmiri cuisine, and one of the signature dishes of the wazwan, the traditional multi-course Kashmiri feast. It is made with red meat, traditionally mutton or goat, slow-braised in a gravy flavoured with garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic whole spices including cloves, bay leaves, green and black cardamom, and cinnamon. The dish takes its characteristic deep red colour primarily from Kashmiri chillies, which are relatively mild in heat, and sometimes from alkanet flower (ratan jot). The name is thought to derive from the Persian rawghan (clarified butter or oil) and jōš (to braise or stew), indicating meat stewed in ghee; an alternative etymology links it to the Kashmiri word for "red" combined with "meat." Two main cooking traditions exist: the Kashmiri Pandit method, which avoids onions, garlic, and ginger and relies on yoghurt and ghee for the base, and the Kashmiri Muslim method, which uses a paste of fried wild onions (pran) for depth of flavour. The meat is seared, then braised slowly in the spiced gravy until tender. The final dish has a rich, deeply aromatic sauce that is typically not fiery but full of warmth from the spice blend. Rogan josh is traditionally served with plain basmati rice or Kashmiri breads such as sheermal. The dish reflects a Persian and Mughal culinary heritage, brought to the Kashmir Valley during the Mughal era.

title: Chole bhature
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Chole bhature is a popular North Indian dish combining chole (spiced chickpea curry, also called chana masala) with bhatura, a large deep-fried bread made from maida (refined wheat flour). The chole is prepared by soaking white chickpeas overnight, then pressure-cooking them in a thick gravy of tomatoes, onions, ginger, garlic, and a blend of spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, amchur (dried mango powder), and garam masala. The resulting curry is rich, tangy, and deeply spiced. The bhatura is made from a soft, leavened dough of maida, yoghurt, and a small amount of semolina, which is rolled out and deep-fried in oil until puffed and golden. Chole bhature is particularly associated with the street food culture of Delhi and Punjab, and is widely regarded as a quintessential Punjabi dish. It is commonly eaten as a hearty breakfast or lunch, typically accompanied by sliced raw onion, pickled chillies, and mango pickle. In Delhi, the dish has iconic establishments dedicated to serving it, and it is also popular across Pakistan. While indulgent and calorically dense, it remains one of the most beloved and widely recognised combinations in Indian street food culture.

title: Dosa
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Dosa is a thin, crispy savoury crepe originating in South Indian cuisine, made from a fermented batter of ground black gram (urad dal) and rice. According to food historian K. T. Achaya, references in Sangam literature suggest dosa was already part of ancient Tamil cuisine around the first century CE, making it one of the oldest documented Indian dishes. The standard preparation involves soaking rice and black gram separately for several hours, then grinding them to a smooth batter, combining them, and allowing the mixture to ferment overnight. The ratio of rice to lentils is typically 3:1 or 4:1. Fermentation enhances the nutritional profile and produces the characteristic mild tang. The batter is ladled onto a hot greased iron griddle (tawa) and spread in a thin, circular motion. It is cooked with oil or ghee until golden and crisp, then folded or rolled. Dosa is high in carbohydrates and protein and is naturally gluten-free and vegan. It is most commonly served hot with coconut chutney, sambar, and idli podi (a dry spiced lentil powder). The most popular variety is masala dosa, in which a filling of spiced potato and onion is placed inside the crepe before serving. Other variants include plain (sada) dosa, paper dosa (very thin and crispy), neer dosa, rava dosa (made with semolina), and pesarattu (made from green gram). Dosa is now widely eaten across India and in South Asian diaspora communities worldwide.

title: Idli
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Idli (also spelled idly) is a soft, round steamed rice cake that is one of the most important staple foods of South Indian and Sri Lankan cuisine. It is made from a fermented batter of parboiled rice and hulled black gram (urad dal) in an approximate ratio of 4:1, which are soaked separately for several hours, ground to a smooth and fluffy batter, combined, and then allowed to ferment overnight in a warm environment. The fermentation process is essential: it breaks down starches for easier digestion, increases vitamin B and C content, and produces the characteristic light, slightly sour taste. After fermentation, the batter is poured into perforated round moulds and steam-cooked for ten to twenty-five minutes until firm and spongy. Food historian K. T. Achaya notes that the modern idli may have roots in present-day Indonesia, which has a long tradition of fermented foods. Written records in Kannada literature from around 920 CE describe a dish called iddali made only of fermented black gram; the inclusion of rice in the batter appears in Indian texts after the twelfth century. Idli is typically eaten for breakfast or as a snack and is considered one of the healthiest foods in Indian cuisine due to its steamed preparation and fermented ingredients. It is traditionally served with sambar (a lentil and vegetable stew), coconut chutney, and podi (dry spice powder). There are numerous regional variations, including rava idli (made with semolina), stuffed idli, and mini button idli.

title: Vada
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Vada (also spelled wada or wade) is a category of savoury fried fritters or dumplings that are a staple in South Indian cuisine and widely eaten across India. The most common variety is medu vada (also called ulundu vada), a ring-shaped fritter with a characteristic hole in the centre, made from a thick batter of soaked and ground black gram (urad dal) seasoned with black pepper, cumin, green chillies, ginger, and curry leaves. The batter is shaped by hand or with a mould and deep-fried in oil until golden brown and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and fluffy inside. Medu vada is traditionally served as a breakfast or snack alongside sambar and coconut chutney, or soaked in sambar to make the popular dish sambar vada. Other well-known varieties include masala vada (also called chana dal vada), made from coarsely ground Bengal gram with onions, spices, and green chillies; dahi vada, soft lentil dumplings soaked in chilled, sweetened yoghurt and garnished with tamarind chutney and chaat masala; and batata vada, a Maharashtrian deep-fried potato dumpling coated in chickpea batter, popular as a street food and the filling in vada pav. Vada is a versatile dish found across regions under different names and with slightly different preparations, reflecting the diversity of South Asian cooking traditions.

title: Sambar
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Sambar (also spelled sambhar) is a lentil-based vegetable stew that is a cornerstone of South Indian cuisine, eaten in the states of Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana. It is made by cooking split pigeon peas (toor dal) until soft and combining them with a tamarind-based broth, a variety of vegetables, and a distinctive spice powder known as sambar masala or sambar powder. Common vegetables include drumstick (moringa pods), tomatoes, shallots, okra, pumpkin, carrots, eggplant, and green beans. The spice powder blend typically contains coriander seeds, cumin, fenugreek, dried red chillies, mustard seeds, urad dal, black pepper, turmeric, and asafoetida (hing). The dish is finished with a tempering (tadka) of oil or ghee bloomed with mustard seeds, dried red chillies, and curry leaves, which is poured over the stew. The recorded history of sambar is debated: one popular legend holds that it was first made in the Thanjavur Maratha kingdom during the seventeenth century and named after the Maratha king Sambhaji. Tamil records from the sixteenth century also reference sambar-like preparations. Sambar is integral to South Indian meals and is served with steamed rice, idli, dosa, medu vada, and uttapam. Each household and region has its own variant, with proportions of tamarind, spice, and vegetables varying considerably. A coconut-based version is common in Kerala, while the Tamil Nadu version is known for its sharp tamarind tartness.

title: Paneer tikka
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Paneer tikka is a popular North Indian appetiser and snack made by marinating cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) in a spiced yoghurt mixture and grilling them in a tandoor (clay oven) or over a charcoal grill. The marinade typically contains yoghurt, ginger-garlic paste, red chilli powder, turmeric, garam masala, cumin, coriander, and sometimes carom seeds (ajwain) and dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi). The paneer cubes are usually threaded onto skewers along with coloured capsicum (bell peppers), onion wedges, and tomatoes to create visually striking and flavourful skewers. After grilling, the paneer tikka is served hot, finished with a squeeze of lemon juice and a sprinkle of chaat masala, alongside mint chutney and sliced onion rings. The dish is considered the vegetarian counterpart of chicken tikka and shares a similar preparation philosophy. Paneer tikka has become one of the most widely recognised Indian vegetarian dishes internationally and is a fixture at weddings, restaurants, and celebrations. A variant known as paneer tikka masala is made by simmering the grilled paneer in a creamy tomato-based sauce, paralleling the structure of chicken tikka masala. The dish has been adapted into fusion applications such as pizza toppings, wraps, and sandwiches.

title: Palak paneer
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Palak paneer is a dish from North Indian and Punjabi cuisine consisting of cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) served in a thick, smooth gravy made from pureed spinach (palak in Hindi). It is one of the most widely recognised North Indian vegetarian dishes and is a staple in both home cooking and Indian restaurants worldwide. To prepare palak paneer, fresh spinach is blanched briefly in boiling water, then blended into a smooth puree. Separately, onions, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes are sautéed to build a base, then the spinach puree is added. The gravy is seasoned with garam masala, turmeric, cumin, coriander, and red chilli powder. Paneer cubes, which may be lightly fried or added directly, are then simmered in the puree until heated through. The dish is often finished with a small amount of cream and a squeeze of lemon juice to balance the bitterness of the spinach. The resulting dish is vibrant green in colour, mild to moderately spiced, and nutritionally dense, combining the protein of paneer with the iron and vitamins of spinach. It is commonly served with roti, paratha, or naan, and accompanies dal and rice in a traditional North Indian thali. A related dish, saag paneer, uses a mixture of spinach and other leafy greens such as mustard greens, creating a more robust and earthy flavour profile.

title: Dal makhani
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Dal makhani is a rich, slow-cooked lentil dish from North Indian and Punjabi cuisine, made primarily from whole black lentils (urad dal) and red kidney beans (rajma), simmered overnight with tomatoes, cream, and butter. It is one of the most famous dishes of Punjabi cooking and is associated with the dhaba (roadside restaurant) culture of Punjab. The preparation begins by soaking the lentils overnight, then slow-cooking them for many hours — traditionally in a clay pot over a wood fire — until the skins soften and the lentils become creamy. The gravy is built from a base of butter, onions, ginger, garlic, and pureed tomatoes, seasoned with cumin, coriander, chilli powder, and garam masala. Generous quantities of butter and cream are added toward the end of cooking to give the dish its characteristic richness and silky texture. The slow, prolonged cooking is essential to developing the deep, complex flavour and the thick, unctuous consistency for which dal makhani is renowned. The dish is often garnished with a swirl of cream, fresh coriander, and a knob of butter before serving. It is typically eaten with naan or roti and is considered one of the quintessential dishes of North Indian cuisine, particularly associated with Punjabi celebrations and restaurant menus worldwide.

title: Masala dosa
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Masala dosa is a roasted, stuffed variant of the South Indian dosa — a thin, crispy crepe made from fermented rice and black gram batter — filled with a spiced potato and onion mixture called aloo masala. It is considered one of the most iconic dishes of South Indian cuisine and is eaten widely across India. The dosa batter is prepared in the same way as a plain dosa: rice and urad dal are soaked, ground to a smooth batter, and fermented overnight. The aloo masala filling is made by boiling potatoes and mashing them roughly, then tempering them with mustard seeds, curry leaves, dried red chilli, green chilli, ginger, turmeric, and fried onions. The batter is spread thinly on a hot greased griddle, the filling is placed in the centre, and the dosa is folded or rolled around it. Masala dosa is served with coconut chutney, sambar, and sometimes tomato chutney. It is often described as a breakfast dish, although it is eaten at all times of day in South India and is a popular restaurant item nationwide. Udupi in Karnataka has a strong historical association with the masala dosa, with Udupi restaurants credited with popularising it across India. The dish is also widely available internationally in Indian diaspora restaurants. Paper masala dosa is a thinner, larger, and crisper variant, while set masala dosa is thicker and softer, reflecting regional preferences.

title: Pav bhaji
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Pav bhaji is a popular street food and fast food dish from Maharashtra, particularly associated with Mumbai, consisting of a thick, spiced vegetable curry (bhaji) served with soft, buttered bread rolls (pav). The bhaji is made from a medley of mashed vegetables that commonly include potatoes, green peas, carrots, cauliflower, bell peppers, and tomatoes, all cooked together with onions, ginger, garlic, and a distinctive spice blend called pav bhaji masala. The mixture is cooked on a large flat griddle (tawa) with generous amounts of butter, mashed vigorously as it cooks to achieve a thick, uniform consistency. The pav (small dinner rolls of Portuguese origin) are sliced and toasted on the same buttered griddle. Pav bhaji is served garnished with finely chopped raw onion, fresh coriander, a squeeze of lemon, and a pat of extra butter. The dish is said to have originated in the 1850s as a quick, nourishing meal for textile mill workers in Mumbai, allowing a filling lunch to be prepared and eaten rapidly. Today it is one of the most recognisable street foods of India, sold from roadside stalls, street carts, and restaurants alike, and has a significant presence in Indian diaspora communities. Variations include cheese pav bhaji, jain pav bhaji (without onion and garlic), and mushroom pav bhaji.

title: Poha
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Poha (also spelled pohé or pohe) is a light, flattened rice dish commonly eaten as a breakfast food or snack throughout India, especially in the states of Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Gujarat, and parts of Rajasthan. It is made from flattened rice (also called poha, chivda, or beaten rice), which is rice that has been parboiled, rolled flat, and dried. To prepare the dish, the flattened rice flakes are briefly rinsed in water to soften them, then cooked with a tempering of oil, mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chillies, onions, and turmeric, which gives the dish its characteristic pale yellow colour. Roasted peanuts, fresh coriander, lemon juice, and sometimes finely grated coconut are added as garnish. Regional variations are significant: Indore in Madhya Pradesh is particularly famous for its poha, which is served with sev (crispy chickpea flour noodles) and jalebi on the side. In Maharashtra, kanda poha (onion poha) is the most common form. Batata poha includes cooked potato cubes. Poha is valued for being quick to prepare (usually ready in under fifteen minutes), light on the stomach, and relatively nutritious. It is a popular choice for school and office tiffin boxes across central and western India.

title: Kheer
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Kheer (also known as payasam in South India and payesh in Bengal) is a sweet pudding that is one of the oldest and most widely consumed desserts in the Indian subcontinent. It is made by slowly simmering milk with rice until the mixture thickens into a creamy porridge, sweetened with sugar or jaggery and flavoured with cardamom, saffron, and sometimes rose water or kewra water. The dish is often garnished with dry fruits and nuts such as cashews, almonds, raisins, and pistachios. According to food historian K. T. Achaya, kheer has been consumed in ancient India for thousands of years and is mentioned in temple traditions and ancient texts. It is a staple at Hindu religious occasions, weddings, and festivals such as Diwali and Holi. The most classic version uses basmati rice cooked slowly in full-fat milk, with the ratio of one part rice to four parts milk typical for a rich result. Regional variations are numerous: in Bengal, payesh is made with gobindobhog rice and sometimes sweetened with nolen gur (date palm jaggery); in South India, paal payasam is made with jaggery and coconut milk; and in many households, other ingredients such as vermicelli (seviya kheer), tapioca, or mung dal replace the rice. Kheer is considered a symbol of prosperity and is prepared as an offering (bhog) in temples across India.

title: Rasgulla
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Rasgulla (also spelled rosogolla or rosogolla in Bengali) is a syrupy dessert from the eastern Indian subcontinent, made from soft, spongy ball-shaped dumplings of chhena (freshly curdled cottage cheese) cooked in a light sugar syrup until the syrup fully permeates the balls. The dessert is closely associated with the culinary traditions of West Bengal and Odisha, and its precise origin has been the subject of significant debate between the two states. In 2017, West Bengal was granted a Geographical Indication (GI) status for Banglar Rosogolla; Odisha followed with a GI for Odisha Rasagola in 2019. To prepare rasgulla, milk is curdled with lemon juice or vinegar to form chhena, which is drained, kneaded to a smooth and slightly fatty dough, then shaped into small balls. These are simmered in a large pot of light sugar syrup at a rolling boil, causing the balls to expand to approximately twice their original size and become airy and spongy as the syrup permeates them. The dessert is typically chilled before serving and is flavoured with rose water or cardamom. Rasgulla is considered foundational to Bengal's mithai (sweet) culture and is celebrated during festivals, religious occasions, and family celebrations. Its canned version was innovated by Nobin Chandra Das's son K. C. Das in the early twentieth century, enabling the sweet to travel and be consumed across the subcontinent.

title: Gulab jamun
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Gulab jamun is a deep-fried milk-solid sweet soaked in a fragrant sugar syrup, and one of the most beloved desserts across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal. The name combines the Persian words gulab (rose water) and jamun (a dark Indian berry of similar size and colour). The sweet consists of small oval or round balls made from khoya (milk reduced to a solid paste by prolonged simmering), kneaded with a small amount of refined flour (maida) and a leavening agent, then deep-fried in ghee or oil at a low temperature until deep golden-brown, and immediately soaked in a warm sugar syrup flavoured with green cardamom, rose water, and sometimes saffron. The balls absorb the syrup over several hours, becoming soft, dense, and intensely sweet with a floral aroma. Modern recipes frequently substitute powdered milk for khoya. Gulab jamun developed during the Mughal Empire, blending Persian influence — in particular the use of rosewater syrup — with indigenous Indian milk-based confectionery traditions. The 12th-century encyclopedia Manasollasa references fried cheese fritters soaked in scented syrup, though without the rosewater connection. Gulab jamun is now sold at every level of Indian sweet-making, from street stalls and wedding buffets to fine restaurants, and is widely available in canned form internationally. Regional variants include kala jamun (darker, with a slightly caramelised crust) and Bengali pantua.

title: Samosa
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Samosa is one of the most widely recognised and popular snacks of South Asian cuisine — a triangular or cone-shaped pastry with a crisp, flaky outer shell made from refined wheat flour (maida), filled with a spiced mixture and deep-fried until golden brown. The most common filling in India is a combination of mashed boiled potatoes, green peas, green chillies, ginger, and spices including cumin, coriander, amchur (dried mango powder), and garam masala. Meat-filled versions, using minced lamb or chicken, are common in Pakistan and Afghanistan. The name samosa derives from the Middle Persian word sambosag; the dish can be traced to Central Asian and Middle Eastern culinary traditions, where it was known as samsa or sanbusak. It was introduced to South Asia during the Delhi Sultanate period, likely by travelling merchants and cooks from Central Asia and the Middle East. In India, samosas are ubiquitous street food and appear at tea-time, celebrations, religious gatherings, and in restaurant menus. They are typically served hot with mint-coriander chutney, tamarind chutney, or yoghurt. The dish has many international variants under different names: sambousek in the Arab world, samsa in Central Asia, sambusa in East Africa, and chamuça in Goa and Portugal. In the Maldives, a fish-filled version called bajiyaa is the local adaptation. Samosa chaat — a crushed samosa topped with yoghurt, chutneys, onions, and sev — is a popular street food variation.

title: Atta
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Atta (Hindi: आटा) is a type of whole-wheat flour produced in the Indian subcontinent, made by grinding the entire wheat kernel — bran, germ, and endosperm — in a stone mill (chakki) or disc mill. It is the most widely used flour in the Indian subcontinent and forms the basis of the most commonly eaten flatbreads: chapati, roti, paratha, and puri. Unlike Western whole-wheat flour, which is milled at lower temperatures, atta is ground at higher temperatures in the chakki process, resulting in characteristic starch damage that gives the dough greater water-absorption capacity and produces the soft, pliable texture essential for rolling thin flatbreads. Atta contains significant gluten, which provides the dough with elasticity and extensibility needed for the flatbreads to puff when cooked. It has a slightly nutty, earthy flavour compared to refined flour. Nutritionally, atta is superior to refined white flour (maida), retaining higher amounts of dietary fibre, B vitamins, iron, and magnesium from the bran and germ. Nearly 80–85% of wheat produced in India is processed into atta, reflecting how central flatbreads are to Indian daily diet. Atta is also used in Indian sweets such as atta halwa, and in some fried snacks. In areas of Pakistan, India, and Bangladesh where rice is less available, roti made from atta serves as the primary staple carbohydrate.

title: Ghee
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Ghee is a form of clarified butter originating in the Indian subcontinent, made by simmering unsalted butter over low heat until the water evaporates and the milk solids separate and settle, leaving behind a clear, golden, intensely nutty-flavoured fat. Unlike regular clarified butter, ghee is simmered for longer, which caramelises the milk solids slightly and gives it a distinctive roasted, rich aroma. The word ghee derives from the Sanskrit ghṛta. Ghee has been a cornerstone of South Asian cooking, medicine, and religious practice for thousands of years; it is referenced in the Vedas and used in Hindu ritual offerings. Because the milk solids and water have been removed, ghee has a higher smoke point than butter (approximately 250°C), making it well suited for high-temperature frying, sautéing, and tempering (tadka). It is also shelf-stable at room temperature for several months. In Indian cooking, ghee is used to cook flatbreads, to temper spices at the start of curries, to finish dal and rice dishes, and as a component of many Indian sweets including halwa, laddu, and kheer. It is considered a sacred and auspicious food in Hindu tradition. Ghee contains negligible amounts of lactose and casein, making it generally tolerable for those with mild dairy sensitivities. It is a rich source of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K, though its high saturated fat content means moderation is generally recommended.

title: Paneer
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Paneer is a fresh acid-set cheese that is the most widely consumed cheese in South Asian cuisine. It is made by heating full-fat cow or buffalo milk to boiling, then curdling it with an acidic agent such as lemon juice, vinegar, or citric acid, which causes the milk proteins to coagulate and separate from the liquid whey. The resulting curds are drained through muslin cloth, rinsed, and pressed under weight into a firm block. Unlike aged cheeses, paneer is unfermented and requires no rennet, making it suitable for vegetarians. Its mild, milky flavour and firm, non-melting texture — it holds its shape when cooked — make it exceptionally versatile. Paneer can be cubed and added to curries, marinated and grilled (as in paneer tikka), crumbled into fillings for parathas and samosas, or pressed more firmly and used in sweets. The origin of paneer is debated: some historians trace it to Afghan and Iranian influences during the Mughal period; others suggest the Portuguese introduced acid-curdling techniques to Bengal in the 17th century. Today, paneer is the foundational ingredient in many of North India's most beloved vegetarian dishes, including palak paneer, paneer tikka masala, shahi paneer, and matar paneer. It is a significant source of protein and calcium, particularly important in vegetarian diets across the subcontinent.

title: Turmeric
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Turmeric (Curcuma longa) is a rhizomatous plant native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, and its dried, powdered root is one of the most essential spices in Indian and South Asian cooking. The powder has a brilliant golden-yellow colour, a warm, earthy, slightly bitter and peppery flavour, and a mildly pungent aroma. Turmeric derives its colour and much of its biological activity from a compound called curcumin. In Indian cuisine, turmeric is used in virtually every savoury dish — from dals and curries to vegetable preparations and rice dishes — typically added in small quantities, around a quarter to half teaspoon, to provide colour, a mild earthy depth, and its preservative properties. It is a key component of curry powders and many regional spice blends. Turmeric has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for thousands of years for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties; fresh turmeric paste is still applied to wounds in traditional households. Turmeric is also integral to Indian religious and cultural life, used in Hindu ceremonies, wedding rituals, and festivals. India produces most of the world's turmeric, with Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Odisha, and Maharashtra being the major producing states. In South Indian cooking, fresh turmeric leaves are sometimes used to wrap and steam dishes such as Goan patoleo.

title: Cardamom
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Cardamom is one of the most prized and aromatic spices in the world, native to the forests of the Western Ghats in Southern India. Two main varieties are used in South Asian cooking: green cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum), also called "true" cardamom, which has a sweet, intensely floral, camphor-like aroma; and black cardamom (Amomum subulatum), which is larger, with a smoky, camphor-like flavour suited to savoury dishes. India, particularly the Cardamom Hills of Kerala, is the world's second-largest producer of cardamom after Guatemala. In Indian cuisine, green cardamom is ubiquitous in both sweet and savoury preparations: it is a core component of garam masala and chai (spiced tea), an essential flavouring in biryanis, rice pilafs, and kormas, and appears prominently in sweets such as kheer, gulab jamun, and halwa. The pods are often used whole in rice dishes and curries to infuse their fragrance; the seeds may be ground for use in spice powders and desserts. The word cardamom traces back to Sanskrit through Greek and Latin. In Ayurvedic medicine, cardamom is considered beneficial for digestion and is used to freshen breath. Black cardamom is reserved primarily for savoury preparations including slow-cooked meat curries, biryanis, and spice blends for North Indian cuisine.

title: Cumin
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Cumin (Cuminum cyminum) is a flowering plant in the family Apiaceae whose dried seeds are among the most widely used spices in South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Latin American cuisines. In Indian cooking, cumin (jeera in Hindi) is an indispensable spice used in virtually every regional cuisine. The small, ribbed seeds have a distinctive warm, earthy, slightly bitter flavour that intensifies considerably when toasted or fried in oil. Cumin is used in two primary forms in Indian cooking: whole seeds are added to hot oil at the start of cooking for tadka (tempering), producing a characteristic nutty fragrance; and ground cumin powder is added mid-cooking to build the base flavour of curries, dals, and vegetable dishes. It is a key ingredient in garam masala, chaat masala, dhana-jeera (a coriander-cumin blend popular in Gujarati cooking), and in spice rubs for tandoori preparations. In South Asian cooking, cumin is combined with coriander in roughly equal proportions to form the most common foundational spice pairing. Whole cumin seeds are used to flavour rice (jeera rice), raita (yoghurt condiment), and many flatbreads. Cumin is also an important ingredient in the spiced tea lassi and in digestive spice preparations. India is the world's largest producer of cumin, with most production concentrated in Gujarat and Rajasthan.

title: Coriander
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum), also known as cilantro or Chinese parsley, is a herb whose every part is used extensively in South Asian cooking: the fresh leaves, the dried seeds, and the roots. In Indian cuisine, coriander exists as two distinct ingredients. Fresh coriander leaves (dhania in Hindi) are used as an all-purpose garnish and fresh flavouring, scattered over finished curries, dals, biryanis, chutneys, and chaats for their bright, citrusy, herbal flavour. Coriander seeds, when dried and either used whole or ground into a powder, are a fundamental cooking spice with a warm, earthy, mildly floral character that differs markedly from the fresh leaves. Ground coriander is one of the most widely used ground spices in Indian cooking, typically paired with cumin in spice pastes and powder blends for curries, rice dishes, and kebabs. It forms the base of numerous regional spice blends including garam masala, sambar powder, and curry powder. Whole coriander seeds are used in tadka, pickles, and some rice preparations. India is among the world's leading producers of coriander seeds, with Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, and Andhra Pradesh as major producing states. Coriander has been cultivated in the Indian subcontinent for thousands of years and is mentioned in ancient Sanskrit texts. In Ayurveda, it is considered cooling and beneficial for digestion.

title: Garam masala
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Garam masala (Hindi: गरम मसाला, meaning "warm/hot spice blend") is a fragrant ground spice mixture that is one of the most important and distinctive seasoning blends in Indian and South Asian cooking. The name refers to the warming quality of the spices, which are associated in Ayurvedic tradition with raising body temperature and stimulating digestion. Unlike single-spice additions, garam masala is a complex blend whose composition varies significantly by region, household, and cook. However, core components typically include some combination of cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper, cumin, coriander, bay leaves, nutmeg, and mace. In North Indian cooking, the blend tends to emphasise cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves for sweetness and warmth; Kashmiri versions may include fennel and ginger; South Indian versions often incorporate more pepper. Garam masala is used differently from other spice powders: rather than being added at the start of cooking, it is most often added near the end, or sprinkled over a finished dish, to preserve its delicate volatile aromas which dissipate with prolonged heat. The spices are typically dry-roasted before grinding to intensify their flavour. Commercially prepared versions are widely available, though many Indian households still prepare their own, resulting in regional and family-specific flavour profiles. Garam masala is used in curries, biryanis, kebabs, marinades, and a wide range of vegetarian and meat dishes across the subcontinent.

title: Tamarind
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Tamarind (Tamarindus indica) is a leguminous tree native to tropical Africa but long cultivated throughout South Asia, and its tart, sour pulp is one of the most important souring agents in Indian cuisine, particularly in South Indian and West Indian cooking. The tree produces long brown pods containing a sticky, dark brown, acidic pulp surrounding hard seeds. Tamarind is available in several forms: as whole pods, as a compressed block of seedless pulp (the most common form in Indian households), as concentrated paste, and as a powder. In cooking, the pulp is dissolved in warm water and the resulting liquid is strained to produce tamarind water or tamarind paste, which is added to dishes for its characteristic tangy, fruity sourness. It is a defining ingredient in sambar (the South Indian lentil stew), rasam (a peppery thin soup), chutneys (particularly the dark sweet-sour tamarind chutney served with chaat and snacks), and in many South Indian rice dishes such as tamarind rice (puliyodarai). In West India, tamarind is used in Gujarat's sweet-sour dals and Maharashtrian fish curries. The Worcestershire sauce used in British cooking also contains tamarind. Tamarind is valued nutritionally for its tartaric acid content, which has antioxidant properties, as well as for its significant amounts of B vitamins, potassium, and iron. It is also used in traditional Ayurvedic medicine.

title: Coconut
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Coconut (Cocos nucifera) and its various products — fresh grated coconut, coconut milk, coconut cream, dried coconut (desiccated), and coconut oil — are foundational ingredients in South Indian, Sri Lankan, Goan, Keralite, and Maharashtrian cuisines. The coconut palm is often called the "tree of life" in the coastal regions of South India and Sri Lanka, where it grows abundantly and forms the culinary backbone of the local kitchen. Fresh grated coconut is used extensively in South Indian chutneys (particularly coconut chutney, served alongside idli and dosa), in vegetable stir-fries, in wet spice pastes called masalas, and as a finishing garnish for many dishes. Coconut milk, extracted by blending grated coconut with water and straining it, is the base of most Keralite curries and stews (particularly the iconic Kerala fish curry), as well as the rich rice dish paal payasam. Coconut oil is the primary cooking fat of Kerala and coastal Karnataka, imparting a distinctive flavour considered essential to authentic South Indian cooking. In Sri Lankan cuisine, coconut is equally central: coconut milk is used in curries, hoppers, and kottu, while the dried smoked flesh of the coconut (copra) is used in some preparations. Coconut also features in the sweets and festival foods of Goa, Bengal, and Maharashtra.

title: Dum cooking
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Dum cooking (also called dum pukht, meaning "to breathe in" in Persian/Urdu) is a slow-cooking technique used extensively in South Asian cuisine, particularly in Mughlai, Awadhi, and Hyderabadi cooking, in which food is sealed inside a heavy pot and cooked over very low heat using its own trapped steam and moisture. The technique was brought to India by the Mughals in the 16th century, influenced by Persian culinary traditions, and was refined in the royal kitchens of Lucknow, Delhi, and Hyderabad. In traditional dum cooking, a heavy clay or metal pot (handi) is partially cooked on the stove, then sealed with a tight-fitting lid secured with a rope of wheat-flour dough (atta), which forms an airtight gasket. The sealed pot is then placed over a very low flame while burning charcoal embers are placed on top of the lid, so that the food receives gentle heat from both above and below. This traps all the steam, aromas, and volatile spice compounds inside, allowing them to permeate every ingredient deeply, resulting in extremely tender meat, perfectly cooked rice, and an intense concentration of flavour. Dum cooking is most famously associated with biryani — particularly kacchi dum biryani, in which raw marinated meat and raw rice are layered together and cooked entirely by this method — as well as dum aloo, dum ka murgh (whole chicken), and various Awadhi-style slow-cooked kebabs. Modern adaptations often use a tight-fitting cast-iron lid or even a Dutch oven in place of the traditional sealed handi.

title: Tadka / tempering
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Tadka (also called tarka, baghar, chonkna, or phodni depending on the region) is one of the most fundamental and distinctive techniques in Indian cooking, referring to the process of heating oil or ghee to a high temperature and then frying whole spices and aromatic ingredients briefly in the hot fat to release their essential oils, volatile aromatic compounds, and flavours. This tempered fat mixture is then either added at the start of the dish as a flavour base or poured over the finished dish as a finishing touch. The technique is practised across the entire subcontinent in virtually every regional cuisine and is considered essential to the character of Indian cooking. Common ingredients used in tadka include cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chillies, curry leaves, asafoetida (hing), fenugreek seeds, nigella seeds, garlic, ginger, and onions. The correct sequence is important: whole spices go in first in the hot oil, followed by aromatics, then any fresh chillies or ginger-garlic. The process takes only seconds to minutes — when the seeds splutter and the spices turn fragrant, the tadka is ready. The same base technique underlies the construction of most Indian curries, dals, and vegetable dishes. In South India, tadka often features mustard seeds and curry leaves in coconut oil; in North India, it typically uses cumin seeds, garlic, and dried red chillies in ghee.

title: Tandoor cooking
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Tandoor cooking is a method of baking, grilling, and roasting food in a tandoor — a large cylindrical clay oven traditionally fired with charcoal or wood — at extremely high temperatures that can reach 480°C (900°F). The tandoor is of ancient origin in the Indian subcontinent, with clay ovens similar to the modern tandoor found at Harappan archaeological sites dating to around 2500 BCE. The technique was refined and popularised in the Punjab and across North India, and became closely associated with Mughal court cooking. Tandoor cooking is uniquely suited to two main applications: cooking flatbreads and roasting spiced meats and vegetables. Flatbreads such as naan and tandoori roti are slapped onto the clay interior walls of the oven, where they stick and cook rapidly in 90 seconds to two minutes, acquiring a characteristic charred, blistered surface and a smoky flavour from the wood fire. Marinated meats and vegetables are placed on long metal skewers, which are suspended vertically inside the burning tandoor. The intense dry heat cooks the food quickly from all sides, sealing in juices while creating a slightly charred, caramelised exterior. The clay walls also absorb and radiate heat evenly, contributing to the unique texture. Tandoori chicken, seekh kebabs, paneer tikka, and tandoori fish are among the most famous products of this technique. Because of the high temperatures, tandoors are mainly found in commercial restaurants; home cooks use grills or very hot ovens as alternatives.

title: Fermentation
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Fermentation is a cornerstone technique in South Indian cuisine and plays an essential role in the preparation of several of the most important foods in the region. Unlike the pickled, vinegar-based fermentations of other culinary traditions, South Indian fermentation is primarily lacto-fermentation driven by naturally occurring bacteria (particularly Lactobacillus species) present in the ingredients and environment. The most critical application is in the preparation of the batter for idli and dosa: rice and urad dal (black gram) are soaked separately, ground to a smooth batter, combined, and then left to ferment at room temperature for six to twelve hours (or longer in cooler weather). During fermentation, the batter rises and becomes airy, slightly sour, and significantly more digestible than the raw ingredients, as the fermentation process breaks down complex starches and anti-nutrients. The fermentation also increases the vitamin B and C content of the final product. The resulting idli and dosa are notably light, nutritious, and easy to digest. The fermentation principle also applies to appam (South Indian rice pancakes), uttapam, and idiyappam (string hoppers in Sri Lanka). Fermented rice water (panta bhat in Bangladesh and kanji in South India) is another traditional fermented preparation consumed for its probiotic and cooling properties. In Bhutan, fermented cheeses (datshi) and dried meats rely on fermentation and drying. The success of fermentation depends on ambient temperature, the quality of the water (chlorine inhibits fermentation), and the type of rice and dal used.

title: Bhuna
region: Bengali, Bihari, Gujarati, Kashmiri, Mughlai, Punjabi, Rajasthani, Tamil, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra, Hyderabadi, Awadhi, Goan, Maharashtrian, Northeast Indian
full_text: Bhuna (also spelled bhunao or bhuno) is a fundamental Indian cooking technique in which onions, ginger, garlic, spices, and other base ingredients are slowly sautéed and fried in oil or ghee over medium to medium-high heat with constant stirring until they are deeply browned, caramelised, and reduced to a thick, concentrated paste-like consistency. The word bhuna comes from the Hindi/Urdu verb bhunna, meaning "to roast" or "to fry." The technique is the foundation of most North Indian curries and gravies. The process begins with frying whole spices in hot oil, then adding onions and cooking them through three stages: translucent, then golden, then a deep caramel-brown. Ginger and garlic pastes are then added and fried until the raw smell is fully cooked out. Ground spice powders are added next, followed by tomatoes, and the entire mixture is fried continuously until the fat begins to separate from the masala at the edges of the pan — this separation, called "bhunoing off," indicates that the base is properly cooked and caramelised, which is critical to the depth of flavour in the finished curry. A dish described as "a bhuna" (as in bhuna gosht or bhuna chicken) is one cooked specifically with this dry-fried, intensely concentrated technique, resulting in very little gravy and very bold, deep flavour. Without thorough bhunoing, curries taste raw and flat; the technique is what gives North Indian curries their characteristic richness and depth.

title: Maldivian cuisine
full_text: Maldivian cuisine (also called Dhivehi cuisine) is the traditional food culture of the Republic of Maldives, an island nation scattered across the Indian Ocean, and is built almost entirely around three foundational ingredients: fish (primarily tuna), coconut (in all its forms), and starchy staples including rice, breadfruit, and various tubers. The cuisine is among the most seafood-centric in South Asia, reflecting the Maldives' geography as an archipelago entirely surrounded by the Indian Ocean with no land borders and extremely limited agricultural land. Skipjack tuna (kandu mas) is the dominant protein in virtually every meal, consumed fresh, grilled, smoked, sun-dried, and as the cured product known as Maldives fish (valhomas) — a shelf-stable, intensely umami-rich ingredient that is also exported widely and used in the cuisines of Sri Lanka and South India. Coconut is used in grated form, as coconut milk, and as coconut oil, providing richness, creaminess, and sweetness in curries and snacks. Rice, though not grown locally, has become a staple imported starch. The daily meal structure typically includes a breakfast of mas huni (tuna and coconut mixture) with roshi (flatbread) and sweet tea, lunch and dinner centred on garudhiya (tuna broth) or fish curry with rice, and an afternoon tea-time snack ritual (hedhika) with small fried savouries. Spices including chilli, curry leaves, cardamom, cinnamon, garlic, ginger, and black pepper are used generously, reflecting historical Indian Ocean trade route influences. The Maldives is a Muslim country; pork is absent and alcohol prohibited on local islands.

title: Garudhiya
full_text: Garudhiya (Dhivehi: ގަރުދިޔަ) is the national dish of the Maldives and the most fundamental preparation in Maldivian cuisine — a clear, deeply flavoured fish broth made by simmering fresh tuna (most commonly skipjack tuna) in water with salt and sometimes curry leaves and onion, until a richly flavoured, translucent broth is produced. It is one of the simplest dishes in the Maldivian culinary repertoire, requiring only a few ingredients, but its flavour is deep and satisfying, improving with successive reheatings over a period of days. Garudhiya is served at the centre of the traditional Maldivian meal, accompanied by plain steamed rice, fresh lime wedges, sliced raw onion, fresh or dried green chillies, lonumirus (a paste of ground dried chillies), and sometimes rihaakuru (the concentrated tuna paste), allowing each diner to season their own bowl to taste. The combination of the neutral broth poured over rice with the bright acid of lime and the heat of chilli constitutes the flavour template of Maldivian daily eating. Garudhiya is classified as a living cultural reference point: virtually every Maldivian household prepares it regularly, it is the first dish taught to children learning to cook, and it is often cited by Maldivians abroad as the dish that most powerfully evokes home. Beyond its role as food, garudhiya represents the fisherman's direct, unmediated connection to the sea — the morning's catch transformed with minimal intervention into the day's sustenance.

title: Mas huni
full_text: Mas huni (Dhivehi: މަސްހުނި, meaning "fish coconut") is the quintessential Maldivian breakfast dish and one of the most distinctive foods of the country, made from a simple combination of shredded or flaked tuna, freshly grated coconut, finely chopped onion, green chilli, lime juice, and salt, mixed together without cooking. The name directly describes the two primary ingredients: mas (fish) and huni (grated coconut). Traditionally, the tuna used was valhoamas — the cured, smoked tuna produced throughout the Maldives — which imparts a concentrated, smoky, intensely savoury flavour; today, canned tuna is widely used as a practical substitute. The mixture is served at room temperature, scooped up with freshly baked roshi (Maldivian flatbread), and accompanied by sweetened black tea. The combination of protein-rich fish, the fat and sweetness of coconut, the sharp heat of raw chilli and onion, and the brightness of lime creates a balanced, satisfying morning meal that is both nutritious and deeply flavourful. Mas huni is also notable for its adaptability: historically, when fish was scarce during the monsoon season, chopped leaves from local plants including moringa and sweet potato were added to extend or replace the fish component. The dish requires no cooking equipment beyond the coconut scraper (hunigondi), the traditional implement used to grate fresh coconut, and can be prepared rapidly. Mas huni is considered by Maldivians one of the most important expressions of their food identity and a dish inseparable from the experience of island morning life.

title: Bis keemiya
full_text: Bis keemiya is a popular Maldivian snack and street food — a small deep-fried triangular pastry filled with a savoury mixture, closely resembling the South Asian samosa in shape and method but with a distinctly Maldivian filling and flavour. The outer shell is made from a thin dough of refined wheat flour, shaped into a crescent or triangle around the filling and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The most traditional filling for bis keemiya combines thinly shredded cabbage and hard-boiled egg with onions, curry leaves, and sometimes tuna, seasoned with green chilli and black pepper. Some versions include a tuna-based filling mixed with coconut. The name is believed to derive from a combination of words meaning "egg pastry" — bis refers to egg in Dhivehi. Bis keemiya is one of the most widely consumed items at the hedhika (traditional afternoon tea-time snack ritual) that takes place in Maldivian households and tea shops daily, alongside other fried snacks such as bajiyaa (tuna-filled triangular pastry, directly comparable to Indian samosa), gulha (fried fish and coconut dumplings), masroshi (tuna-stuffed flatbread), and various fishcakes. The hedhika ritual accompanies strong, sweet black tea (sai) and is a fundamental social institution in Maldivian daily life. Bis keemiya demonstrates the South Asian and Arab trade influence on Maldivian cuisine — pastry-based snacks filled with spiced ingredients are found throughout the Indian Ocean trade sphere.

title: Fihunu mas
full_text: Fihunu mas is a popular Maldivian grilled fish dish in which whole fresh tuna or other ocean fish are coated in a spiced paste and grilled over hot coals or in a hot oven until the exterior is charred and caramelised while the flesh inside remains moist and flaky. The spice paste applied to the fish typically includes a blend of dried or fresh red chillies, cumin, curry leaves, onion, garlic, black pepper, and salt, which together create a pungent, warming crust on the fish during grilling. The name "fihunu" means grilled or roasted in Dhivehi, and "mas" means fish. Fihunu mas is a reflection of the Maldivian fisherman's tradition of cooking freshly caught fish over open fires or coals on the fishing boats (dhoni) or on the beach immediately after landing — a direct, uncomplicated way of transforming the raw catch into a meal with minimal equipment. The dish also appears as a celebration food at communal gatherings and as a standard item in traditional Maldivian home cooking. Skipjack tuna, yellowfin tuna, wahoo, and various reef fish species are all used for fihunu mas depending on what is freshly available. The grilled fish is typically served with plain rice, roshi (flatbread), or coconut rice, alongside a fresh salad of onion and lime or a small portion of garudhiya (tuna broth). The charred, spiced exterior of fihunu mas contrasts with the lighter preparations of everyday Maldivian fish cooking, giving it a festive and robustly flavoured character.

title: Tuna
full_text: Tuna, particularly skipjack tuna (Katsuwonus pelamis, called kandu mas or kanḍumas in Dhivehi), is the absolute centrepiece of Maldivian cuisine and the defining food of the Maldives — more culturally and nutritionally central to the Maldivian diet than any other ingredient in any South Asian cuisine. The Maldives' position in the centre of the Indian Ocean provides extraordinary access to large tuna populations, and sustainable pole-and-line fishing (a traditional method that targets individual tuna without bycatch) has been the foundation of the Maldivian economy and food supply for centuries. Tuna appears in Maldivian cooking in multiple preserved and fresh forms: fresh tuna (mas) is grilled, fried, boiled in garudhiya, or used in curries; cured and smoked tuna (valhoamas or Maldives fish) is the most important preserved ingredient, used in mas huni, masroshi, gulha, and as a flavouring in the cuisines of Sri Lanka and South India; rihaakuru is a thick, intensely flavoured dark paste produced by boiling down fresh tuna for many hours until it becomes concentrated, used as a condiment and paste; and dried tuna flakes are used in many snack preparations. The cultural importance of tuna in the Maldives parallels that of rice in continental South Asia or corn in the Americas — it is the irreplaceable food around which the entire culinary system is organised, and the fish that defines Maldivian identity. The government of the Maldives has protected its tuna fishing rights and export industry as a fundamental national resource.

title: Coconut
full_text: Coconut (Cocos nucifera) is the second most important ingredient in Maldivian cuisine after tuna and, together with fish, defines the foundational flavour of Dhivehi cooking. The coconut palm grows abundantly throughout the low-lying coral atolls of the Maldives, and the use of coconut in almost every aspect of daily cooking reflects the island nation's dependence on what the local environment provides. Fresh coconut is grated using the hunigondi — a traditional Maldivian implement consisting of a low wooden seat with a serrated steel blade at the end, which women use to grate coconut by pushing halved coconuts against the blade — and the grated flesh is used directly in preparations such as mas huni and various snacks. Coconut milk (kaashi kiru), produced by soaking grated coconut in water and squeezing out the liquid, is the essential base for Maldivian fish curries, providing the creamy richness and mild sweetness that characterises the cuisine. Coconut oil, extracted by further processing, is used as the standard frying medium in Maldivian deep-frying, giving fried foods a subtle coconut flavour. Rihaakuru (the concentrated tuna paste) is sometimes enriched with coconut. The combination of tuna and coconut in multiple dishes — mas huni being the most iconic — represents the essential flavour pairing of Maldivian cuisine, contrasting the intense savouriness of the fish with the milky sweetness of the coconut.

title: Rice
full_text: Rice is the primary starch of the Maldivian daily meal and the most important imported food ingredient in the country, as the Maldives' limited, low-lying coral island geography provides insufficient arable land for large-scale grain cultivation. Despite being entirely imported, rice has become so central to Maldivian eating that it is considered a dietary necessity rather than a luxury, and food security planning in the Maldives relies heavily on maintaining adequate rice stockpiles. Rice is consumed boiled as plain white rice (bhat), served alongside garudhiya (tuna broth), fish curry (mas riha), and other accompaniments. Coconut rice — rice cooked with coconut milk and sometimes curry leaves — is a popular and distinctly Maldivian variation. Rice flour is used for making traditional snacks and sweets, including kulhi boakibaa (savoury tuna and rice cakes), boakiba (sweet rice cakes for celebrations), and handulu bondibai (sweet sticky rice dessert). Before rice imports became affordable and reliable, taro (ala), sweet potato (kattala), cassava (dandialuvi), and especially breadfruit (bambukeyo) served as the primary starchy foods of the Maldivian diet. These tubers and fruits remain important in traditional cooking and as backup staples. The shift to rice as the central staple over the twentieth century reflects the Maldives' increasing integration into regional trade and the global rice market, while local starchy alternatives have become secondary or ceremonial foods.

title: Breadfruit
full_text: Breadfruit (Artocarpus altilis, called bambukeyo in Dhivehi) is one of the most historically important local food plants of the Maldives and the most significant starchy staple produced on the islands themselves, predating the widespread adoption of imported rice as the primary carbohydrate. Breadfruit trees grow prolifically throughout the Maldivian atolls, requiring minimal cultivation and providing large, starchy fruits that can be cooked in multiple ways. The fruit's flesh, when unripe and starchy, can be boiled, roasted, fried, or fermented, functioning as a carbohydrate substitute for rice or bread. In traditional Maldivian cuisine, breadfruit was boiled and eaten plain, or cooked in curries and stews alongside fish and coconut milk. Bambukeyo harisa is a traditional Maldivian breadfruit curry made with tuna, coconut, and spices. Breadfruit is also used in festive and ceremonial preparations: handulu bondibai, a sweet pudding made for celebrations, sometimes incorporates breadfruit. The seeds of mature breadfruit are also edible when boiled or roasted, providing a nutritious snack. Breadfruit's role as a famine food and emergency staple was historically critical for isolated island communities that might face disruptions to fish supplies or trade. Today, while rice has replaced breadfruit as the primary everyday starch, breadfruit remains an important supplementary food, a traditional ingredient in preserved and processed forms, and a culturally valued local product that connects Maldivians to their pre-trade-route island foodways.

title: Smoking / drying fish
full_text: Smoking and drying tuna is the most important traditional food preservation technique of the Maldives and the process by which the country's most significant culinary export — Maldives fish (valhomas) — is produced. Because the Maldives historically had no refrigeration and island communities were separated by sea, preserving the abundant tuna catch for long-term storage, inter-island trade, and export was a critical practical and economic activity. The process for making Maldives fish involves several stages: fresh skipjack tuna is cleaned, filleted into large pieces, briefly boiled in brine until partially cooked, transferred to smoking racks over smouldering coconut husks or wood fires where it is hot-smoked for several hours, and then sun-dried for days to weeks until the flesh is completely desiccated and rock-hard. The resulting product is extremely dense, dark brown to black in colour, with an intensely concentrated fishy-umami flavour and an almost indefinite shelf life when kept dry. This cured tuna is called valhomas in Dhivehi and is known internationally as "Maldives fish" — it is exported to Sri Lanka (where it is called Maldive fish and used in almost every rice and curry dish), to South Indian coastal states (particularly Kerala and Tamil Nadu), and to diaspora communities globally. The rihaakuru — a thick, dark paste made by boiling fresh tuna for many hours until the liquid completely evaporates into a concentrated residue — is a related preservation technique that produces a different but equally flavour-intense product used as a condiment and sauce base. These preservation techniques reflect centuries of maritime trading culture in the Indian Ocean.

title: Nepalese cuisine (corpus planning note)
full_text: Nepalese cuisine is included in this South Asian corpus because the South Asian cuisine reference page lists Nepal among the cuisines within scope. This corpus should cover the following regional/state or subregional traditions where relevant: Newa / Himalayan / Maithil / Tibetan-influenced. Priority dish pages to collect from allowed sources: Dal bhat tarkari, Momo, Sel roti, Gundruk, Yomari. Priority ingredient pages to collect: Rice, Lentils, Buffalo meat, Timur pepper. Priority technique pages to collect: Fermentation, Steaming dumplings. Recommended overview/regional pages from allowed resources: Nepalese cuisine, Newa cuisine.
