title: Nepalese cuisine
full_text: Nepalese cuisine encompasses the diverse food traditions of Nepal, a landlocked Himalayan country situated between India and China, and reflects the country's extraordinary geographical and cultural range — from the tropical Terai plains to the high-altitude Himalayan ridges. The cuisine draws on both Indian (particularly North Indian) and Tibetan culinary influences, blending them with indigenous traditions of Nepal's many ethnic groups including the Newars, Thakalis, Sherpas, Gurungs, Rais, Limbus, and Madhesi communities. The national dish and near-universal daily meal is dal bhat tarkari — lentil soup, steamed rice (or another grain in higher altitudes), and a spiced vegetable stew — eaten twice daily by most Nepalis, often supplemented with pickles (achar), yoghurt, and meat or fish dishes. Regional variation is significant: in the Terai (southern plains), rice, wheat, and lentils dominate, with strong similarity to North Indian cuisine; in the Middle Hills, the same trinity appears but with millet, maize, and buckwheat supplements and a wider use of fermented preparations; in the Himalayas, buckwheat, barley, potato, and yak products take precedence. Common spices include cumin, coriander, turmeric, fenugreek, mustard seeds, ginger, garlic, and the uniquely Nepali timur (Nepalese Sichuan pepper, Zanthoxylum armatum) and jimbu (a dried Himalayan herb). Mustard oil is the standard cooking fat. Nepal is also home to a vibrant dumpling culture centred on the beloved momo, which has spread globally as perhaps the most recognisable symbol of Nepali cuisine.

title: Newa cuisine
full_text: Newa cuisine (also called Newari cuisine) is the culinary tradition of the Newar people, the indigenous inhabitants of the Kathmandu Valley, and is considered one of the oldest and most elaborate food cultures in South Asia. It developed over centuries in the exceptionally fertile Kathmandu Valley, where the availability of diverse produce, wealthy households, and a culture of festival feasting supported a far more complex and varied culinary tradition than those of most other Nepali communities. Food is deeply embedded in Newar religious and cultural life: every festival, life-cycle ceremony, and community gathering has specific associated dishes with ritual significance. The most iconic Newari food experience is the samay baji platter — a ceremonial set of dishes served at feasts including flattened beaten rice (baji/chiura), smoked and marinated buffalo meat (haku chhoyla), fried soybeans, spiced potato salad, roasted black soybeans (bhatmas), and other items, traditionally accompanied by the home-brewed local spirits aila and thwon. Buffalo meat is the centrepiece of Newari non-vegetarian cooking; the community uses virtually every part of the animal, including intestines, bone marrow, liver, and brain, each appearing in distinct traditional preparations. Other important Newari dishes include bara (crispy fried black lentil cakes, eaten plain or topped with egg or meat), chatamari (savoury rice flour crepe, sometimes called "Newari pizza"), yomari (sweet steamed rice flour dumplings filled with molasses and sesame), and kachila (raw spiced buffalo meat). Timur (Sichuan pepper) is a defining flavouring in Newari cuisine.

title: Dal bhat tarkari
full_text: Dal bhat tarkari (Nepali: दाल भात तरकारी) is the national dish and the foundational daily meal of Nepal, eaten twice a day by the vast majority of the Nepali population regardless of region, caste, or economic level. The name combines three components: dal (a spiced lentil or legume soup), bhat (steamed grain, usually rice, but millet, maize, or buckwheat in higher altitudes), and tarkari (a vegetable or meat curry). These three elements are served together on a large plate (thali) or brass platter, often accompanied by pickles (achar), yoghurt or buttermilk, and sometimes meat or fish preparations. The dal is most commonly made from black lentils, green mung beans, or red lentils, tempered with cumin, mustard seeds, turmeric, ginger, garlic, and asafoetida. The tarkari varies by season and region — seasonal vegetables such as potato, cauliflower, spinach, bamboo shoots, and ferns are common. In Nepal, the full dal bhat meal is considered not merely food but a complete nutritional framework and a symbol of hospitality: it is customary to offer second helpings without being asked, and a host's generosity is measured by how freely they refill a guest's plate. Among trekkers and mountaineers, "dal bhat power" has become a local catchphrase for the meal's sustaining qualities on long Himalayan expeditions. The Thakali people of the Thak-Khola Valley are particularly celebrated for their refined, flavourful version of dal bhat, served in inns (bhattis) with an expanded set of side dishes including gundruk (fermented greens) and a variety of achars.

title: Momo
full_text: Momo is a type of steamed dumpling that has become the most popular and most widely recognised dish of Nepal, deeply embedded in daily life as both a beloved street food and a home meal. Momo consists of a thin, round wrapper of refined wheat flour dough enclosing a filling of minced meat or vegetables, seasoned with garlic, ginger, onion, cumin, coriander, and sometimes timur (Nepalese Sichuan pepper). The dumplings are sealed into a distinctive pleated shape and then cooked by steaming over a broth or plain water in a tiered metal steamer (mucktoo). They are typically served with a tomato-based dipping sauce (achar) made from roasted tomatoes, garlic, sesame seeds, green chillies, and sometimes timur, which gives the sauce a slight numbing quality. Variants include kothey momo (pan-fried), jhol momo (served in a spiced broth soup), fried momo (deep-fried after steaming), and sadeko momo (steamed then tossed with spiced dressing). Originally, momos were filled primarily with minced buffalo meat — a tradition still associated with the Newar community of the Kathmandu Valley — but chicken, goat, pork, and a wide range of vegetarian fillings are now equally common. The precise origin of momo in Nepal is debated: one account credits Newari traders who adopted the dumpling during trade journeys to Lhasa, Tibet; another credits Tibetan settlers who migrated into Nepal. In any case, the dish has been adapted and claimed as distinctly Nepali, and has since spread to neighbouring Sikkim, Darjeeling, Bhutan, and far beyond.

title: Sel roti
full_text: Sel roti is a traditional Nepali ring-shaped fried bread made from a batter of ground rice flour, sugar, and water, deep-fried in oil until crisp and golden on the outside while remaining soft and slightly chewy on the inside. It has a distinctive hollow, bagel-like ring shape produced by pouring the runny batter in a circle directly into hot oil. The texture is unique — crispier than a doughnut but lighter than a Western ring bread — and the flavour is mildly sweet with a faint hint of cardamom or banana (which may be added to the batter). Sel roti is primarily a festival and ceremonial bread in Nepal, prepared and consumed in large quantities during major Hindu festivals including Dashain, Tihar (Diwali), and during weddings and the Nepali New Year. It is especially associated with the festivals of the Magar, Gurung, Rai, and Limbu communities of the eastern hills, but is now made and enjoyed across Nepal. The name "sel" is believed to refer to the ring or circle shape. The preparation requires skill: the batter consistency must be correct to produce the even ring shape and the characteristic crisp exterior; the frying temperature must be carefully managed. Sel roti is often eaten as a snack or breakfast with yoghurt, pickle, or potato curry. In diaspora communities and modern Kathmandu, sel roti is also sold commercially at sweet shops, though home-made versions for festivals remain the most culturally valued form.

title: Gundruk
full_text: Gundruk is a fermented leafy green vegetable preparation that is one of the most important and distinctive foods of Nepal, particularly among hill communities, and has been described as Nepal's national fermented food. It is made from the outer leaves of mustard greens, radish leaves, cauliflower leaves, or other leafy greens that would otherwise be discarded — typically the parts of the plant not eaten fresh. The leaves are first wilted in the sun for a day or two, then tightly packed into clay or metal containers, lightly moistened with water, sealed, and left to ferment at room temperature for one to two weeks. During fermentation, lactic acid bacteria naturally present on the leaves break down the plant material, producing a sour, strongly flavoured, slightly pungent condiment that retains its nutritional value and keeps for many months when dried. The fermented leaves are then removed and sun-dried for storage. Gundruk is used in multiple ways: as a sour, pickled condiment (gundruk ko achar) served alongside dal bhat; as an ingredient in soups and vegetable curries; and sometimes sold as a dry ingredient. Its flavour is intensely sour, earthy, and pungent — acquired but deeply beloved by those who grew up with it. Gundruk is considered an important source of nutrients, particularly during winter when fresh vegetables were historically unavailable in the hills. It provides significant amounts of iron, calcium, and beneficial bacteria. Ethnically, gundruk is associated particularly with Gurung, Rai, Limbu, and Magar communities.

title: Yomari
full_text: Yomari (also spelled yamari) is a traditional sweet steamed dumpling from the Newar community of the Kathmandu Valley and one of the most culturally significant foods in Newari culinary tradition. The dumpling is made from a pliable dough of rice flour, which is shaped into a distinctive elongated teardrop or fish-shaped form with a pointed top, representing a grain of rice or a fig in Newar symbolism. The filling is a sweet mixture of chaku (traditional Newari jaggery or molasses) combined with sesame seeds or grated coconut, which melts during steaming into a dark, fragrant, sticky sweet filling enclosed in the soft rice flour shell. Yomari has a particularly close association with the festival of Yomari Punhi (also called Yomari Purnima), celebrated on the full moon day of the month of Thinlaa in the Nepal Sambat calendar (usually falling in November or December), which marks the end of the rice harvest. Families gather to make yomari in communal settings, and the dumplings are offered to household deities and to neighbours as gifts of goodwill to celebrate the harvest. The shape of the dumpling is said to represent a grain of rice, honouring the crop that sustains life. Sweet filled versions with chaku and sesame are the most traditional, but savoury versions and modern fillings including chocolate and khoya (condensed milk solids) have also appeared. Yomari is a reminder that food in Newari culture is inseparable from religious ritual, seasonal cycles, and community solidarity.

title: Rice
full_text: Rice (Nepali: bhāt or chāmal) is the primary staple grain of Nepal and the central element of the national meal dal bhat tarkari. It is cultivated throughout the country's lower and middle altitude regions, particularly in the fertile Terai plains bordering India, in the Kathmandu Valley, and in river valleys of the Middle Hills. Several varieties are grown and consumed, including common long-grain white rice (typically marketed as Masino in Nepal) and shorter-grain aromatic varieties such as Taichung and local hill rices. Among Newari communities, fragrant short-grain varieties such as Gobindobhog, Karpurkanti, and Kalijira are prized for ceremonial preparations including kheer (rice pudding), polao (pilaf), and festival dishes. At high altitudes above approximately 2,500 metres where paddy cultivation is impossible, rice is imported from the lowlands and supplemented or replaced by millet, buckwheat, maize, and barley as the primary starch. In the cuisine of the Terai and Middle Hills, rice is eaten twice daily, typically boiled and served alongside dal and tarkari. Beaten and flattened rice (chiura or baji) is also widely consumed — it is a staple of Newari ceremonial cuisine, eaten at room temperature with various accompaniments, and commonly consumed as a snack or portable meal. Puffed rice (bhuteko makai or khaja) and parched rice are additional important rice-based snack forms consumed across Nepal. Rice cultivation and the harvest festival of Nabonno hold deep cultural and spiritual significance in Nepali society.

title: Lentils
full_text: Lentils and other dried pulses (collectively called dal in Nepali) are the most important protein source in Nepali cuisine and the second essential component of the national meal dal bhat tarkari. Nepal grows and consumes a wide variety of pulses including black lentils (maas ko dal), yellow lentils (pahelo dal), green mung beans (mung dal), red lentils (musur dal), chickpeas (chana), black-eyed peas (bodi), pigeon peas (rahar dal), and kidney beans (rajma). Among these, black lentils (urad dal) are considered the most prestigious and flavourful; a dal made from whole black lentils with a rich, slightly earthy taste is a mark of a good Nepali kitchen. Yellow split lentils produce a lighter, milder soup that is more commonly eaten daily. Lentil soup is prepared by simmering the dal with turmeric, salt, and water until soft, then finishing with a tempering (tarkā) of oil or ghee bloomed with cumin seeds, garlic, ginger, dried red chillies, and asafoetida. The range of flavours produced by different dal varieties and different regional seasonings is enormous. In addition to soup, lentils are used to make bara (black lentil cakes, a Newari staple), papadums, and a wide range of pickles and fermented preparations. Kwati — a soup of nine types of sprouted beans — is a traditional Newari dish eaten at the festival of Janai Purnima and considered nutritionally significant. Lentils represent an irreplaceable source of plant protein in Nepal, where most households eat little or no meat.

title: Buffalo meat
full_text: Water buffalo (Nepali: rāgo/maisi) meat, known locally as buff, is the most important non-vegetarian ingredient in Nepali cuisine, particularly within the Newar community of the Kathmandu Valley and other janajati (indigenous nationality) communities throughout Nepal. Its centrality in Newar cooking is especially notable: virtually every edible part of the buffalo — muscle meat, liver, lung, intestines, bone marrow, blood, and brain — is used in distinct traditional preparations, reflecting a philosophy of whole-animal utilisation and respect for the sacrifice. Buffalo is the meat of choice in Newari cuisine because cows are considered sacred by Hindus (Nepal was a Hindu kingdom for centuries) and beef is therefore off-limits for most higher-caste Hindus, while buffalo — which are considered separate from the cow — is widely accepted. Iconic Newari buffalo meat dishes include haku chhoyla (smoked, diced, and marinated buffalo meat served cold with mustard oil, ginger, garlic, and timur), kachila (raw, finely minced and spiced buffalo meat), sapu mhicha (buffalo tripe stuffed with bone marrow), and choyla (boiled or grilled marinated buffalo). Buffalo meat is also the original filling for momo dumplings, before chicken and other meats became common alternatives. In non-Newar communities, goat (khasi ko masu) and chicken are the more common meat choices, with buffalo, pork, yak, and lamb consumed regionally.

title: Timur pepper
full_text: Timur (Zanthoxylum armatum), also called Nepalese Sichuan pepper or Nepalese pepper, is a unique spice native to the Himalayas and one of the most important and distinctive flavour elements of Nepali and Newari cooking. It is the dried husk of the berry of the prickly ash tree, closely related to Sichuan pepper (Zanthoxylum simulans) used in Chinese cooking, but with a distinct flavour profile: simultaneously pungent, intensely citrusy (reminiscent of lime zest), floral, and — most characteristically — numbing. The numbing quality (like Sichuan pepper) comes from compounds called hydroxy-alpha-sanshool, which cause a tingling, mild anaesthetic sensation on the tongue and lips. Timur is used throughout Nepal in many contexts: ground with dried red chillies, salt, and local herbs to make timur ko chhop (a dry spice mixture for dipping dried meat and potatoes in highland Thakali cuisine), as a key component in momo dipping sauce (achar) where it provides the characteristic numbing tingle, in Newari pickles and condiments (particularly sukuti ko achar, made with dried buffalo meat), and in various meat marinades. In Newari cuisine specifically, timur appears in many of the most important preparations and is considered essential to authentic flavour. The spice is harvested from wild and semi-cultivated prickly ash trees in the Middle Hills and sold at markets throughout Nepal. Outside South Asia, timur is increasingly available in specialty spice shops internationally as interest in Himalayan cuisine grows.

title: Fermentation
full_text: Fermentation is a deeply embedded food preservation and flavour development technique in Nepali cuisine, particularly important in the Middle Hills and Himalayan communities where fresh vegetables are seasonally unavailable, and where the cold, dry climate creates ideal conditions for certain fermentation processes. The most important Nepali fermented food is gundruk — tightly packed, slightly wilted mustard or radish leaves left to lacto-ferment for one to two weeks, then sun-dried for long-term storage. Gundruk's sour, intense flavour is an irreplaceable element of hill cuisine, used in soups, curries, and pickles. Related to gundruk is sinki, a similar fermented preparation made from radish or turnip taproot (rather than leaves), which is dried and used as a sour-tangy ingredient in soups and stews. Fermented soybean preparations are important among Rai and Limbu communities of eastern Nepal: kinema (fermented whole soybeans with a pungent smell similar to natto) is made by fermenting cooked soybeans wrapped in leaves or stored in warm conditions, and is used as a protein-rich condiment in stews and soups. The Newars brew their own traditional fermented alcoholic beverages including aila (distilled spirit made from rice, millet, or other grains) and thwon (home-brewed grain beer), which play ritual roles in Newari festivals and ceremonies. Traditional fermented pickles (achar) made from mustard greens, radish, bamboo shoots, and various fruits are ubiquitous throughout Nepal's cuisine, providing sour, pungent counterpoints to the mild staple foods.

title: Steaming dumplings
full_text: Steaming is the essential cooking technique for momo — Nepal's most beloved food — and the method around which a distinctive Himalayan dumpling-making culture has developed. The technique involves placing raw-filled dumplings in a tiered metal steamer (mucktoo) set above simmering water or broth, covering tightly, and cooking by the trapped steam for ten to fifteen minutes until the wrapper becomes translucent and silky and the filling is fully cooked through. The steam cooks gently without drying or toughening the dough, producing the characteristically smooth, tender, slightly yielding texture that distinguishes good momos from inferior versions. The broth beneath the steamer may be plain water or may be a simple stock of bones or vegetables; some cooks believe steaming over broth rather than plain water imparts additional flavour to the dumplings. Beyond momo, steaming is used for several other important preparations in Nepali cuisine: yomari (the sweet Newari rice flour dumplings filled with chaku and sesame) are steamed in bamboo steamers for ten to fifteen minutes; dhokla-style snacks and various rice cakes are also steamed. The bamboo steamer has deep cultural roots in Himalayan cooking, used by Tibetan, Bhutanese, Nepali, and Sikkimese communities alike for generations before modern metal mucktoo became standard. The filling and folding of momos — producing the characteristic pleated or crescent shapes — is itself a significant cultural skill, taught within families and often performed communally as a social activity before festivals and celebrations.

title: Pakistani cuisine (corpus planning note)
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Pakistani cuisine is included in this South Asian corpus because the South Asian cuisine reference page lists Pakistan among the cuisines within scope. This corpus should cover the following regional/state or subregional traditions where relevant: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi. Priority dish pages to collect from allowed sources: Nihari, Haleem, Karahi, Biryani, Chapli kebab, Saag, Sajji. Priority ingredient pages to collect: Wheat, Rice, Yogurt, Ghee, Whole spices. title: Pakistani cuisine
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Pakistani cuisine (Urdu: پاکستانی پکوان) is a rich and diverse culinary tradition that blends the food cultures of South Asia, Central Asia, Persia, and the Arab world, shaped by the historical legacies of the Indus Valley Civilisation, the Mughal Empire, and the 1947 partition of the Indian subcontinent. It inherits closely from the culinary traditions of North India, particularly Punjab, while maintaining distinct provincial identities shaped by climate, geography, and cultural heritage. Pakistani cuisine is strictly halal — pork is entirely absent, and meat must be slaughtered according to Islamic dietary law. The primary proteins are beef, mutton (goat and lamb), chicken, and seafood in coastal areas. Wheat and rice are the foundational staple grains; wheat is consumed mainly as flatbreads (roti, naan, paratha, chapati), while rice appears in dishes such as biryani and pulao. Lentils (dal) and chickpeas are important everyday protein sources. The spice palette is rich and aromatic — cumin, coriander, turmeric, black pepper, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, chillies, ginger, garlic, and garam masala — though the emphasis is more on aromatic warmth than fiery heat in many regions. Yoghurt (dahi) is used extensively in marinades, as a cooking base, and as a cooling accompaniment. Ghee is the traditional cooking fat, and tandoor-baked breads are central to the culinary identity. Iconic national dishes include nihari (slow-cooked meat stew), haleem (wheat, lentil, and meat porridge), karahi (wok-cooked spiced meat curry), biryani, and seekh kebabs. Regional sub-cuisines differ significantly between Punjab, Sindh, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, and Balochistan.

title: Balochi cuisine
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Balochi cuisine is the traditional food culture of the Baloch people of Balochistan, Pakistan's largest but most sparsely populated province, and reflects the province's arid climate, vast semi-desert landscape, and centuries of pastoral nomadism. In contrast to the spice-forward cuisines of Punjab and Sindh, Balochi cooking is characterised by simplicity, restraint in spicing, and an emphasis on allowing the quality of the primary ingredient — usually lamb or goat — to speak for itself. Salt, green papaya paste (as a meat tenderiser), and basic aromatics are the typical seasonings, rather than elaborate spice blends. The most iconic dish of Balochi cuisine is sajji — whole lamb or chicken that has been rubbed with salt, sometimes stuffed with rice, skewered on a long spit, and slow-roasted over a wood fire for many hours until the exterior is crisp and golden and the interior extremely tender and moist. Dampukht is another celebrated dish in which meat (usually lamb) is sealed inside a clay pot or covered pot and cooked entirely in its own rendered fat and natural moisture, requiring no additional water or oil — a technique that produces an extraordinarily rich, concentrated flavour. Lamb and goat are the most important proteins, reflecting the region's sheep- and goat-herding pastoral economy. Other staples include dates, dried fruits, and bread varieties such as kaak (a very hard, twice-baked bread) which is traditionally paired with sajji. Tea is consumed throughout the day. The cuisine has been influenced by Persian and Afghan cooking traditions due to Balochistan's borders with Iran and Afghanistan.

title: Pashtun cuisine
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Pashtun cuisine is the food tradition of the Pashtun (Pukhtun) people of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KPK) and the tribal belt of northwestern Pakistan, and reflects the rugged mountainous terrain, Central Asian and Afghan cultural connections, and the Pashtun tradition of lavish hospitality (melmastia). The cuisine is notably more restrained in spicing than Pakistani Punjab or Sindh — flavour comes from the quality of meat and simple aromatics rather than complex masala blends. Salt, fresh tomatoes, ginger, and sometimes just animal fat are the defining seasonings of the most traditional Pashtun dishes. Lamb, beef, and chicken are the primary proteins. The region is particularly famous for its kebabs: chapli kebab (a large, flat, coarsely-spiced minced meat patty with pomegranate seeds, now famous across Pakistan), seekh kebab (spiced minced meat moulded on skewers and grilled), and tika (marinated grilled meat pieces). Karahi — meat cooked rapidly in a wok (karahi) with tomatoes, green chillies, and minimal spicing — is a signature dish of the region, particularly in the Namak Mandi area of Peshawar, where shinwari karahi (made with just tomatoes, green chillies, salt, and rendered fat) is iconic. Kabuli palaw (rice cooked with raisins, carrots, and lamb) reflects Afghan influence. Rice is more central to Pashtun cooking than in Punjab. Bread traditions include the elongated Kandahari naan and various tandoor-baked varieties. Qehwa (green tea flavoured with cardamom) is the preferred beverage, offered to every guest as a gesture of hospitality.

title: Punjabi cuisine
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Punjabi cuisine from Pakistan is the food tradition of the Punjab province — Pakistan's most populous and agriculturally richest region, centred on Lahore — and is characterised by robust, full-bodied flavours, generous use of dairy products (ghee, butter, yoghurt, lassi), and a strong emphasis on wheat-based foods and hearty meat dishes. Pakistani Punjabi cuisine shares deep roots with the cuisine of Indian Punjab but has developed distinctive emphases reflecting the region's Muslim majority, particularly the prominence of beef and the absence of pork. Wheat is the absolute staple: roti, naan, paratha, puri, and makki di roti (corn flatbread) are eaten daily. The iconic winter dish is sarson da saag (mustard greens slow-cooked in ghee) served with makki di roti and a generous pat of white butter — a preparation deeply embedded in Punjabi rural culture. Dairy products including pure desi ghee, fresh butter, cream, yoghurt, and lassi (sweet or salted yoghurt drink) are consumed in large quantities. The Lahori culinary tradition is particularly noted for its street food culture, including Lahori fish fry, murgh cholay (chicken and chickpea curry), naan-chanay, and various karahi preparations. Slow-cooked meat dishes — nihari, haleem, paya (trotters stew) — have strong roots in Lahore's old city food culture. Biryani is celebrated throughout Punjab. The cuisine is more spice-forward and richer in dairy than the cuisines of KPK or Balochistan.

title: Sindhi cuisine
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Sindhi cuisine is the food tradition of the Sindh province, Pakistan's southernmost province, a region defined by the Indus River delta, the Arabian Sea coastline, and the Thar Desert — geographical diversity that is directly reflected in the cuisine's range from seafood and rice dishes in the south to heartier, desert-adapted preparations in the north and east. Sindhi cuisine is generally considered the spiciest and most aromatic of Pakistan's regional cuisines, noted for its bold use of dried red chillies, tomatoes, tamarind, and a characteristic sour-tangy flavour profile. Rice is the primary staple grain, consumed more than wheat in comparison to Punjab. Sindhi biryani is a nationally beloved rice dish distinguished by its inclusion of potatoes and dried plums (aloo bukhara), giving it a tangy, slightly sweet character unique among Pakistani biryanis. Fish and seafood are central to coastal Sindhi cooking: palla fish (Indus River hilsa), pomfret, prawns, and crab are popular preparations. Sindhi curry (a tamarind-based vegetable and meat gravy without onion) is a distinctive everyday dish. The province's Hindu minority community has contributed important vegetarian traditions, including dishes using seasonal vegetables, lentils, and chillies. Unique spice preparations including the Sindhi spice blend and specific varieties of dried red chilli lend the cuisine its characteristic intensity. Sai bhaji (a spinach and lentil preparation) and Sindhi kadhi (yoghurt and gram flour gravy) are important regional preparations.

title: Nihari
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Nihari (Urdu: نہاری) is a slow-cooked meat stew that originated in the Mughal court tradition of Delhi and Lucknow and has become one of the most iconic dishes of Pakistani cuisine, particularly in Karachi and Lahore. The word derives from the Arabic nahar (نهار), meaning "morning" — nihari was originally eaten as a dawn breakfast by Mughal nawabs and workers following the Fajr prayer, having cooked slowly overnight. It was brought to Pakistan in large numbers following the 1947 partition, when Urdu-speaking Muslim migrants from Delhi and northern India settled in Karachi and established restaurants serving the dish; it subsequently spread across the country. Nihari is made from bone-in beef shank (most commonly in Pakistan, though lamb and goat versions exist), slow-cooked for four to eight hours with bone marrow, aromatic whole spices (cinnamon, cloves, black cardamom, star anise, fennel seeds, mace, nutmeg, long pepper, cumin), red chilli, and ginger in a thin broth. The stew is thickened with a paste of atta (whole wheat flour) added toward the end of cooking, which gives the gravy a silky, slightly coarse body. The marrow extracted from the bones and fat that rises to the surface (rogan) are considered the most prized elements. Nihari is served topped with julienned fresh ginger, green chillies, fried onions, fresh coriander, and a squeeze of lime, and eaten with naan, khamiri roti, or sheermal. It is associated with cold winter mornings, festive celebrations, and the street food culture of old city areas.

title: Haleem
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Haleem (also spelled halim) is one of the most beloved and widely consumed dishes of Pakistan, a thick, slow-cooked porridge-like stew made from a combination of cracked wheat, barley, split lentils (usually chana dal, masoor, and moong), and meat (typically beef or mutton, though chicken versions are common). The dish is cooked for many hours — traditionally overnight — until all the ingredients break down and blend into a smooth, fibrous, intensely flavoured mass, with threads of tender meat running through the thick grain-and-lentil base. The origin of haleem traces to the Arab dish harees (a wheat and meat porridge), which spread through the Muslim world during the early Islamic caliphates and reached South Asia during the Delhi Sultanate period. In the Mughal Empire's kitchens it was refined with South Asian spices including garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli, evolving into the haleem now known across Pakistan and South Asia. Haleem is seasoned with a complex spice blend, then garnished before serving with crispy fried onions, julienned fresh ginger, green chillies, fresh coriander, a squeeze of lemon, and a drizzle of ghee. It is particularly associated with Muharram (the Islamic month of mourning), when it is prepared communally and distributed as sadaqah (charitable food). It is also a Ramadan staple, a wedding banquet item, and a year-round street food sold in bazaars throughout Pakistan. Karachi haleem is a particularly celebrated regional variant.

title: Karahi
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Karahi (also spelled kadai or kadahi, Urdu: کڑاہی) is one of the most popular everyday dishes of Pakistani cuisine and takes its name directly from the cooking vessel in which it is prepared — a thick, deep, round-bottomed wok of cast iron or steel with two handles, similar to a Chinese wok but heavier. The dish is a rapid, high-heat curry of chicken (murgh karahi) or mutton/goat (gosht karahi) cooked directly in the karahi vessel with fresh tomatoes, ginger, garlic, green chillies, whole spices, red chilli powder, and a small amount of yoghurt or cream, producing a thick, flavourful, relatively dry sauce that clings to the meat. Unlike many South Asian curries, the authentic Pakistani karahi does not rely on a base of caramelised onions; in the most traditional Pashtun versions from the Namak Mandi area of Peshawar (shinwari karahi), it is prepared with only tomatoes, green chillies, salt, and rendered animal fat — a minimalist masterpiece of flavour. The karahi is cooked over very high heat and served still bubbling in the vessel. Chicken karahi is thought to have originated in the Punjab region (particularly Lahore), while gosht karahi has strong roots in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The dish is now ubiquitous at roadside dhabas, street stalls, and restaurants across Pakistan, served with fresh naan or roti. It is considered the quintessential Pakistani restaurant dish and is one of the most-ordered preparations at celebratory gatherings and weddings.

title: Biryani
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Pakistani biryani is a rich, aromatic rice dish made by layering partially cooked basmati rice with a spiced meat or vegetable marinade and finishing it by the dum cooking method (sealed pot over low heat), and represents one of the most important and celebratory foods in Pakistani cuisine. Pakistani biryani is generally considered bolder, more robustly spiced, and higher in oil content than many Indian biryanis, reflecting the country's preference for deeply aromatic, pungent flavours. There are several distinct regional varieties: Sindhi biryani is the most internationally recognised Pakistani style, made with a sharp spice blend, generous quantities of fried potato, dried plums (aloo bukhara) for tartness, and sometimes slit green chillies; Lahori biryani from Punjab emphasises rich fried onions, whole spices, and a deeply caramelised meat base; Karachi biryani is known for its rich, boldly-spiced profile and liberal use of red chilli and tomato. All Pakistani biryanis share the fundamental technique: meat is marinated in yoghurt and spices, partially pre-cooked, layered with parboiled basmati rice, sealed, and finished over low heat until the steam melds the flavours. Saffron or orange food colouring gives the upper layer its characteristic golden hue. Biryani is considered a celebration food — eaten at weddings, Eid gatherings, and communal feasts — as well as a widely available everyday street food. It is served with raita, cucumber salad, and sometimes a sour pickle.

title: Chapli kebab
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Chapli kebab (Pashto: چپلي کباب) is a flat, disc-shaped minced meat patty originating from Peshawar and the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region of Pakistan, and is considered a signature dish of Pashtun cuisine. The name derives from the Pashto word chaprikh, meaning "flat," referring to the distinctive wide, flat shape of the patty. It is also known as the Peshawari kebab. Chapli kebab is made from coarsely minced beef (or sometimes lamb), combined with a distinctive set of spices and flavourings that set it apart from other kebab traditions: coarsely ground coriander seeds, cumin, dried red chilli flakes, fresh or dried pomegranate seeds (anardana — a defining ingredient that adds a fruity tartness), finely chopped onions, green chillies, fresh tomatoes or tomato slices pressed into the patty, fresh coriander, and sometimes egg and wheat flour as binders. The meat is seasoned, shaped into large flat patties roughly the size of a human palm, and pan-fried or shallow-fried in a generous amount of ghee, tallow (animal fat), or oil until deep golden and slightly crisp on both sides. The result is juicy inside with a slightly crisp exterior and a complex, aromatic flavour profile with a fruity-sour note from the pomegranate. Chapli kebab is served with yoghurt raita, sliced raw onion rings, fresh coriander chutney, and warm naan or lachha paratha. It is a beloved street food and restaurant staple throughout Pakistan and Afghanistan.

title: Saag
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Saag (Punjabi: ਸਾਗ / Urdu: ساگ) is a traditional winter dish made from leafy green vegetables — most commonly sarson (mustard greens), though spinach, fenugreek leaves (methi), and bathua (pigweed) are often added or substituted — slow-cooked with aromatics until deeply reduced and intensely flavoured. In Pakistan, saag is most closely associated with Punjab, where sarson da saag (mustard greens saag) paired with makki di roti (corn flatbread) and fresh white butter is the quintessential rural winter meal. To prepare saag, washed mustard greens and spinach are boiled together until very soft, then blended or hand-mashed to a coarse, thick puree. The puree is then cooked slowly on a low flame in a large heavy pot, stirring frequently, for one to several hours — this prolonged cooking concentrates and mellows the slightly bitter, pungent flavour of the raw greens into a rich, sweet-savoury depth. A tempering (tadka) of ghee with finely sliced onions, ginger, garlic, and green chillies is added during or at the end of cooking. The finished saag is typically topped with a generous knob of pure white butter (makkhan) or desi ghee before serving. Saag is a deeply seasonal and culturally symbolic food across Pakistan and Indian Punjab, eaten communally during the cold months from November to March when fresh mustard greens are at their peak. In Pakistan, saag is also commonly cooked with meat (saag gosht) — lamb or beef simmered together with the greens to produce a rich, hearty one-pot meal.

title: Sajji
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Sajji (Balochi: سجی) is a traditional whole-roasted meat dish originating from Balochistan, Pakistan's largest province, and is considered the signature dish and a symbol of Baloch cultural identity and hospitality. It is made by rubbing a whole lamb or chicken with salt (and sometimes a marinade of green papaya paste, which tenderises the meat) and then skewering it on a long iron rod which is vertically planted in the ground over a smouldering wood fire, where the meat rotates slowly and roasts for several hours. The outer skin becomes deeply golden, caramelised, and slightly crisp, while the interior remains remarkably moist, tender, and juicy. In the most traditional Baloch style, sajji is seasoned with almost nothing beyond salt, allowing the natural flavour of the meat and the woodsmoke to define the dish — a philosophy of minimalism that contrasts sharply with the spice-heavy cooking traditions of Punjab. In more elaborate versions, the cavity of the whole animal may be stuffed with seasoned rice that absorbs the rendered fat and juices as the meat cooks, producing a rice-based accompaniment called sajji rice. The dish is typically served with kaak (a very hard, twice-baked Balochi bread), fresh lime wedges, and sometimes a simple green chutney. Chicken sajji — more affordable than lamb — has become popular across Pakistan far beyond Balochistan. Sajji is prepared for weddings, communal gatherings, and celebrations, and its preparation is considered an expression of Baloch generosity.

title: Wheat
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Wheat is the most important agricultural crop and staple food of Pakistan, central to the diet of the Pakistani population in a way that few crops are anywhere in the world. Per capita wheat consumption in Pakistan is among the highest globally, and approximately 70-80% of the daily caloric intake in an average Pakistani household derives from wheat, primarily consumed in the form of flatbreads. Nearly every meal in Pakistan features some form of wheat bread: roti (a thin, unleavened flatbread made on a tava), naan (leavened, slightly thicker, baked in a tandoor), paratha (layered, pan-fried flatbread), chapati, puri (deep-fried), and sheermal (a mildly sweet, saffron-tinged flatbread). Pakistan is one of the world's top wheat producers, with cultivation concentrated in Punjab and Sindh. The wheat flour used in Pakistani cooking is primarily atta — whole-wheat flour milled from Pakistani durum wheat varieties — which has a distinctly nutty flavour and high gluten content that makes it ideal for flatbreads. Atta is also used to thicken dishes such as nihari (where a slurry of atta is added to give the broth its characteristic silky texture) and haleem. Bread in Pakistan is not merely an accompaniment but a functional utensil — flatbreads are used to scoop up curries, dal, and vegetables, replacing the need for cutlery in traditional eating. The tandoor (clay oven) for baking naan is a fixture of neighbourhoods, street corners, and restaurant kitchens throughout the country, with fresh naan baked to order throughout the day.

title: Rice
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Rice is the second most important staple grain of Pakistan after wheat, with consumption strongest in Sindh, Balochistan, and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, and in urban centres where biryani and pulao are major foods. Pakistan is one of the world's leading producers and exporters of basmati rice — a long-grain aromatic variety with an exceptional fragrance, nutty flavour, and characteristic elongation when cooked. Pakistani Super Basmati and 1121 Basmati are among the most prized varieties grown primarily in the Punjab, and are exported globally as premium rice. Basmati is the rice of choice for Pakistan's most important rice dishes: biryani (layered rice with spiced meat, cooked by dum method), pulao (rice cooked in spiced broth with meat or vegetables), and pilaf preparations. Rice is also central to Sindhi cuisine, where it is consumed more frequently than wheat in many households. In KPK and Pashtun cooking, the Afghan-influenced Kabuli palaw (rice with raisins, fried carrots, and lamb) is a celebrated festive dish. In everyday Pakistani cooking, plain steamed basmati rice is eaten with dal, curries, and vegetable preparations. Tahiri (a vegetarian rice and vegetable preparation) and yakhni pulao (rice cooked in a bone broth) are important rice-based preparations. Rice flour is used in some traditional sweets and fried snack preparations. The export of Pakistani basmati rice is a significant component of the agricultural economy, particularly valued in the Middle East, Europe, and North American diaspora markets.

title: Yogurt
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Yoghurt (Urdu: دہی, dahi) is one of the most essential and versatile ingredients in Pakistani cuisine, used at virtually every stage of cooking and eating — as a marinade base for meats, as a souring and tenderising agent in curries, as a thickener and enricher of gravies, as a cooling accompaniment, and as the base for drinks. Pakistan has a strong dairy tradition, particularly in Punjab, where desi dahi (homemade full-fat cultured yoghurt made from the milk of desi cows or buffaloes) is highly valued for its thick, creamy texture and rich flavour. As a marinade base, yoghurt is indispensable for tikka and tandoori preparations: the lactic acid in yoghurt breaks down the surface proteins of meat, allowing spices to penetrate and the meat to cook more evenly while remaining moist. In karahi and curry preparations, yoghurt is added mid-cooking to enrich and slightly thicken the sauce and add a gentle tang. Raita — yoghurt mixed with cucumber, mint, cumin, and chilli — is the standard cooling accompaniment served alongside biryani, kebabs, and spicy curries. Lassi (a yoghurt-based drink, either sweet or salted) is particularly associated with Punjabi culture and is one of Pakistan's most popular traditional beverages, consumed widely in summer and at breakfast. Shami kebab binders include yoghurt. Dahi bharay (fried lentil dumplings served in chilled yoghurt with tamarind and chaat masala) is a classic street food and Ramadan iftar item.

title: Ghee
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Ghee (Urdu: گھی) — clarified butter made by simmering unsalted butter until the water evaporates and milk solids separate and are discarded — is the traditional cooking fat of Pakistan and occupies a place of central cultural, culinary, and nutritional significance in Pakistani food culture. In Pakistan, desi ghee (made from the milk of desi cows or water buffalo, sometimes clarified from cultured butter) is distinguished from commercial ghee and considered far superior in flavour, richness, and aroma. It has a deep golden colour, a powerful nutty, caramel-like fragrance, and a taste significantly richer than regular ghee. Ghee is used to make traditional flatbreads (parathas and naan are brushed with ghee after baking), to enrich slow-cooked preparations (nihari, haleem, and pulao are finished with a drizzle of ghee), to prepare the tarka (oil-bloomed spice tempering) that finishes dal and rice dishes, and as the primary cooking fat for karahi, kebabs, and curries in traditional cooking. Sarson da saag is considered incomplete without a generous knob of fresh butter or desi ghee. In Pakistani Punjabi cooking culture, serving food cooked generously in ghee is an expression of hospitality and abundance, and refusing ghee-enriched food can be considered impolite in traditional households. Beyond cooking, desi ghee holds Ayurvedic and folk medicinal value and is given to new mothers, children, and the elderly for its perceived nutritional and warming properties.

title: Whole spices
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Whole spices are the foundational flavour architecture of Pakistani cuisine and are used in ways that differ meaningfully from ground spice blends: they are typically added to hot oil or ghee at the very beginning of cooking (the bloom stage), releasing their essential oils in a controlled burst that infuses the entire dish with fragrance, or they are added whole to slow-cooked preparations to provide deep background warmth without harsh sharpness. The most important whole spices in Pakistani cooking are: cardamom (both green and black), used in biryani, pulao, haleem, nihari, and korma for floral warmth; cinnamon sticks, for sweet background depth in rice and meat dishes; cloves, for their pungent, slightly bitter aromatic intensity; black peppercorns, for clean heat; cumin seeds (whole), the near-universal first addition to the cooking oil in nearly every Pakistani curry; bay leaves; star anise (in nihari and biryani); dried red chillies; fenugreek seeds; and mace. In traditional Pakistani cooking, a spice blend called garam masala is assembled from these whole spices — roasted briefly and then ground — and added toward the end of cooking. However, whole spices are also used unground in many preparations to provide diffuse background fragrance rather than the sharp directness of ground powder. Whole spice shopping in Pakistani bazaars is a distinctive cultural experience; dedicated spice merchants (masala wallahs) sell these ingredients by weight from large open sacks, and many cooks grind their own spice blends at home for maximum freshness.

title: Slow cooking stews
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Slow cooking of meat stews is one of the most important and culturally embedded cooking traditions in Pakistani cuisine, producing the country's most celebrated comfort foods — nihari, haleem, paya (trotters stew), and dum gosht (slow pot-cooked meat). The technique involves placing tougher cuts of meat (particularly shanks, trotters, oxtail, and other collagen-rich pieces) in a heavy pot with water, whole spices, and aromatics, then cooking at the lowest possible heat for four to twelve hours, or even overnight. The long, slow cooking process allows collagen in the connective tissue and marrow to break down into gelatine, producing a deeply rich, silky, unctuous broth with an extraordinary depth of flavour that cannot be achieved by any faster method. The fat renders slowly and rises to the surface as rogan (clarified rendered fat with dissolved spice compounds), which is prized as a flavour indicator and served pooled over the finished stew. In nihari, wheat flour (atta) is added at the end to thicken the broth. In haleem, the grains and lentils are stirred vigorously during the final cooking stage to produce the characteristic smooth, fibrous porridge texture. Many Pakistani cooks still prepare these dishes overnight, leaving the pot on the lowest possible flame (or using a coal-heated brazier) and finishing it in the morning — the traditional preparation that gave nihari its name (from the Arabic for "morning"). Slow-cooked stews are associated with communal hospitality, religious observances (Muharram, Eid), and the old city food culture of Lahore and Karachi.

title: Karahi stir-cooking
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Karahi cooking (named after the vessel used — a deep, round-bottomed, cast-iron or steel wok) is one of the most distinctive and widely practised cooking techniques in Pakistani cuisine, involving the rapid, high-heat preparation of meat (usually bone-in chicken or mutton) in a tomato-based sauce cooked directly in the karahi vessel. The technique differs fundamentally from most South Asian curry-making: rather than building a long-cooked onion masala base, karahi cooking typically involves cooking the meat and aromatics together at high heat with fresh tomatoes, fresh ginger and garlic paste, green chillies, and either minimal spicing (in the Pashtun shinwari style) or a moderate spice blend (in the Lahori Punjabi style). The high heat caramelises and reduces the tomatoes rapidly into a thick, coating sauce, while the meat sears and develops flavour simultaneously. The finished karahi has a dry or semi-dry sauce that clings to the meat pieces rather than forming a thin gravy — the characteristic texture that defines the dish. The karahi is brought to the table still sizzling on a metal stand, and the dish is eaten directly from the vessel with fresh naan torn to scoop up the meat and sauce. In restaurant settings, the spectacle of the bubbling karahi arriving at the table is part of the eating experience. Variants include Lahori karahi (with cream and mild spicing), KPK-style namak mandi karahi (salt, tomato, green chilli, fat only), and butter karahi (enriched with butter and cream).

title: Barbecuing / grilling
region: Balochi, Chitrali, Kalash, Karachi, Lahori, Pashtun, Punjabi, Saraiki, Sindhi
full_text: Barbecuing and grilling — the cooking of marinated meat over direct heat from coals or an open wood fire — is one of the most celebrated and culturally central culinary traditions of Pakistan, associated with both everyday street food culture and festive celebration. Pakistani barbecue culture is particularly vibrant in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Punjab, where grilled meats are considered a mark of hospitality and the preparation and sharing of grilled food is a deeply social activity. The two primary barbecue formats are tandoor cooking (marinated flatbreads and meats cooked in a cylindrical clay oven at extremely high temperatures, producing distinctive char marks and a smoky, lightly caramelised flavour) and open-fire or grill cooking (meats cooked over smouldering charcoal or wood). Tandoor preparations include tandoori chicken, seekh kebab (minced meat moulded on skewers), and various naan varieties baked on the clay walls. Open-fire grilling is used for chapli kebab (flat minced meat patties from KPK), tika (marinated chunks of chicken, beef, or mutton grilled on skewers), and boti (boneless marinated meat cubes). Marinades almost always contain yoghurt as a tenderising and binding agent, along with spice blends of cumin, coriander, red chilli, garam masala, ginger-garlic paste, and lemon juice. Sajji — the Balochi tradition of whole-animal spit-roasting over open wood fire — represents a distinct and ancient variation of this technique. Pakistanis also barbecue communally at family gatherings using traditional sigris (coal braziers).

title: Sri Lankan cuisine
full_text: Sri Lankan cuisine is the culinary tradition of the island nation of Sri Lanka (formerly Ceylon), situated in the Indian Ocean off the southern tip of India. It is characterised by intense spicing, pervasive use of coconut in multiple forms, and a structural reliance on rice as the central staple, accompanied by a wide array of curries, sambols, and condiments. Sri Lankan cuisine is shaped by the island's geography as a historic spice-trading hub, and by successive waves of cultural contact — with South India, Portugal (1505), the Netherlands (1658), and Britain (1815) — each of which left distinct culinary imprints. Two major ethnolinguistic cooking traditions coexist: Sinhalese cuisine (the majority community) and Sri Lankan Tamil cuisine, particularly from the Jaffna region in the north, which shares close affinities with South Indian food. The cuisine is distinguished from nearby South Asian traditions by its use of distinctly Sri Lankan ingredients: Ceylon cinnamon (Cinnamomum verum, milder and sweeter than cassia), goraka (Garcinia cambogia, a souring agent with no direct equivalent), Maldive fish (sun-dried cured tuna used as a flavour base), pandan leaf (rampe), and kithul palm jaggery. Curry powders are typically toasted darker than Indian equivalents, giving Sri Lankan curries a deeper, more roasted character. Coconut milk, scraped fresh coconut, and coconut oil are near-universal cooking mediums. Rice and curry, hoppers (appam), string hoppers (idiyappam), kottu roti, kiribath, and pittu are signature preparations, alongside sambols such as pol sambol and lunu miris.

title: Kottu
full_text: Kottu roti (Tamil: கொத்து ரொட்டி; Sinhala: කොත්තු රොටි) is Sri Lanka's most iconic street food — a stir-fry of finely chopped godhamba roti (a thin wheat flatbread) combined on a hot iron griddle with vegetables, egg, curry gravy, and optionally meat, seafood, or cheese. The name comes from the Tamil word koththu, meaning "to chop," which describes the defining preparation technique: the cook uses two heavy iron blades to rhythmically chop and mix all the ingredients against a large flat metal griddle, producing the characteristic loud, metallic clanging sound that announces the presence of a kottu stall from streets away and has become one of the most evocative sensory signatures of Sri Lankan urban life. Kottu originated in the eastern province of Sri Lanka — likely Batticaloa — in the 1960s or 1970s as an economical way to use unsold day-old godhamba roti, chopping it up and stir-frying it with whatever vegetables and spices were available. The godhamba roti is cut into strips, added to a hot griddle where onions, leeks, carrots, green chillies, and egg are already sizzling, then seasoned with curry powder, black pepper, and a splash of dark curry gravy. Protein additions include chicken, beef, mutton, or fresh cheese (for a vegetarian version). The result is a savoury, spicy, slightly oily jumble of chewy roti pieces bound together with egg and gravy — considered the ultimate Sri Lankan comfort food and a popular late-night meal.

title: Hoppers
full_text: Hoppers (Sinhala: ආප්ප, appa; Tamil: appam) are bowl-shaped pancakes unique to Sri Lanka and parts of South India, made from a batter of fermented rice flour and coconut milk that is cooked in a small, round, wok-shaped pan called an appa thattiya. The batter is swirled around the hot, oiled pan so that it coats the sides in a thin, lacy layer while a thicker pool settles in the bowl-shaped base, creating a pancake with an extraordinarily crispy, golden lace-like outer rim and a soft, spongy, slightly tangy centre. The fermentation agent is traditionally palm toddy (fermented coconut palm sap), which gives the hoppers their characteristic slightly sour flavour; commercial yeast is widely used as a substitute. The most popular variant is the egg hopper, in which a raw egg is cracked into the centre as the hopper cooks, producing a fried egg set within the soft base. Milk hoppers have coconut milk pooled in the centre. Sweet hoppers (vandu appa or pani appa) are drizzled with treacle or jaggery. Hoppers are served with lunu miris (a fiery sambol of ground dried red chillies, red onions, Maldive fish, and lime), pol sambol (fresh coconut sambol), or a mild dhal curry. They are eaten primarily at breakfast and dinner, and their preparation requires a specific pan and considerable skill to swirl the batter correctly. Hoppers are considered one of the most emblematic dishes of Sri Lankan cuisine and a highlight of any Sri Lankan meal.

title: String hoppers
full_text: String hoppers (Sinhala: ඉඳිආප්ප, idiyappa; Tamil: idiyappam) are delicate, nest-shaped steamed noodles made from rice flour pressed through a special mould onto small circular wicker mats, then steamed. They are one of the most important everyday breakfast foods of Sri Lanka, particularly in the north, south, and Tamil-majority regions. To prepare string hoppers, a dough is made from fine rice flour mixed with hot water and a little oil and salt until it reaches a pliable consistency. The dough is loaded into a cylindrical string hopper press fitted with a fine-holed disc, and the cook squeezes the press in a circular motion over a small woven mat, creating a thin circular nest of fine, vermicelli-like strands. Many mats are stacked and steamed simultaneously in a tiered steamer for a few minutes until the noodle nests are cooked through but still delicate. The result is a light, airy, slightly sticky nest of soft white rice noodles that has a subtle, neutral flavour perfectly suited to absorbing the accompaniments served alongside it. String hoppers are typically served with pol sambol (fresh coconut, chilli, and Maldive fish sambol), kiri hodi (a mild, turmeric-tinged coconut milk gravy), or dhal curry. They are naturally gluten-free, light, and easily digestible, making them popular as a gentle morning food. String hoppers are distinct from bowl-shaped hoppers despite the shared name; the two are entirely different preparations that happen to share a coconut-milk and rice-flour base.

title: Kiribath
full_text: Kiribath (Sinhala: කිරිබත්, meaning "milk rice"; Tamil equivalent: paal soru) is a ceremonially significant Sri Lankan rice preparation in which short-grain or medium-grain rice is cooked in coconut milk — rather than water — until the grains absorb the liquid and become soft, creamy, and slightly sticky. The name directly describes the method: kiri (coconut milk) and bath (rice). The cooked kiribath is transferred to a flat plate or banana leaf and pressed into a smooth layer about 2 cm thick, left to cool and firm slightly, then cut into diamond or square shapes for serving. It is eaten with lunu miris (ground red chilli and onion sambol), pol sambol, or sometimes with jaggery and banana for a sweet version. Kiribath is deeply embedded in Sinhalese ritual and ceremonial life: it is prepared and eaten on the Sinhala and Tamil New Year (Aluth Avurudu/Puthandu, in April), on the first day of each month, at birthdays, weddings, housewarming ceremonies, and other auspicious occasions. The dish symbolises prosperity, good fortune, and new beginnings. Kiribath is traditionally the first solid food given to infants in Sinhalese custom. While it is found throughout the island as an everyday breakfast food, its ritual associations make it far more than ordinary sustenance — it is a marker of the most important moments in Sri Lankan cultural and family life. The creamy richness of the coconut milk gives the finished kiribath a gentle sweetness and a smooth, comforting texture that pairs well with the sharpness of sambol.

title: Lamprais
full_text: Lamprais is a unique Sri Lankan dish of Dutch Burgher origin — a complete meal-in-a-parcel in which stock-cooked rice, a mixed meat curry, frikkadels (Dutch-style meatballs), blachan (a shrimp paste condiment), eggplant (brinjal) curry, and seeni sambol (caramelised onion relish) are all bundled together inside a banana leaf and baked in an oven until the banana leaf imparts a distinctive sweet, grassy fragrance to the entire contents. The name derives from the Dutch lomprijst (loosely "a lump of rice"), anglicised to lamprais. The dish was created by Sri Lanka's Dutch Burgher community — an ethnic group of mixed Dutch, Portuguese Burgher, and Sri Lankan descent — who adapted it from the Javanese snack lemper during the Dutch colonial period (1658–1796), when Sri Lanka was called Dutch Ceylon. The rice is first cooked in a rich meat broth (typically made from a combination of beef, pork, and lamb) infused with whole spices including cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves, giving it an aromatic depth before it is even wrapped. Everything is assembled — a generous portion of rice surrounded by small mounds of each curry, sambol, and accompaniment — on a softened banana leaf, which is then folded into a compact parcel and tied or toothpicked shut before being baked. The banana-leaf baking merges all the flavours and seals in the steam. Lamprais is a rich, labour-intensive dish associated with festive occasions and the Burgher community's culinary heritage, and is considered one of the most sophisticated preparations in the Sri Lankan repertoire.

title: Kool
full_text: Kool (also spelled kuul) is a distinctive and complex seafood broth from the Jaffna region of northern Sri Lanka, the culinary heartland of Sri Lankan Tamil cuisine. It is one of the most elaborate and richly flavoured preparations of the Jaffna kitchen, combining a wide variety of fresh seafood with vegetables, legumes, tubers, and a souring agent, all thickened with palmyra root flour (a flour made from the roots of the palmyra palm, a tree deeply embedded in Jaffna's agricultural and cultural identity). A full traditional kool includes crab, whole small fish, cuttlefish, prawns, and crayfish (jaffna lobster), cooked together in a broth with long beans, jak (jackfruit) seeds, manioc (cassava), spinach, tamarind for acidity, and various aromatics. The use of palmyra root flour as a thickener is highly distinctive — it gives the broth a somewhat gelatinous, viscous body that distinguishes kool from other seafood soups and curries. The dish has a bold, deeply savoury, moderately spiced, and pleasantly sour character. Kool is strongly associated with the Jaffna Tamil community and is an important feature of Tamil festival occasions, family gatherings, and the region's identity as a fishing and seafood culture. The dish is difficult to prepare outside of Jaffna due to the requirement for fresh, locally-caught mixed seafood and the palmyra root flour, which is regionally specific. Kool represents the distinctive culinary heritage of Jaffna that sits apart from both Sinhalese cuisine and mainland South Indian cooking despite their many shared elements.

title: Pol roti
full_text: Pol roti (Sinhala: පොල් රොටි, "coconut roti") is a thick, unleavened Sri Lankan flatbread made by mixing shredded fresh coconut (pol) directly into a simple dough of wheat flour, water, and salt, kneading it into a firm dough, shaping it into small rounds, and cooking it on a dry iron griddle (tawa) over moderate heat until charred in patches and cooked through. No yeast, baking powder, or any leavening agent is used. The embedded shredded coconut gives the flatbread a distinctively moist interior, a slightly sweet, nutty flavour, and a pleasantly chewy, dense texture that sets it apart from the lighter godhamba roti or Indian chapati. The outer surface develops attractive dark spots where it contacts the hot griddle, while the inside remains soft and coconut-scented. Pol roti is eaten as a breakfast or snack food throughout Sri Lanka, typically served with lunu miris (a fiery ground sambol of dried red chillies, red onion, Maldive fish, and lime), pol sambol (fresh coconut, chilli, and Maldive fish), dhal curry, or seeni sambol (sweet caramelised onion relish). It is also popular as a street food and a quick home-cooked bread that requires minimal preparation time and no special equipment beyond a griddle. Pol roti exemplifies the Sri Lankan cuisine's pervasive use of coconut — here incorporated not as a liquid medium but as a textural and flavour component directly embedded in a starchy base.

title: Coconut milk
full_text: Coconut milk (Sinhala: pol kiri, meaning "coconut milk"; also kaashi kiru in Maldivian) is the single most important flavouring and cooking medium in Sri Lankan cuisine, present in a greater proportion of dishes than any other single ingredient. It is extracted from mature, grated coconut flesh by adding warm water to the gratings and squeezing the saturated mixture through cheesecloth or a fine sieve. The first extraction (first press) yields a thick, richly creamy, high-fat milk (called thick coconut milk or ata kiri) used to finish and enrich curries, add creaminess to kiribath, and make desserts. The second extraction from the same squeezed gratings produces a thinner, more watery milk (thin coconut milk or diya kiri) used as the main cooking liquid for curries throughout their cooking period. Sri Lankan curries — fish, meat, prawn, vegetable, and lentil — are almost universally cooked in thin coconut milk and finished with a pour of thick coconut milk. The thick milk must never be allowed to boil vigorously after being added; it is stirred in at the end and simmered gently to prevent splitting. Hoppers and string hoppers both use coconut milk in their batters. Kiribath (milk rice) is named for the coconut milk in which the rice is cooked. Pol sambol and lunumiris are made from grated fresh coconut rather than extracted milk. The island's culinary identity is inseparable from coconut milk, which provides the underlying creamy, slightly sweet, richly rounded quality that distinguishes Sri Lankan curries from the drier or tomato-based curries of North India or Pakistan.

title: Maldive fish
full_text: Maldive fish (Sinhala: umbalakada) is sun-dried, smoked, and cured skipjack tuna produced in the Maldives and imported to Sri Lanka, where it has been a fundamental flavouring ingredient in Sri Lankan cuisine for centuries. It is arguably the single most important flavour base in the cuisine after coconut, providing a deep, intensely savoury, umami-rich backbone to a vast range of preparations. The production process involves boiling fresh tuna, smoking it over coconut husks for several hours, and then sun-drying it until it becomes extremely hard, dense, and deeply concentrated in flavour — virtually an analogue to Japanese katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes) or Korean dried anchovies, but with a more robustly funky, smoky character. In Sri Lankan cooking, Maldive fish is used in several ways: ground or pounded into a coarse powder or flakes and added to sambols (pol sambol, lunu miris), coconut-based dishes, and vegetable curries; added as dry pieces to fish curries and soups for background depth; or crumbled into tempered onion bases. Its intense umami amplifies the flavours of whatever it is added to without making the dish taste explicitly of fish. Maldive fish is so central to Sri Lankan cuisine that pol sambol without it is considered incomplete by most cooks. Buddhist vegetarians and certain Hindu communities may avoid it, substituting additional chilli, lime, or salt. The ingredient is also used in the cuisines of Kerala and Tamil Nadu in South India.

title: Cinnamon
full_text: Ceylon cinnamon (Cinnamomum verum, formerly named Cinnamomum zeylanicum to honour its Sri Lankan origins) is the world's "true cinnamon" — the original, finest, and most delicately flavoured variety of the spice — and Sri Lanka has been its primary source for thousands of years. During the medieval spice trade, Sri Lanka's cinnamon was among the most valuable commodities in the world, drawing Arab traders, Portuguese colonisers (1505), Dutch colonisers (1658), and eventually British colonisers (1815) in a centuries-long struggle for control of the island's spice production. Ceylon cinnamon differs markedly from Cinnamomum cassia (the cinnamon sold in most supermarkets globally): it has a far more delicate, mildly sweet, almost citrusy and floral flavour; its quills are formed from multiple thin, papery layers rolled together rather than a single thick bark; and it has a significantly lower coumarin content, making it healthier for regular consumption. In Sri Lankan cooking, cinnamon is used in its true form (Ceylon variety) and appears throughout the cuisine — in curry powders (where it is dry-roasted and ground with other spices), in rice dishes including lamprais (where whole sticks are cooked into the broth), in meat preparations, and occasionally in sweets. Sri Lanka remains the world's largest producer and exporter of true Ceylon cinnamon, which commands a significant premium over cassia in global spice markets and is a geographically protected product.

title: Goraka
full_text: Goraka (Sinhala: ගොරකා; scientific name: Garcinia cambogia, also known as Malabar tamarind or kudampuli in Tamil) is a pumpkin-shaped tropical fruit native to South and Southeast Asia that is used in Sri Lankan cuisine as a powerful souring and flavouring agent, with no precise equivalent in any other South Asian cuisine. The ripe fruit is orange-yellow and contains segments of deeply sour, resinous flesh. For culinary use, the segments are dried and smoked over a fire until they become hard, blackened, and extremely concentrated — the dried pieces look like small, wrinkled black segments. These dried goraka pieces are soaked in warm water before use, releasing a thick, dark, intensely sour liquid with a fruity, slightly astringent character that is distinct from tamarind (which is also used in Sri Lankan cooking). Goraka is the defining souring agent in ambul thiyal (sour fish curry), Sri Lanka's most celebrated fish preparation, in which chunks of tuna are dry-cooked in a thick paste of goraka, black pepper, curry leaves, turmeric, and garlic until the fish is coated in a concentrated, almost dry dark coating. It is also used in certain meat curries and pickles. The souring effect of goraka is more intense and more resinous than tamarind, creating a distinctive tartness that marks many southern Sri Lankan preparations as unmistakably local. Goraka contains hydroxycitric acid and has become internationally known as a weight-loss supplement, though its culinary use in Sri Lanka long predates these commercial applications.

title: Pandan leaf
full_text: Pandan leaf (Pandanus amaryllifolius; Sinhala: rampe; Tamil: rampa) is an aromatic tropical plant leaf that is one of the most characteristic flavouring ingredients of Sri Lankan cuisine and a signature element that helps distinguish Sri Lankan cooking from the cuisines of neighbouring South Asia. The leaves are long, narrow, and intensely fragrant — their flavour and aroma are often described as a sweet, grassy, slightly vanilla-like, floral fragrance with a faint nutty quality. In Sri Lankan cooking, pandan leaves are almost always used whole as an aromatic additive rather than ground or processed: a knotted leaf or two are dropped into hot oil at the beginning of cooking alongside curry leaves, sliced onions, and other aromatics, releasing their fragrance into the cooking fat. They then remain in the curry or rice dish throughout cooking, their volatile oils slowly infusing the preparation. Pandan is added to virtually all Sri Lankan rice preparations, coconut milk-based curries, dhal, and even into the batter for some sweets. It pairs particularly well with coconut milk, amplifying the sweetness and adding aromatic depth. Pandan leaf is also widely used in Southeast Asian cuisines (particularly Thai, Malaysian, and Indonesian cooking), but its use in Sri Lankan cuisine represents an independent tradition that likely dates back centuries of Indian Ocean spice trade connections. Fresh pandan is available throughout the tropics; dried or frozen pandan is used in diaspora communities. Its most concentrated form, pandan extract, is used as a flavouring in sweets and desserts.

title: Pan-frying hoppers
full_text: Pan-frying hoppers is one of the most technically demanding and distinctive cooking techniques in Sri Lankan cuisine, requiring a specific vessel, a fermented batter, and a practised swirling motion that produces the hopper's characteristic bowl shape with crispy edges and a soft centre. The pan used is the appa thattiya — a small, round-bottomed, wok-like pan approximately 15–20 cm in diameter with a tight-fitting lid. The pan is heated over a medium-high flame and lightly greased with coconut oil. A ladleful of the fermented batter (rice flour, coconut milk, a pinch of sugar and salt, and a small amount of palm toddy or yeast-risen overnight) is poured into the centre of the hot pan. Immediately, the cook picks up the pan by its handles and rotates it in quick, circular swirling motions so that the batter climbs the sides of the pan in a thin layer, while the thicker excess settles back into the bowl base. The lid is then placed over the pan and the hopper cooks undisturbed for one to two minutes, during which the steam trapped under the lid cooks the centre from above while the base cooks by direct contact with the pan. A well-made hopper should release cleanly from the pan and have an outer rim that is thin, lacy, and deeply golden to amber — almost crispy — while the centre is thick, soft, spongy, and barely coloured. For egg hoppers, a cracked egg is dropped into the centre just after swirling and before the lid is applied, setting as the hopper cooks.

title: Fermenting batter
full_text: Fermenting rice flour batter is a foundational technique in Sri Lankan cuisine, essential for producing the distinctive slightly sour, complex flavour and the light, porous texture of both hoppers (appa) and the batter used in certain pancake preparations. The traditional fermenting agent is palm toddy (Sinhala: ra) — the naturally fermented sap collected from cut flower spathes of the coconut palm — which contains wild yeasts and lactic acid bacteria that both leaven and acidify the batter simultaneously. A small amount of toddy is mixed into a batter of rice flour, coconut milk, sugar, and salt, and the mixture is left covered at room temperature for a minimum of six to twelve hours, often overnight. During fermentation, the wild yeasts in the toddy consume the sugars in the batter, producing carbon dioxide (leavening) and alcohol and lactic acid (souring). The resulting batter has a pleasantly tangy, slightly yeasty aroma and a slightly aerated consistency that, when swirled in the heated hopper pan, produces a batter that sets with a porous, spongy texture in the centre and crisps at the thin edges. Commercial yeast has largely replaced palm toddy in modern households and restaurants, as toddy is increasingly difficult to source in urban areas. The resulting hoppers are marginally less complex in flavour than traditional toddy-fermented versions, but structurally similar. String hopper dough (which is pressed rather than poured) does not require fermentation and is made with plain hot-water rice flour dough.

title: Sambol preparation
full_text: Sambol preparation is a fundamental culinary technique of Sri Lankan cooking and produces the category of raw or minimally cooked condiments — called sambol (Sinhala) or chamanthi (Tamil) — that are served alongside virtually every Sri Lankan meal as essential flavour counterpoints to the richness of coconut milk curries and the neutrality of rice. Sambols are prepared by grinding, pounding, or mixing flavour-intense raw ingredients together into a rough, intensely seasoned paste or loose mixture, not cooked in the conventional sense but rather assembled through the mechanical action of a grinding stone (miris gala) or, today, a blender. The most important Sri Lankan sambol is pol sambol (fresh coconut sambol): freshly scraped coconut flesh is combined with dried red chillies (either whole or as ground chilli flakes), grated red onion, Maldive fish flakes, lime juice, and salt, then ground or kneaded together until the mixture is cohesive, evenly combined, and the chilli colour bleeds into the pale coconut. The result is intensely savoury, spicy, sharp, and fresh — a perfect foil for the creamy richness of a coconut curry. Lunu miris is a fierier, more liquid sambol of ground dried red chillies, red onion, Maldive fish, and lime, traditionally made in a stone mortar. Seeni sambol (caramelised onion relish, cooked but cold) uses a different preparation method — slowly frying onions with Maldive fish, dried chilli, and sugar until deeply caramelised and jam-like. Together, these sambols define the flavour intensity that distinguishes the Sri Lankan table.
