title: Afghan cuisine
full_text: Afghan cuisine is the culinary tradition of Afghanistan, a landlocked Central Asian country at the crossroads of South Asia, Central Asia, and the Middle East, and its food reflects this position as a historic junction of Silk Road trade routes. The cuisine draws on Persian (Iranian), Turkic, Central Asian (particularly Uzbek and Tajik), and South Asian (Mughal-influenced) culinary traditions, resulting in a distinctive cooking style that blends elements of all these traditions into a coherent national identity. The diet of most Afghans revolves around rice-based dishes (palao/pilaf preparations), various forms of naan bread eaten with most meals, meat stews (qorma), kebabs, and dumpling dishes. The national dish is Kabuli palaw — a festive rice dish with caramelised carrots, raisins, nuts, and lamb. Afghan cuisine is halal; pork is entirely absent and all meat is prepared according to Islamic dietary law. Lamb is the most prized and widely used meat. Dairy products including qoroot (a very hard, sour dried whey cheese), yoghurt (chakkah), and fresh cheese feature prominently as accompaniments and toppings. Afghan dishes are notable for their characteristic balance of sweet and savoury flavours, achieved by incorporating dried fruits, nuts, and caramelised vegetables into rice and meat preparations — a legacy of Persian culinary aesthetics. Spices are used with restraint: coriander, cumin, cardamom, turmeric, black pepper, dried mint, and locally-grown saffron are the signature flavourings. Afghan food is generally mild rather than fiercely hot. Tea (chai) is the principal beverage, consumed in vast quantities throughout the day.

title: Kabuli palaw
full_text: Kabuli palaw (also spelled qabili palaw, kabuli pulao; Dari: قابلی پلو) is the national dish of Afghanistan and the most celebrated rice preparation in the country — a richly flavoured pilaf of long-grain basmati rice, slow-cooked lamb or beef, and a distinctive topping of julienned caramelised carrots, plump raisins, and slivered almonds or pistachios that together create the characteristic sweet-savoury balance central to Afghan cuisine. The name likely derives from the Persian word qabili ("worthy" or "capable"), reflecting the dish's status as the apex of Afghan cooking, traditionally reserved for guests of honour, weddings, Eid celebrations, and important family gatherings. To prepare it, lamb pieces are braised in a spiced broth (with cardamom, cinnamon, cumin, and sometimes black pepper) until tender. The broth is then used to partially cook parboiled basmati rice, which is drained and layered back into the pot over the meat. The pot is sealed and finished by the dum method — low heat with a tight lid, sometimes weighted — allowing the rice to steam in the meat's fragrant vapour. The topping is made by separately sautéing julienned carrots until caramelised and soft, then frying raisins briefly until they puff, and adding slivered nuts; this is arranged over the finished rice before serving. The dish is presented mounded on a large communal platter, with the meat buried under the rice and the sweet carrot-raisin-nut topping arranged on top. Kabuli palaw is always placed at the centre of the table, with other dishes arranged around its perimeter. The traditional ingredients in Kabuli palaw are raisins, onion, garlic, carrots, almonds, pistachios, lamb, and a spiced syrup made with sugar, lamb stock, garam masala and cardamom which is used to caramelise the carrots and coat the topping before it is arranged over the rice.

title: Mantu
full_text: Mantu (also spelled mantoo; Dari/Pashto: منتو) is one of the most beloved dishes in Afghan cuisine — large steamed dumplings filled with a mixture of coarsely ground or minced lamb or beef, finely chopped onions, and spices (typically black pepper, coriander, and sometimes chilli), served with a complex layering of sauces that transforms a simple dumpling into a richly flavoured ceremonial dish. The dough is made from flour, water, and salt, rolled thin, cut into squares, and each square is filled with the seasoned meat-onion mixture, then folded and sealed into a boat or crescent shape. The filled dumplings are arranged in a tiered steamer over boiling water and cooked for 20–25 minutes until the dough is translucent and the filling cooked through. The distinctive presentation of mantu involves layering the steamed dumplings over a base of split yellow lentils or tomato-based sauce, then generously topping them with chakkah (thick strained yoghurt whipped with garlic and salt), and finishing with a drizzle of tomato-meat sauce, dried mint, and sometimes ground red chilli. The combination of soft dumpling, tangy yoghurt, savoury meat sauce, and the herbal accent of dried mint creates a layered, contrasting flavour profile that is uniquely Afghan. Mantu is traditionally a celebration dish prepared for Eid, Nowruz, weddings, and large family gatherings, as making it requires considerable time and skill — typically a communal effort involving many hands. Its roots lie in the Turkic and Mongol dumpling traditions of Central Asia, related to Turkish mantı and other Silk Road dumpling forms.

title: Ashak
full_text: Ashak (also spelled aushak; Dari: آشک) is a traditional Afghan dumpling dish closely related to mantu but distinctively filled with gandana (a variety of leek or scallion native to Afghanistan) and/or chopped spring onions rather than meat, making it a vegetarian dumpling that is nonetheless served with meat sauces. The name is believed to derive from a Persian word related to leeks. Thin sheets of wheat flour dough are cut into rounds or squares, filled with the chopped seasoned leek-onion mixture (typically seasoned with salt, black pepper, and sometimes dried red chilli), folded, sealed, and then boiled (rather than steamed, as mantu is) in salted water until the dough is cooked through and slightly tender. The boiled dumplings are served on a large platter, layered over a base of cooked tomato-meat sauce (made from minced lamb or beef with onions, tomatoes, and spices), then covered generously with chakkah (thick strained yoghurt with garlic and salt), and finished with dried mint and sometimes a dusting of red chilli powder. The contrast between the slightly yielding dumpling filled with fragrant leeks, the rich savoury meat sauce below, and the cool, tangy, garlicky yoghurt above is characteristic of Afghan flavour layering. Ashak is considered a spring dish associated with the availability of fresh gandana, and is particularly associated with Nowruz (Afghan New Year) celebrations. It is served at festive gatherings and in Afghan restaurants worldwide, where it is one of the dishes that most effectively represents the complexity of Afghan cuisine to international diners.

title: Qorma
full_text: Qorma (also spelled korma, qormah; Dari/Pashto: قورمه) is a broad category of onion- and tomato-based meat or vegetable stew that constitutes the backbone of everyday Afghan cooking and is served as the standard accompaniment to plain steamed rice (challow) at most Afghan meals. Unlike the South Asian korma, which is typically a cream- or yoghurt-based mild curry, the Afghan qorma is a more assertively flavoured, oil-rich stew built on a base of deeply caramelised onions — cooked slowly in oil until they pass through golden and reach a deep amber-brown — which provides the characteristic sweet, slightly bitter backbone of the dish. Tomatoes, spices (cumin, coriander, black pepper, turmeric), and the main protein or vegetable ingredient are then added and simmered together. There are hundreds of qorma varieties in Afghan cooking: qorma-e-gosht (lamb or beef stew), qorma-e-murgh (chicken), qorma-e-sabzi (spinach and lamb), qorma-e-lubi (bean stew), and many others. Characteristic Afghan variations incorporate dried fruits — dried apricots, prunes, or pomegranate seeds — and nuts into the stew, creating the sweet-savoury flavour profile that distinguishes Afghan stews from those of neighbouring cuisines. Qorma is also used as the flavouring base mixed into palaw during preparation, making it an integral part of the pilaf-making process. When served alongside plain challow rice, the qorma sauce is spooned over the white rice and eaten together, with naan used to scoop and accompany.

title: Lamb
full_text: Lamb is the most prestigious, culturally significant, and widely consumed meat in Afghan cuisine, and its centrality to the food culture reflects Afghanistan's long pastoral nomadic tradition and the country's geography as highland sheep-herding territory. Sheep (both fat-tailed breeds and Karakul) have been raised throughout Afghanistan for millennia, providing wool, milk, and above all meat — a protein source that has sustained communities across the country's diverse and sometimes harsh landscapes. In Afghan cooking, lamb appears in virtually every meat preparation: kabuli palaw (the national dish) uses braised lamb as its protein base; mantu dumplings are filled with minced lamb; kebabs are made from lamb skewered and grilled over charcoal; qorma stews simmer lamb slowly with caramelised onions; and whole spit-roasted lamb is prepared for weddings and large celebrations. A particularly prized component is jijeq — the fat from the tail of fat-tailed sheep breeds — which is added to kebab skewers to baste the meat as it grills and is considered a flavour enhancer. Lamb fat in general is rendered and used as a cooking medium in traditional preparations. Lamb is also central to religious and cultural observance: at Eid al-Adha, families sacrifice a lamb (qurbani) and distribute the meat to family, neighbours, and the poor. In Afghan cuisine, lamb is generally cooked more simply than in South Asian traditions — minimally spiced, allowing the quality of the meat to express itself — which reflects the Persian and Central Asian culinary aesthetic of ingredient-led cooking.

title: Rice
full_text: Rice is the most important and culturally prestigious grain in Afghanistan, forming the centrepiece of the cuisine's most celebrated dishes despite the country's status as a wheat-growing nation. The Afghan relationship with rice is fundamentally different from that in neighbouring South Asian cuisines: in Afghanistan, rice is not merely a staple but a vehicle for culinary artistry, and the ability to cook a perfect palaw (pilaf) — with every grain separate, fragrant, and properly coloured — is considered a fundamental marker of cooking skill and hospitality. The principal rice used is long-grain basmati, though locally grown Afghani varieties of medium-grain rice are also used for everyday preparations. Afghan rice cookery centres on two distinct preparations: challow (plain steamed white rice, cooked to a light, fluffy texture with a golden crust at the bottom of the pot called the palaw crust or tahdig) and palaw (rice cooked in meat broth and aromatics, then finished with meat, caramelised vegetables, fruits, and nuts). The rice for palaw is typically parboiled first in heavily salted water, then drained and returned to the pot with the meat broth, layered with the garnishes, and finished by the dum method (sealed low heat steaming). The bottom of the rice pot develops a crust called zard-e-palaw (golden rice) which is considered a delicacy. Rice cultivation in Afghanistan is concentrated in the northern provinces of Kunduz, Baghlan, and the fertile plains of the Amu Darya basin, where irrigation supports paddy farming.

title: Yogurt
full_text: Yoghurt (Dari: mast; also qoroot when dried and concentrated) is one of the most important dairy products in Afghan cuisine and functions both as an everyday condiment and as a fundamental flavouring and textural element in some of the country's most important dishes. Afghanistan has a strong pastoralist tradition and dairy products have been central to the diet of nomadic and rural communities for thousands of years. Fresh yoghurt (chakkah when strained thick; mast when plain) is consumed throughout the day — as a breakfast accompaniment, as a cooling side dish alongside grilled meats, as a dipping sauce, and as the base for the complex layered sauces that finish mantu and ashak dumplings. For dumpling dishes, yoghurt is strained through cheesecloth to produce a very thick, creamy chakkah, then whipped with raw garlic and salt until smooth and pungent — a sauce that provides cooling acidity and richness against the savoury meat sauces and warm dumplings. Qoroot is a highly distinctive Afghan dairy product — a very hard, extremely sour, dried ball of whey solids produced by straining and drying yoghurt or buttermilk — that has virtually no equivalent in other South Asian cuisines. It can be eaten raw as a pungent, dry snack or dissolved in water to make qoroot sauce, which is served over mantu, kichri, and other preparations. Borani dishes (sautéed vegetable preparations topped with garlic yoghurt) are another category of Afghan preparations that showcases yoghurt as a finishing element.

title: Dried fruits
full_text: Dried fruits are among the most characteristic and culturally distinctive ingredients of Afghan cuisine, reflecting both the country's agricultural tradition of fruit cultivation in its fertile valleys and the Persian culinary heritage that incorporates fruit into savoury preparations as a matter of aesthetic philosophy rather than just sweetening. Afghanistan is one of the world's most important producers of dried apricots, raisins (including many heirloom varieties from Kandahar and the Kunduz region), dried mulberries, dried figs, dried plums, and pomegranate seeds (anardana), and these products have been central to Afghan trade and daily diet for centuries. In cooking, dried fruits appear most iconically in kabuli palaw, where plump raisins are fried briefly in oil until they puff and sweeten, then arranged with caramelised carrots over the finished rice — their sweetness contrasting with the savoury lamb broth-cooked rice. Dried apricots and prunes are added to qorma stews for sweet-sour depth. Dried mulberries are eaten as a snack or added to pulao. Pomegranate molasses (rob-e-anar) and ground dried sour grapes (ghore angoor) are used as souring and flavouring agents in salads and condiments. Fresh dried fruits are also eaten abundantly as snacks and as part of the traditional Afghan hospitality ritual of presenting guests with a tray of dried fruits and nuts (kishmish-o-badam) alongside green tea. The combination of sweet dried fruits with savoury meat and rice dishes is a signature of the Persian-influenced culinary tradition running through Afghan cooking.

title: Pilaf cooking
full_text: Pilaf cooking (palaw preparation) is the most important and technically demanding cooking technique in Afghan cuisine, and mastery of a perfectly cooked palaw — with every grain of rice separate, fragrant, and properly coloured, surrounded by tender meat and topped with sweet-savoury garnishes — is considered the pinnacle of Afghan culinary skill. The Afghan palaw method differs from most other pilaf traditions in the world in its use of a two-stage cooking process combined with the dum (sealed steaming) technique. In the first stage, basmati rice is pre-soaked, parboiled in heavily salted boiling water until just three-quarters cooked (the grain should still have a firm white centre), then drained. Separately, meat (usually lamb) is braised in a spiced broth with caramelised onions until tender. In the second stage, the pot is lightly oiled, the parboiled rice is returned to the pot and pressed gently, the meat broth is poured over the rice, and the garnishes (caramelised carrots, fried raisins, nuts) are layered on top. The pot is tightly sealed — sometimes with a cloth wrapped around the lid to trap all steam — and placed over very low heat for 30–45 minutes. This dum cooking allows the rice to finish absorbing the meat's fragrant broth through gentle steaming without any direct contact with liquid, producing rice that is fluffy, separate, and intensely aromatic. A prized side effect is the development of a golden, slightly crisp layer at the bottom of the pot (zard-e-palaw), which is scraped out and served separately as a delicacy.

title: Stuffed dumplings
full_text: The making of stuffed dumplings — primarily mantu and ashak — is one of the most socially and technically significant cooking traditions in Afghan cuisine, associated with celebration, family gatherings, and the expression of hospitality through labour-intensive preparation. Both mantu and ashak require the making of a simple thin wheat dough (flour, water, a little oil and salt), rolling it very thin, and cutting it into regular pieces — squares for mantu, rounds or larger squares for ashak — that are then filled, folded, and sealed before being cooked. For mantu (steamed meat dumplings), the filling is a mixture of coarsely minced lamb or beef with finely chopped onion and black pepper; each square of dough is filled in the centre and the four corners pinched or gathered together at the top to create a purse shape. The sealed dumplings are arranged on oiled steamer trays in a tiered steamer and cooked over boiling water for 20–30 minutes. For ashak (boiled leek dumplings), the leek and spring onion filling is placed in the centre of larger dough rounds, which are folded in half and sealed at the edges, then boiled in salted water. The communal aspect of dumpling-making is central to Afghan food culture: in traditional households, making mantu for a celebration involves many family members working together at a large table, each folding dumplings with characteristic speed and precision. The finished dumplings are never considered complete without their toppings — for both mantu and ashak, layered sauces of cooked tomato-meat, strained garlic yoghurt (chakkah), and dried mint are essential components of the presentation.

title: Chaat
full_text: Chaat (Hindi: चाट, from the verb chāṭnā meaning "to lick") is a broad and vibrant family of fried dough snacks and street food preparations originating in the Indian subcontinent, unified by their characteristic combination of contrasting flavours — simultaneously salty, sour, sweet, and spicy — and contrasting textures — crispy fried elements alongside soft fillings and cooling yoghurt. Chaat originated in Uttar Pradesh, attributed by some historians to the Mughal imperial era, and has since spread across the entire subcontinent and Indian diaspora globally. The defining elements shared by virtually all chaat preparations are: fried dough (puris, papri, or sev); a tangy tamarind chutney (imli ki chutney); a bright, herby green coriander-mint chutney (hari chutney); yoghurt (dahi); chaat masala (a tangy spice blend including amchoor/dried mango, cumin, kala namak/black salt, and red chilli); and sev (fine crunchy chickpea flour noodles). The most famous chaat varieties include panipuri (golgappa) — small, crispy hollow puris filled with spiced potatoes and peas, then dipped in tangy spiced water and eaten in one bite; bhel puri — a dry mix of puffed rice, papri, sev, onion, tomato, and chutneys from Mumbai; sev puri — flat papri topped with potato, onions, and chutneys; dahi puri — puris topped with yoghurt and chutneys; aloo tikki chaat — spiced potato patties topped with chickpea curry, yoghurt, and chutneys; and papri chaat — flat fried crisps layered with boiled potatoes, chickpeas, yoghurt, and chutneys. The category also includes samosa chaat, raj kachori, and hundreds of regional variations. Panipuri is known as golgappa in Delhi, pani puri in Mumbai and Maharashtra, phuchka in Bengal, and gup chup in Odisha and Hyderabad. Chaat stalls (chaatwalas) are ubiquitous across South Asia and in diaspora communities worldwide.

title: Korma
full_text: Korma (also qorma, kurma; Urdu: قورمہ; Hindi: क़ोरमा) is a rich braised meat or vegetable curry dish originating in Mughal court cuisine and now one of the most widely eaten curries across India, Pakistan, and the South Asian diaspora worldwide. The name derives from the Turkic qawirma ("a fried thing"), referring to the core technique of braising meat in yoghurt or cream-based sauces to produce a mild, aromatic, luxuriously smooth curry. The defining character of korma is its mildness and creaminess: unlike most South Asian curries, korma avoids strong chilli heat, instead developing flavour through the slow reduction of yoghurt or cream with ground nuts (cashews or almonds), whole aromatic spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, mace), and sometimes saffron. Onions are first caramelised slowly in ghee to develop deep sweetness; the meat (usually lamb, chicken, or goat) is then added and braised slowly in the yoghurt-cream-nut sauce until it is deeply tender and coated in a rich, ivory-coloured sauce. Korma originated in the Mughal imperial kitchens of 16th–17th century northern India, where it was an elite dish first recorded in cookbooks from the reign of Shah Alam (r. 1643–1712). Distinct regional styles exist: the Lucknawi (Awadhi) korma is subtly spiced and very delicate; the Hyderabadi korma uses ground coconut and sesame alongside nuts; vegetarian korma (navratan korma — "nine gems" — made with nine vegetables and nuts) is popular across India. In British and international Indian restaurants, chicken korma has become one of the most popular curry dishes in the world, typically milder and creamier than the original Mughal preparation.

title: Tandoori chicken
full_text: Tandoori chicken is a celebrated South Asian dish of chicken marinated in yoghurt and spices — including Kashmiri red chilli (which gives the characteristic deep red-orange colour), cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — then cooked at extremely high temperatures in a cylindrical clay oven called a tandoor. The result is chicken with a distinctive charred, smoky exterior, a slightly caramelised crust, and moist, flavourful interior that is substantially different from any oven- or pan-cooked preparation. The dish is most closely associated with Punjabi cuisine and North India, and its name reflects the tandoor oven in which it is cooked. The tandoor reaches temperatures of 300–480°C (570–900°F), far hotter than a conventional oven, which produces the rapid surface charring and the characteristic smoky flavour that define the dish. Tandoori chicken's origins are attributed to Kundan Lal Gujral, who is said to have adapted the Mughal tandoor tradition to chicken at his restaurant Moti Mahal in Peshawar (then British India, now Pakistan) in the 1940s. After the 1947 partition, Gujral relocated to Delhi where the dish gained enormous popularity and became emblematic of North Indian restaurant cooking. Tandoori chicken is served with lemon wedges, sliced raw onion rings, and mint chutney, and is eaten with naan or plain rice. It is the direct precursor to tikka (boneless marinated chicken pieces cooked in the tandoor) and the globally ubiquitous chicken tikka masala, which adds a tomato-cream sauce to the tikka. The preparation is central to Indian restaurant culture globally and is one of the most recognisable Indian dishes internationally.

title: Lassi
full_text: Lassi is a traditional yoghurt-based drink that is one of the most important and beloved beverages in the Indian subcontinent, particularly in Punjab, Rajasthan, and the broader North India belt. It is made by blending yoghurt (dahi) with water and typically either salt or sugar, whisked or churned until smooth and slightly frothy. The basic lassi is simply yoghurt thinned to a drinkable consistency, but the drink exists in two principal forms and innumerable regional variations: sweet lassi (meethi lassi) — blended with sugar, rosewater, cardamom, and sometimes saffron or mango; and salty/savoury lassi (namkeen lassi) — made with salt, roasted cumin, and sometimes black pepper, consumed as a digestive and cooling drink. The most celebrated variety is mango lassi — yoghurt blended with fresh or canned Alphonso mango purée and sugar — which has become one of the most internationally recognised Indian beverages. In Punjab, lassi is a cultural institution: thick, full-fat dahi churned in a large clay vessel (kullar) until frothing and creamy, served in large quantities at roadside dhabas, wedding feasts, and as a daily summer drink. Amritsari lassi is particularly famous for its extraordinary thickness and fat content from whole buffalo milk yoghurt. In Rajasthan, a salty version called chaas (buttermilk) or chaach is consumed throughout the day for its cooling properties. Lassi's cultural significance extends beyond refreshment: it is a symbol of Punjab's dairy-rich agricultural prosperity and is intrinsically linked to festivals including Holi, Baisakhi, and daily religious life. A similar unseasoned version called ayran is consumed in Pakistan and Afghanistan.

title: Rajma
full_text: Rajma (Hindi: राजमा) is a hearty North Indian curry of red kidney beans cooked in a thick, deeply spiced onion-tomato gravy, and one of the most beloved everyday comfort foods across North India, Nepal, and the Punjab region of Pakistan. The combination of rajma with steamed basmati rice — rajma chawal — is considered a quintessential Punjabi home meal, as culturally fundamental to the region as dal makhani or chole bhature. Red kidney beans (phaseolus vulgaris) are not native to India; they were introduced from Mexico via the Columbian Exchange after the 16th century. The finest rajma is said to grow in the hilly regions of Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, and Jammu — particularly the small, speckled Himalayan varieties known for their nutty flavour and creamy texture when cooked. To prepare rajma, dried beans are soaked overnight, then pressure-cooked until very tender. Separately, a bhuna masala base is made by frying onions until golden, adding ginger-garlic paste, chopped tomatoes, and spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, Kashmiri red chilli, garam masala), and cooking until the oil separates. The cooked beans are added to this masala and simmered together, with some beans mashed against the pot to thicken the gravy. The finished curry should have a thick, clinging sauce that saturates the soft beans. Garnishing with fresh coriander, a squeeze of lemon, and a drizzle of ghee completes the preparation. Rajma is eaten alongside jeera rice (cumin-tempered rice) or with roti and a simple salad of raw onion, tomato, and cucumber. Together with dal makhani and chole, rajma is considered one of the three pillars of Punjabi vegetarian cooking.

title: Dhokla
full_text: Dhokla is a savory steamed snack from Gujarat, western India, made from a fermented batter of rice and split chickpeas (chana dal) that is leavened, steamed in flat trays, cut into squares, and finished with a tempering of mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chillies in oil. It is one of the most emblematic dishes of Gujarati cuisine and a nationally beloved Indian snack. The basic khaman dhokla — now often just called dhokla — is made from a batter of besan (chickpea flour), sour yoghurt or citric acid, water, salt, sugar, ginger-chilli paste, and a leavening agent (either baking soda or eno fruit salt). The batter is poured into greased flat trays and steamed for 10–15 minutes until the sponge sets. The tempering (tadka) of oil with mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chillies, and sesame seeds is poured hot over the steamed sponge immediately before serving. The finished dhokla is soft, slightly spongy, pale yellow, pleasantly sour, mildly spiced, and inherently light — it is both low in fat and naturally gluten-free in its original chana dal-based form. It is served with green coriander chutney and occasionally tamarind chutney. Traditional Gujarat makes several dhokla varieties: the original khatti dhokla (sour dhokla from fermented dal-rice batter), white dhokla, khaman dhokla (the quick modern besan version), Surti dhokla, and handvo (a savoury steamed cake of fermented rice and lentils with vegetables). In Gujarati food culture, dhokla is eaten as breakfast, as an afternoon snack (farsan), and as part of the festive thali. It has spread from Gujarat to become one of the most popular snacks sold across Indian cities, in supermarkets, and in international Indian food stores.

title: Seekh kebab
full_text: Seekh kebab (Urdu/Hindi: سیخ کباب / सीख कबाब, from seekh meaning "skewer") is a popular grilled meat preparation originating in Mughal court cuisine and now ubiquitous across the Indian subcontinent, particularly in North India, Pakistan, and Afghanistan. It is made from minced or finely ground meat — traditionally mutton or lamb, though beef (common in Pakistan) and chicken are also widely used — mixed with finely chopped or grated onion, fresh ginger and garlic, green chillies, fresh coriander, and a spice blend typically including cumin, coriander, garam masala, and sometimes dried pomegranate seeds (anardana) or raw papaya as a tenderiser. The seasoned mince is kneaded thoroughly to develop binding, then shaped around long flat metal skewers (seekhs) in elongated cylindrical rolls. These are cooked in a tandoor clay oven, over charcoal, or on a grill, rotating until cooked through with a slightly charred exterior. The preparation requires sufficient fat content in the mince (lamb or mutton are naturally suited; chicken often requires added fat or cheese) to hold the meat on the skewer without crumbling. The finished seekh kebabs are served with freshly baked naan or roomali roti (a thin, handkerchief-like flatbread), raw onion rings, fresh green coriander chutney, and lime wedges. In Lucknow, the galoti kebab (a patty version of seekh with an exceptionally fine texture) and kakori kebab (a very delicate, aromatic seekh) are celebrated sub-varieties of the Awadhi tradition. Seekh kebabs are a staple at Indian and Pakistani barbecue gatherings, roadside dhabas, and restaurant menus worldwide.

title: Chicken tikka masala
full_text: Chicken tikka masala is one of the most internationally famous dishes associated with Indian cuisine — a preparation of boneless chicken pieces (tikka) that have been marinated in spiced yoghurt and cooked in a tandoor, then served in a creamy, mildly spiced tomato-cream sauce enriched with butter or ghee. The dish has contested origins: the most commonly cited account attributes it to South Asian immigrant cooks in Britain who adapted the dry tandoori chicken tikka to the British preference for sauced curries, possibly by accident when a customer requested a sauce for his dry tikka and a cook improvised with a tomato-cream gravy. A 2009 survey by the British Curry Club estimated it was the most popular curry in UK restaurants. The word tikka refers to boneless marinated and grilled meat pieces; the masala is the tomato-cream sauce. The tikka preparation — chicken pieces marinated overnight in yoghurt, Kashmiri red chilli, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice, then cooked in a hot tandoor or grill — is authentically North Indian (Punjabi) in origin. The masala sauce of onion, tomato purée, cream or yoghurt, spices, and sometimes fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi) gives the dish its characteristic orange-red colour and mild, slightly sweet, fragrant character. While its precise birthplace remains disputed between India and the UK, chicken tikka masala has become one of the global ambassadors of South Asian cooking and a staple of Indian restaurant menus worldwide. It is typically served with basmati rice or naan.

title: Mustard seeds
full_text: Mustard seeds (Hindi: sarson/rai; Bengali: sorshe; Tamil: kadugu) are among the most universally used spices in Indian and South Asian cooking, employed as a key aromatic in the tempering (tadka or chaunk) technique that initiates or finishes countless dishes across every region of the subcontinent. Three main varieties are used: small black mustard seeds (Brassica nigra) — sharp and intensely pungent, used across South and East India for tempering; brown mustard seeds (Brassica juncea) — milder, widely grown in North India and used in condiments and pickles; and yellow/white mustard seeds (Sinapis alba) — the mildest, used in Western cooking and some Bengali preparations. In tempering, whole mustard seeds are added to hot oil where they rapidly pop and splutter, releasing a nutty, pungent flavour that suffuses the cooking oil and is then poured over or stirred into lentils, chutneys, chutneys, vegetable dishes, and rice. In South Indian cooking, mustard seed tempering (along with curry leaves and dried red chilli) is the foundational flavour base for sambar, rasam, chutneys, and most vegetable preparations. In Bengali cuisine, mustard seeds are ground with water and turmeric into a pungent yellow paste (shorshe bata) that is used as the primary flavouring in shorshe ilish (mustard-marinated hilsa curry) and numerous fish preparations. Mustard oil (extracted from pressing mustard seeds) is the defining cooking fat of Bengal and Bangladesh. Mustard seeds are also the primary ingredient in South Asian pickles (achar), where they are used whole or ground as a preservative and flavouring agent. Mustard greens (sarson ka saag) from the Brassica napus plant are consumed as vegetables in Punjab and other northern regions.

title: Curry leaves
full_text: Curry leaves (Murraya koenigii; Hindi: kadi patta; Tamil: karivepilai; Telugu: karivepaku; Kannada: karibevu) are fresh aromatic leaves from the curry leaf tree native to the Indian subcontinent, and one of the most characteristic and irreplaceable flavouring ingredients in South Indian, Sri Lankan, and Maharashtrian cuisine. Fresh curry leaves have a distinctive warm, aromatic fragrance — simultaneously herbal, slightly citrusy, and faintly spicy — that is impossible to replicate with dried versions or substitutes. In South Indian cooking, curry leaves are almost invariably included in the tempering (tadka) that begins or finishes preparations: oil is heated, mustard seeds are added until they pop, then curry leaves are added and allowed to sizzle and crisp briefly — the combination of popped mustard and fried curry leaf is the foundational flavour signature of Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, and Kerala cooking. They appear in sambar, rasam, chutneys, curd rice, various rice preparations (lemon rice, tamarind rice), coconut chutneys, and most vegetable stir-fries (poriyal). In Sri Lanka, curry leaves are similarly used in all forms of curry preparation and are one of the defining aromatics alongside pandan leaf and lemongrass. In Maharashtra, they appear in the tempering for poha, upma, and various curries. In Kerala, they are fried directly in coconut oil and used to finish fish curries. Curry leaves are also used in Gujarati cooking (particularly in kadhi and some vegetable preparations) and appear in North Indian cooking in some regional preparations. Unlike bay leaves or cassia leaves, curry leaves are generally eaten in the dish rather than removed.

title: Black pepper
full_text: Black pepper (Piper nigrum; Hindi: kali mirch; Tamil: milagu; Malayalam: kurumulaku) is one of the world's oldest and most historically significant spices, and India — particularly the Malabar Coast of Kerala — is its original home and has been its primary producing region for over 2,000 years. The spice trade in black pepper was a primary motivation for European exploration of sea routes to Asia in the 15th century, and Kerala's pepper ports (Kozhikode, Kochi) were at the centre of global trade networks for centuries. Black pepper berries are the dried, unripe fruits of the Piper nigrum vine; white pepper is made from the same berries with the outer skin removed; green peppercorns are the fresh, unripe berries. The pungent heat of black pepper comes primarily from the alkaloid piperine, which has a clean, sharp bite different from the capsaicin heat of chilli peppers. Before chillies were introduced to India via the Columbian Exchange in the 16th century, black pepper was the primary source of heat in Indian cooking. In South Indian cooking, black pepper remains central — in rasam (a thin pepper-tamarind broth), in pepper chicken, in Chettinad cuisine (which is particularly famous for its bold pepper use), and in Kerala's traditional preparations. Whole black peppercorns are an essential component of garam masala and most North Indian spice blends. In traditional Ayurvedic medicine, black pepper is considered a digestive stimulant and is combined with ginger and long pepper (Piper longum) in the classical trikatu preparation. India remains one of the world's top producers, with Kerala producing the finest Malabar pepper.

title: Saffron
full_text: Saffron (Crocus sativus; Hindi/Urdu: kesar or zafran; Kashmiri: kong) is the world's most expensive spice by weight — consisting of the hand-harvested dried stigmas of the saffron crocus flower — and is used in Indian cuisine primarily as a flavouring and colouring agent in the most prestigious and celebratory preparations. India's finest saffron grows in the Pampore region of the Kashmir Valley, where the cool high-altitude climate, specific soil conditions, and dry autumns produce a saffron of exceptional quality with intense colour and aroma. Kashmiri saffron is considered among the world's best and commands significant premium prices. In Indian cooking, saffron provides two key qualities: an extraordinary golden-yellow to deep amber colour, and a distinctive floral, honey-like, slightly metallic aroma and flavour that is unlike any other spice. It is used in small quantities — typically steeped in warm milk or water before being added to dishes — in biryani (where it colours and flavours the upper rice layer), kheer and payasam (rice puddings), shahi tukra (Mughlai bread pudding), kulfi (Indian ice cream), rasmalai, and various Mughlai and Kashmiri meat preparations. In Kashmiri cooking specifically, saffron is used more generously than elsewhere in India — in the celebratory yakhni (lamb in yoghurt broth), in the festival drink kahwa (saffron green tea), and as a food colouring for special rice dishes. Saffron is also used in the preparation of paneer (in saffron-infused versions), in certain Bengali sweets, and in the Hyderabadi biryani tradition. Its cultural prestige in India is enormous — it is associated with royalty, celebration, and spiritual offerings.

title: Fenugreek
full_text: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum; Hindi: methi; Tamil: vendhayam; Bengali: methi) is a multifaceted plant used extensively in Indian cooking in three distinct forms — fresh green leaves (fresh methi), dried leaves (kasuri methi), and seeds — each playing a different culinary role. Fresh methi leaves have a slightly bitter, pungent, herbal flavour and are used as a vegetable in their own right: in methi paratha (fenugreek-stuffed flatbread), methi paneer, methi chicken, methi aloo (fenugreek and potato), and as an ingredient in various dals and saag dishes. Dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi) have a more concentrated, slightly bitter, aromatic quality and are used as a finishing herb in North Indian curries — crumbled over butter chicken, palak paneer, dal makhani, and korma — where they add a distinctive depth and complexity. Kasuri methi derives its name from the Kasur region of Punjab (now in Pakistan) where it was historically grown. Fenugreek seeds are small, hard, square-ish yellow-brown seeds with an intense bitterness when raw that mellows significantly with cooking. They are used in tempering alongside mustard seeds and curry leaves in South Indian cooking, as a component of panch phoron (the Bengali five-spice blend), in pickling spice blends, in some North Indian curry pastes, and in the spice blend used to make Maldive fish-based preparations. Fenugreek seeds are also soaked in water and consumed for their medicinal properties — they are considered beneficial for blood sugar regulation and digestion in traditional Indian medicine. Fenugreek is grown widely across India, with Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh being major producing states.

title: Asafoetida (hing)
full_text: Asafoetida (Ferula asafoetida; Hindi: hing; Tamil: perungayam; Kannada: inguva) is one of the most pungent and distinctive spices used in Indian cooking — a dried gum resin extracted from the roots of Ferula plants native to Afghanistan, Iran, and Central Asia. In its raw state, asafoetida has an extraordinarily powerful, sulfurous smell that has earned it the nickname "devil's dung" in medieval European texts; however, when a small amount is cooked briefly in hot oil or ghee, it undergoes a chemical transformation that produces a remarkably pleasing aroma reminiscent of onion and garlic. This transformation is the key to its culinary use: asafoetida is added in tiny pinches (a fraction of a teaspoon for an entire dish) to the hot fat at the beginning of tempering, where it blooms and mellows instantly. Asafoetida is of fundamental importance in the vegetarian cooking traditions of India's Jain and certain Hindu communities who avoid onion and garlic for religious reasons. In these cuisines, asafoetida serves as the essential substitute providing the allium flavour notes that other cookery achieves with onion and garlic. Kashmiri Pandit cuisine, which also excludes onion and garlic, uses asafoetida as a primary aromatic base. Asafoetida is also widely used in South Indian dal preparations, rasam, sambar, and various vegetable dishes irrespective of religious restrictions, providing a background savouriness. It is particularly valued in lentil dishes for its reputed digestive properties — it is believed to reduce the gas-producing effects of legumes. The finest quality asafoetida (heeng) comes from Iran and Afghanistan and is traded across South Asia.

title: Rice (India)
full_text: Rice (Oryza sativa; Hindi: chawal; Tamil: arisi; Telugu: biyyam; Bengali: bhaat) is the most important staple grain in India for the majority of the population, dominant in the southern, eastern, coastal, and northeastern regions of the country, where it forms the absolute centre of every meal. India is one of the world's largest producers and exporters of rice and home to extraordinary rice biodiversity — over 6,000 traditional varieties are documented, ranging from the globally famous aged long-grain basmati of the Himalayan foothills to short-grain aromatic varieties prized in specific regional cuisines. Basmati rice (from the Hindi for "full of aroma") is India's most prestigious and internationally traded variety — cultivated in Punjab, Haryana, and Uttar Pradesh, its exceptional elongation (grains double or more in length when cooked), fragrant aroma, and fluffy texture make it the rice of choice for biryani, pulao, and formal dining. Other important Indian rice varieties include sona masuri (a medium-grain variety widely used in South India for daily consumption), gobindobhog (a short-grain, fragrant Bengali variety used in kheer and ritual preparations), red rice varieties (consumed widely in Kerala and as specialty heirloom rices), and broken rice (used in South Indian fermented batters for idli and dosa). Rice is the vehicle for South India's most important condiments — sambar, rasam, chutneys, and various pickles — and the base for its most important breakfast preparations (idli, dosa, appam, puttu, idiyappam). In Bengal, the combination of machher jhol (fish curry) with rice is the foundational cultural meal.

title: Lentils and pulses (India)
full_text: Lentils and pulses (collectively called dal in Hindi, though the term technically refers specifically to split pulses) are the most important source of plant protein in Indian cuisine and the dietary cornerstone for a population where around 30% are vegetarian and many more eat limited quantities of meat. India grows and consumes a wider variety of lentils and pulses than any other country. The principal varieties and their culinary roles: masoor dal (red lentils; Lens culinaris) — the quickest-cooking, most widely eaten everyday lentil, used for thin everyday dals; toor/arhar dal (pigeon pea; Cajanus cajan) — the standard dal used for sambar in South India and many North Indian preparations; chana dal (split Bengal gram; Cicer arietinum) — slightly sweet, nutty flavour, used in haleem, tadka dal, and as a primary ingredient in South Indian preparations; urad dal (black gram; Vigna mungo) — the primary dal in dal makhani (when whole, the unhusked black version) and in South Indian fermented batters for idli, dosa, vada, and papad (when split and husked, the white version); moong dal (mung bean; Vigna radiata) — the most easily digestible dal, used in khichdi and light everyday cooking; and rajma (kidney beans), chana (chickpeas), and lobiya (black-eyed peas) used as whole pulse curries. Each regional cuisine has developed distinct approaches to dal cooking: South Indian dals are typically thin and tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and dried red chilli; North Indian dals are thicker and tempered with cumin and onion; Bengali dal uses panch phoron; and Rajasthani dal uses asafoetida and minimal water. Dal-roti or dal-chawal (dal with flatbread or rice) is the fundamental everyday meal of the majority of the Indian population.

title: Thali
full_text: Thali (Hindi: थाली, meaning "plate") is the traditional South Asian style of serving a complete meal — a round, flat tray made of brass, steel, silver, or banana leaf on which multiple small dishes (katoris) are arranged around a central mound of rice or breads, creating a single harmonious meal that encompasses multiple flavours, textures, and nutritional components simultaneously. The thali concept reflects the Indian philosophical ideal of a balanced meal that includes all six tastes recognised in Ayurvedic tradition: sweet, sour, salty, pungent, bitter, and astringent. Each regional cuisine has its own distinct thali tradition. A North Indian thali typically includes roti or paratha, dal, two or three vegetable dishes (sabzi), raita or yoghurt, pickle (achar), and a small sweet. A South Indian thali (served on a banana leaf, particularly for festivals and ceremonial meals) may include rice, sambar, rasam, two or three vegetable dishes (kootu, poriyal, avial), papad, pickle, curd, and payasam. The Gujarati thali is among the most extensive in India — often including 10–15 dishes: rotli, dal, kadhi, two sabzis, rice, papad, pickle, farsan (snacks), chutney, and a sweet. The Rajasthani thali features dal baati churma and multiple desert-adapted preparations. The Onam sadya of Kerala, served on banana leaf for the harvest festival, is perhaps the grandest Indian thali tradition — with 28 or more dishes arranged in a precise traditional order. Restaurant thalis have made regional Indian cuisines accessible and popular for both domestic and international diners, and represent one of the most effective ways of experiencing the breadth of any regional cuisine in a single meal.

title: Masala chai
full_text: Masala chai (Hindi: मसाला चाय, meaning "spiced tea") is the ubiquitous spiced milk tea of the Indian subcontinent — the most widely consumed beverage across South Asia and one of the most internationally recognised preparations associated with Indian food culture. It is made by brewing black tea (typically a strong, robust CTC — crush, tear, curl — variety) together with fresh ginger, whole spices (most commonly cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper, with regional and personal variations including nutmeg, fennel, or star anise), milk, and sugar in a simmering pot until the flavours infuse and the tea achieves its characteristic deep amber colour, creamy body, and warmly spiced character. There is no single canonical masala chai recipe — every household, every chaiwala (roadside tea vendor), and every region of South Asia has its own proportions and blend. In Punjab, chai tends to be rich with a high milk ratio and heavy cardamom. In Maharashtra, ginger predominates. In Rajasthan, the tea is brewed with less water and very sweet. In southern India, filter coffee (kaapi) is the daily beverage of choice and chai is consumed differently. Small roadside tea stalls (chai dhaba or chaikhana) are among the most important social institutions in South Asian daily life — they are the places where workers take breaks, where conversations happen, and where communities gather throughout the day. Chaiwallas selling small glasses of tea are found beside every road, railway platform, and busy corner across the subcontinent. The beverage's global profile has expanded enormously through the South Asian diaspora and Western specialty coffee culture's adoption of "chai lattes" (typically much sweeter and spiced differently from authentic South Asian chai).

title: Roti and flatbread varieties
full_text: Roti and flatbreads are the foundational bread tradition of South Asia, representing an extraordinary diversity of preparations made primarily from wheat flour (atta) but also from corn, millet, rice, and chickpea flours, cooked through a range of methods — on a flat griddle (tava), in a tandoor clay oven, deep-fried in oil, or directly over an open flame. The term roti in its broadest sense refers to any unleavened flatbread, and chapal or chapati is often used interchangeably. The major flatbread varieties and their characteristics: Roti/chapati — the most basic everyday flatbread of North India and Pakistan, made from whole wheat atta and water, rolled thin, cooked on a dry tava until it puffs and blisters, then finished directly over a flame; Naan — a leavened, slightly chewy bread made from refined wheat flour with yeast or yoghurt, baked against the clay wall of a tandoor at high heat, producing a bubbly, slightly charred, teardrop-shaped bread historically associated with Mughal court cuisine; Paratha — a layered, pan-fried flatbread made from whole wheat dough with ghee or oil incorporated through folding, producing flaky layers; stuffed parathas (aloo, gobi, paneer, radish) are a Punjabi breakfast staple; Puri — a small round of wheat dough deep-fried in oil until it puffs into a hollow ball, served with aloo or chickpea curry; Makki di roti — coarse yellow corn flour flatbread associated with Punjabi winter cuisine, served with sarson da saag; Jowar/bajra/ragi roti — sorghum, pearl millet, and finger millet flatbreads from the Deccan plateau, Gujarat, and Karnataka respectively; Luchi — a Bengali version of puri made with refined flour (maida); Bhatura — a large, leavened, deep-fried bread made with fermented dough, served with chole in the classic Punjabi street food combination.

title: Biryani variants across South Asia
full_text: Biryani is the most celebrated and widely eaten rice dish across South Asia, existing in dozens of distinct regional variants that reflect the history, culture, and ingredient palette of each region where it has taken root. All biryanis share the fundamental structure of fragrant long-grain rice (almost always basmati) layered with a spiced meat or vegetable preparation and finished by the dum method — sealed cooking over low heat that allows the components to steam together. The major variants: Hyderabadi biryani — considered by many connoisseurs the finest biryani tradition, using the kacchi method (raw marinated meat layered directly with parboiled rice) for extraordinary tenderness, with generous saffron, fried onions, and mint; Lucknawi (Awadhi) biryani — the pakki method (pre-cooked meat layered with rice), more delicate and aromatic; Kolkata biryani — distinguished by whole boiled eggs, potatoes, and a subtle saffron-and-rose-water flavour, developed by the exiled Nawabs of Awadh; Sindhi biryani (Pakistan) — boldly spiced with potatoes and dried plums for tang; Lahori biryani — richly caramelised onions and bold spice blend; Malabar biryani (Kerala) — cooked with kaima rice (a short-grain aromatic variety) and the distinctive addition of fried cashews, raisins, and Kerala spicing; Thalassery biryani (Kerala) — the only South Indian biryani using kaima rice; Chettinad biryani (Tamil Nadu) — intensely spiced with kalpasi (stone flower) and marathi mokku; Ambur biryani (Tamil Nadu) — seeraga samba rice, tomato-heavy; Bangladeshi kacchi biryani — made with mutton, potatoes, and a distinctive spice blend. All share the dum technique but their flavour profiles, rice types, meat choices, and cultural contexts differ significantly.

title: Seekh kebab (Pakistan)
full_text: Seekh kebab in Pakistani cuisine is a fundamental preparation and one of the country's most beloved forms of barbecued meat — a skewered, grilled minced meat preparation that occupies a central place at Pakistani roadside dhabas, wedding feasts, restaurant menus, and family barbecues. In Pakistan, seekh kebab is most commonly made from beef mince (unlike the Awadhi Indian tradition which typically uses lamb), shaped around flat or round metal skewers and grilled over charcoal until cooked through with a lightly charred exterior. The mince is seasoned with finely grated onion, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, fresh coriander, cumin, coriander powder, red chilli, garam masala, and sometimes raw papaya paste as a tenderiser. The fat content of the mince must be sufficient to hold the mixture on the skewer — too lean and it crumbles off during grilling. The cooked seekh kebabs are served with fresh naan, sliced raw onions, mint-coriander chutney, and lemon wedges. In the Pakistani barbecue culture, seekh kebab is typically eaten alongside tikka (marinated grilled meat pieces), boti (marinated lamb or chicken pieces), and chicken wings. The Karachi style tends to use more spice and dried pomegranate seeds (anardana); the Lahori style may be milder and richer; the Peshawari style is made with coarser mince and fewer spices, reflecting the KPK preference for ingredient-led flavour. Seekh kebab rolls — seekh kebab wrapped in a thin flatbread (paratha or naan) with sliced onions and chutney — are one of the most popular Pakistani street food preparations.

title: Paya
full_text: Paya (Urdu: پایا, meaning "foot/leg") is a traditional slow-cooked stew made from the trotters (feet and lower legs) of goat, cow, or sheep, and is one of the most celebrated and beloved morning comfort foods in Pakistani cuisine — particularly in Lahore, Peshawar, and Karachi, where dedicated paya restaurants and dhabas serve it from early morning as a weekend breakfast or brunch. The dish reflects both the Mughal tradition of elaborate nose-to-tail cooking and the practical wisdom of using every part of the animal. To prepare paya, the cleaned trotters are simmered for many hours — typically 6 to 12 — with water, whole spices (cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, black peppercorns), ginger, garlic, onion, and a distinctive spice blend including fennel, cumin, and coriander, until the tough cartilage and connective tissue break down completely into a silky, gelatinous, intensely flavoured broth. The rendered collagen gives paya its characteristic thick, sticky consistency and rich body. The stew is finished with fried onions, fresh ginger julienne, green chillies, and lemon juice, and served with naan or kulcha. The gelatinous broth has a deeply satisfying, unctuous quality that has earned paya a reputation as both a restorative winter food and a hangover cure. Paya is often eaten alongside nihari (slow-cooked beef shank stew) as a complete slow-cooked meat breakfast. In Pakistani culture, the preparation of paya is considered an act of patience and care — it cannot be rushed. The best paya is cooked overnight in a heavy handi (clay or metal pot) over very low heat.

title: Pulao (Pakistan)
full_text: Pulao (also spelled pilau, pilaf, or palao; Urdu: پلاؤ) is one of the most fundamental and widely eaten rice preparations in Pakistani cuisine — a dish in which long-grain basmati rice is cooked in a seasoned meat or vegetable broth rather than plain water, producing aromatic, flavourful rice in which every grain is separate and infused with the essence of the stock. Unlike biryani, which layers separately prepared rice and meat with strong spice blends, pulao is a more restrained, unified preparation in which the rice and meat or vegetables are cooked together in one pot in a subtly spiced broth. The flavour of pulao comes primarily from the quality of the bone broth (yakhni) in which it is cooked rather than from spice intensity. To make Pakistani pulao, a yakhni broth is prepared by simmering meat with aromatic whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, black pepper) and onion until deeply flavoured. The broth is strained, used to cook parboiled basmati rice on low heat, and the meat is served alongside or incorporated into the rice. Regional variations include Kabuli palaw (the Afghan-influenced rice with caramelised carrots, raisins, and lamb, popular in KPK and among Pashtun communities), Bannu pulao (from KPK — made with beef broth and finished with caramelised onions), matar pulao (with green peas), and Sindhi pulao. Pulao is considered more homestyle and modest than biryani but no less satisfying, and is the standard accompaniment to qorma at major feasts when biryani is not served. The distinction between pulao and biryani — in terms of technique, spice level, and cultural register — is an important one in Pakistani culinary discourse.

title: Fish ambul thiyal
full_text: Fish ambul thiyal (Sinhala: ඇඹුල් තියල්, meaning "sour fish curry") is one of the most distinctive and celebrated preparations in Sri Lankan cuisine — a dry, intensely flavoured sour fish preparation from the southern coastal region of Sri Lanka, made with chunks of firm-fleshed tuna marinated and slow-cooked in a thick paste of goraka (Garcinia cambogia, a dried souring fruit unique to Sri Lanka), black pepper, garlic, turmeric, curry leaves, and salt. Unlike most South Asian fish curries, ambul thiyal contains no coconut milk or tomatoes — it is dry-cooked in its own concentrated souring and spice paste, producing a deeply flavoured, almost sticky coating on each piece of fish. The dish's defining character comes entirely from goraka: a small, pumpkin-like tropical fruit that is dried and smoked until jet-black and rock-hard, then soaked in water to release its intensely sour, slightly resinous liquid. This liquid is far more concentrated and distinctively flavoured than tamarind and cannot be substituted. To prepare ambul thiyal, fish pieces (usually skipjack tuna or wahoo, cut into thick chunks) are marinated in the goraka paste with black pepper, turmeric, garlic, and curry leaves. The marinated fish is cooked in a clay pot (which the dish requires for traditional preparation) with minimal added liquid over low heat, turning occasionally until the paste reduces completely and the fish pieces are coated in a thick, dry, dark-coloured concentrate. The result is intensely sour, peppery, and aromatic. Ambul thiyal keeps well without refrigeration due to the preservative properties of goraka and pepper, making it historically important as a travel food and pantry staple. It is served alongside rice and coconut sambol.

title: Rice and curry (Sri Lanka)
full_text: Rice and curry is the national meal of Sri Lanka — the foundational daily eating pattern and the defining structure of Sri Lankan food culture. A traditional Sri Lankan rice and curry is not a single dish but rather a composed meal served on a single plate (or banana leaf for formal occasions) consisting of boiled or steamed rice surrounded by a selection of curries and condiments. A typical everyday Sri Lankan rice and curry spread includes: a main protein curry (fish curry, chicken curry, or beef/pork curry for non-vegetarians, or a legume curry for vegetarians); two or three vegetable curries (jackfruit, green beans, aubergine, potato, bitter gourd, drumstick); dhal (red or green lentil curry with coconut milk); pol sambol (fresh coconut sambol with dried chilli, Maldive fish, and lime); papadam; and pickle. For special occasions, the spread expands substantially. The rice used is typically a medium-grain white rice (such as samba rice) or one of Sri Lanka's traditional red rice varieties. The curries of Sri Lanka are cooked using a characteristic dark-roasted curry powder (made from dry-roasted coriander, cumin, fennel, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and dried chilli), producing a deeper, more intensely flavoured curry base than the raw spice blends of South India. Coconut milk is added in stages — thin coconut milk early in cooking and thick coconut milk (first pressing) at the end to provide richness without boiling the coconut milk. The practice of eating rice and curry with the right hand (never the left), on a banana leaf for ceremonial meals, and in a communal setting reflects Sri Lanka's Hindu and Buddhist cultural heritage and the central role of food in social and spiritual life.

title: Bolani
full_text: Bolani (Dari: بولانی) is a traditional Afghan flatbread that is stuffed with a seasoned filling and pan-fried or baked until golden and crispy on both sides — one of the most popular and beloved everyday foods and street foods of Afghanistan. The word bolani in Dari means "air-filled" or "balloon-like," referring to the way the thin dough puffs slightly when cooking. Bolani is made from an unleavened dough of wheat flour, water, salt, and a small amount of oil, rolled into thin rounds approximately 20–25 cm in diameter. The filling — spread over half the dough round — is then covered by folding the other half over to create a half-moon shape, which is pressed closed at the edges. The most popular fillings are: gandana bolani (filled with gandana, the Afghan leek or scallion native to the country, mixed with green chilli and salt — the most classic and beloved version); katchalu bolani (filled with spiced mashed potato); kadu bolani (pumpkin or squash filling); and occasionally spinach or a spiced lentil filling. The filled pastry is cooked in a shallow layer of oil in a flat pan over medium heat, turning once, until both sides are deeply golden and slightly blistered. Bolani is typically served with chakkah (thick garlic yoghurt), mint chutney, or a fresh tomato-coriander salsa. It is eaten as a main meal, a substantial snack, a street food, and an important accompaniment at communal gatherings. Bolani is also popular during Ramadan for iftar. It is one of the dishes most closely associated with Afghan identity and domestic cooking, and is a standard preparation that every Afghan cook is expected to know.

title: Aush
full_text: Aush (also spelled aash; Dari: آش) is a hearty Afghan noodle soup — one of the most important and culturally significant comfort foods in Afghan cuisine, eaten throughout the country across all seasons but particularly valued during cold weather. The dish belongs to a broader Central Asian tradition of thick noodle soups that extends across Afghanistan, Iran, and Central Asia, and aush is closely related to the Persian aash. Afghan aush is made from thick, hand-made wheat noodles (or increasingly from commercial pasta) cooked in a rich broth of meat (usually lamb or beef), lentils (typically dried yellow split peas or red lentils), chickpeas, fresh vegetables (particularly spinach, leeks, and coriander), and a warming spice blend including turmeric, coriander, and black pepper. The dish is characteristically finished with layers of toppings that contrast with the hearty soup base: a generous drizzle of chakkah (thick strained yoghurt whipped with garlic), a spoonful of qoroot sauce (sour dried whey sauce), kashk (fermented whey), crispy fried onions, dried mint, and sometimes a drizzle of chilli oil. This combination of hot savoury soup, cool tangy dairy, sharp garlic, and aromatic dried mint is distinctively Afghan and creates a layered flavour experience unique to the cuisine. Aush is considered a meal of generosity and community — it is traditionally prepared in large quantities for gatherings, funerals, religious occasions, and charitable distribution. Multiple regional variations exist: some versions include meat, others are entirely vegetarian; some use tomato, others rely solely on the depth of the broth.

title: Thukpa
full_text: Thukpa (also spelled thugpa; Tibetan: ཐུག་པ) is a hearty noodle soup originating in Tibetan cuisine that has been widely adopted across Nepal, particularly in the Himalayan regions, Kathmandu, and wherever Tibetan-influenced mountain communities cook. The word means "noodle" in Tibetan. Thukpa is made from thick, hand-pulled or machine-made wheat noodles cooked in a richly seasoned meat broth, typically with pieces of yak, goat, lamb, or chicken alongside boiled or stir-fried vegetables such as spinach, carrots, and spring onions. The broth is seasoned with garlic, ginger, soy sauce or fish sauce, dried chillies, and various warming spices, giving it a deeply savoury, slightly spicy, warming character ideal for the cold climates of the Himalayas. Nepalese thukpa tends to be spicier than its Tibetan predecessor, reflecting the South Asian preference for chilli heat. Vegetables are sometimes stir-fried before being added to the soup, adding a slight smokiness. Thukpa is one of the most popular dishes in teahouse kitchens along Nepal's trekking routes — on routes to Everest Base Camp, Annapurna, and other high-altitude destinations, thukpa is valued for its warming, protein-rich, easily digestible qualities. It is also a common urban street food in Kathmandu, where restaurants and food stalls serve it as a lunch staple. Regional varieties include Jhol thukpa (with a particularly flavoursome spiced sauce), thenthuk (flat hand-torn noodle soup from Tibetan tradition), and gyathuk (a Sherpa noodle soup made with thin rice noodles). Thukpa is related to the Chinese lamian noodle soup tradition via the Himalayan corridor of cultural exchange.

title: Dhindo
full_text: Dhindo (Nepali: ढिंडो) is a traditional Nepali staple food made from buckwheat, millet, maize, or barley flour cooked with boiling water and stirred continuously until it forms a dense, smooth, slightly sticky dough-like consistency — similar in texture and preparation method to polenta or ugali. It is the primary staple grain preparation of Nepal's hilly and mountainous communities where rice cultivation is impossible due to altitude or difficult terrain, and represents the original, pre-rice food culture of the Nepali hills. To prepare dhindo, water is brought to a vigorous boil in a heavy pot, then the flour is added gradually while the cook stirs continuously and vigorously with a wooden paddle (mosha), preventing lumps from forming and incorporating all the flour into the thickening paste. The stirring must be constant and strong for 15–20 minutes as the mixture becomes extremely thick and stiff. The finished dhindo is a dense, neutral-tasting mass that is served hot in a bowl and eaten by pinching off small pieces with the right hand, shaping them into small balls, making an indentation with the thumb, and using the indentation to scoop up accompaniments. Dhindo is traditionally eaten with gundruk ko achar (fermented greens pickle), saag (stir-fried leafy greens), meat curry or dried fish, and yoghurt or buttermilk. Although long associated with poverty and rural subsistence diets, dhindo has undergone a cultural rehabilitation in Nepal and is increasingly served in Thakali-style restaurants in Kathmandu and Pokhara as an authentic expression of Nepali mountain food culture, appreciated for its nutritional value (buckwheat dhindo in particular is rich in protein and minerals) and unique texture.

title: Kacchi biryani (Bangladesh)
full_text: Kacchi biryani (Bengali: কাচ্চি বিরিয়ানি, meaning "raw biryani") is the most celebrated and prestigious biryani tradition of Bangladesh — a festive, elaborate preparation in which raw, marinated mutton or beef is layered directly with parboiled basmati rice and slow-cooked together in a sealed pot (dum) until both the meat and rice are simultaneously perfectly cooked. The "kacchi" (raw) in the name distinguishes it from pukki biryani (in which the meat is pre-cooked before layering with rice). The kacchi method demands greater precision and skill: the cooking time must be calibrated so that the meat cooks through completely in exactly the same time as the rice reaches perfect texture — a difficult balance achieved by regulating the heat and steaming time very precisely. The marination of the mutton or beef is elaborate: the meat is marinated overnight in yoghurt, raw papaya paste (as a tenderiser), fried onion paste, ginger-garlic paste, dried plums (aloo bukhara), fried potatoes, and a complex spice blend including mace, nutmeg, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper. The parboiled rice is layered over the raw marinated meat in a heavy-bottomed handi, topped with saffron-infused milk, fried onions (beresta), and ghee. The handi is sealed with dough (the dum technique) and cooked over very low heat for 45–60 minutes. Kacchi biryani is associated with Dhaka's Old City (particularly Purana Paltan and the legendary Haji Biryani restaurant) and is the non-negotiable centrepiece of Bangladeshi weddings, Eid celebrations, and major feasts. It is considered a symbol of celebration and Mughal culinary heritage in Bangladesh.

title: Panch phoron
full_text: Panch phoron (Bengali: পাঁচ ফোড়ন, meaning "five tempering spices") is the distinctive whole-spice blend that is the defining aromatic foundation of Bengali and Bangladeshi cooking — used across both Bangladesh and the Indian state of West Bengal, as well as parts of Assam, Bihar, and Orissa. Unlike garam masala (which is ground), panch phoron is always used whole and never ground, and is added directly to hot oil or ghee to bloom and release its flavours before other ingredients are added. The five spices in panch phoron are: fenugreek seeds (methi) — slightly bitter and nutty; cumin seeds (jeera) — earthy and warm; nigella seeds (kalonji, also called black seed or kalo jeera) — slightly peppery, onion-like, and aromatic; fennel seeds (mouri/saunf) — sweet, mild anise-like flavour; and black mustard seeds (shorshe) or alternatively radhuni (wild celery seeds, Trachyspermum roxburghianum), a variety of seeds used particularly in West Bengal in place of mustard. All five spices are used in equal proportions by volume. When added to hot oil, the mustard seeds pop first, followed by the other seeds sizzling and releasing a complex, multi-layered fragrance that is simultaneously bitter, nutty, sweet, and aromatic — impossible to replicate with individual spices. Panch phoron is used to begin the cooking of lentil dishes (dal), vegetable preparations, chutneys, pickles, potato dishes, and fish curries throughout Bengali cuisine. It is the spice blend that most clearly marks a dish as distinctively Bengali, and understanding its character is fundamental to understanding the flavour architecture of the cuisine.

title: Panch phoron (India)
full_text: See bangladesh_ingredient_panch_phoron for full entry. Panch phoron is a whole-spice blend used in eastern Indian cuisines of West Bengal, Assam, Orissa, and Bihar, as well as in Bangladesh. In India specifically, it is most closely associated with Bengali cooking from West Bengal, where it appears in all major dal preparations, vegetable dishes (particularly aloo posto — potato with poppy seed paste), fish curries (machher jhol), chutneys, and pickles. The five spices — fenugreek, cumin, nigella, fennel, and mustard/radhuni — are added whole to hot mustard oil at the start of cooking, producing the characteristic Bengali tempering (phoron) that is the flavour signature of the cuisine. In Orissa, a variant called panch phutana uses slightly different proportions and includes cumin, mustard, fennel, fenugreek, and kalonji in a similar whole-spice tempering tradition.

title: Upma
full_text: Upma is a wholesome South Indian breakfast dish made from rava (semolina, also called cream of wheat or suji), dry-roasted and then cooked with water, onions, mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chillies, ginger, and a tempering of oil or ghee. The rava is first dry-roasted in a pan until lightly golden and fragrant, which prevents it from clumping and develops a nutty flavour. Boiling water is then stirred in rapidly and the mixture is cooked, stirring continuously, until the semolina absorbs the liquid and thickens into a soft, fluffy, savoury porridge. Common additions include vegetables such as carrots, peas, and beans, as well as peanuts and cashew nuts for texture. Upma is served hot, often garnished with fresh coriander leaves and a squeeze of lemon juice. It is particularly popular in Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Kerala as an everyday breakfast or evening snack. Variations include rava upma (the standard form), vegetable upma, bread upma (made with torn bread pieces instead of rava), and vermicelli upma (semiya upma). In Wikibooks' South Asian cuisine section, upma is described as a wholesome Indian dish made of rava (cream of wheat).

title: Ghevar
full_text: Ghevar is a traditional Rajasthani disc-shaped sweet made from a batter of refined wheat flour (maida), ghee, and water, deep-fried in ghee in a special cylindrical mould to produce a porous, lattice-like golden cake that is then soaked in sugar syrup. The characteristic honeycomb texture comes from the way the thin batter is poured in a thin stream into the very hot ghee, where it spreads and sets in an intricate network of fine, crispy strands. After frying, the ghevar is dipped in a thin sugar syrup flavoured with cardamom and saffron. Ghevar is most closely associated with the festival of Teej, celebrated during the monsoon season in Rajasthan, and is also made during Raksha Bandhan. It is typically served with rabri — a thick, reduced, sweetened milk dessert flavoured with cardamom, saffron, and sometimes rose water, in which the milk is simmered until reduced to a fraction of its original volume with a characteristic wrinkled skin on top. The pairing of hot, crispy ghevar with cold, creamy rabri is the classic and traditional way to eat it. Ghevar is also available topped with malai (fresh cream) or dry fruits. It is one of the most celebrated sweets of Rajasthani cuisine, described in culinary traditions as usually served with piping hot rabri, a variety of very thick sweetened milk.

title: Halwa
full_text: Halwa (also spelled halva or halawa) is a broad category of dense, sweet confections found across the Indian subcontinent, the Middle East, and Central Asia, made from a range of base ingredients including semolina (sooji halwa), carrots (gajar halwa), lentils (moong dal halwa), sesame paste, or various flours cooked with ghee and sugar. In India, halwa is one of the most commonly prepared festival and ceremonial sweets. Each region has its own distinct style. In North India, gajar ka halwa (carrot halwa) is the most popular, made by slowly reducing grated carrots in milk and ghee with sugar and cardamom. Moong dal halwa, made from ground yellow lentils, is a rich Rajasthani specialty. Sooji (semolina) halwa is the most widely made everyday version, prepared by dry-roasting semolina in ghee and cooking it with sugar syrup. In Kerala, halwa is a particularly celebrated and regionally distinctive sweet. The most famous is Kozhikode halwa (Calicut halwa), which originated in Kozhikode (Calicut) and is considered the finest variety in South India. According to food histories of the region, Kozhikode halwa is one of the most important sweet dishes in Kerala and has its origins in the Gujarathi (Gujarati) community that settled in Calicut, who brought their halwa-making tradition to the region. It is made from maida (refined wheat flour), water, ghee, sugar, and coconut oil, coloured with food colouring and flavoured with cardamom, giving it a distinctive translucent, chewy, intensely sweet character unlike northern semolina-based halwas.

title: Colonial and foreign culinary influences on Indian cuisine
full_text: Indian cuisine has been shaped significantly by successive waves of foreign contact, trade, and colonial rule. The Portuguese arrived on the Indian coast in the early 16th century and left a profound culinary legacy, particularly in Goa. They introduced vinegar as a cooking and preservation ingredient, brought New World vegetables including tomatoes, potatoes, chillies, sweet potatoes, peanuts, and squash which became fundamental to Indian cooking, and introduced bread (pao) to coastal regions. The British colonial period (1757–1947) further shaped Indian food culture by introducing baking techniques and European-style cooking methods, cauliflower (introduced in 1822), and facilitating the spread of tea cultivation that made India the world's largest tea-drinking nation. The Portuguese and British together introduced cooking techniques such as baking, and foods from the New World and Europe. New World vegetables such as maize, tomato, potato, sweet potatoes, peanuts, squash, and chilli were introduced during the colonial period and became staples of Indian cuisine. Before the Columbian Exchange, Indian cuisine used black pepper, long pepper, and other native spices for heat rather than chillies. The Mughal Empire (1526–1857) contributed tandoor cooking, biryani, kebabs, korma, and the use of dried fruits and nuts in savoury cooking. Arab traders brought saffron, dried fruits, and rice cooking techniques. Portuguese missionaries and traders in Goa shaped the vindaloo and sorpotel traditions.
